mainOrgs.name: Wild Rivers Conservancy Of The St Croix & Namekagon
mainOrgs.missionSummary: Inspire stewardship to ensure ecological integrity of the St. Croix and Namekagon Riverway.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Land and Water program; Invasive Species program.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Focus on watershed protection and water quality in the riverway.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Two main programs centered on land/water protection and invasive species control.
mainOrgs.notableSignals: Official non-profit partner of St. Croix National Scenic Riverway.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: With over 17 years in operation and a $1.8M revenue, the organization expertly leverages partnerships to enhance watershed stewardship and ecological integrity.
mainOrgs.websiteRecentActivity: Upcoming events include volunteer opportunities and paddle trips in the St. Croix and Namekagon Riverway.
mainOrgs.softFlags: Months unrestricted below threshold; Revenue concentrated in Contributions (97%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: THE MISSION OF WILD RIVERS CONSERVANCY OF THE ST. CROIX & NAMEKAGON IS TO INSPIRE STEWARDSHIP TO FOREVER ENSURE THE RARE ECOLOGICAL INTEGRITY OF THE ST. CROIX AND NAMEKAGON RIVERWAY. THE CONSERVANCY OF THE ST. CROIX AND NAMEKAGON WORKS THROUGHOUT THE WATERSHED TO PROTECT THE NATIONAL PARK THAT FLOWS THROUGH ITS HEART. WE ARE THE OFFICIAL NON-PROFIT PARTNER FOR THE ST. CROIX NATIONAL SCENIC RIVERWAY.
mainOrgs.program2Desc: LAND AND WATER: OUR LAND AND WATER PROGRAM PROTECTS, IMPROVES, AND RESTORES IMPORTANT NATURAL AND SCENIC AREAS IN THE RIVERWAY AND THROUGHOUT THE WATERSHED. THE ORGANIZATION LEVERAGES RESOURCES AND BUILDS PARTNERSHIPS TO ENSURE THAT INVESTMENTS IN CLIMATE RESILIENCY, FOREST MANAGEMENT, WATER QUALITY, HABITAT AND RECREATIONAL LAND IMPROVEMENT ARE STRATEGICALLY TARGETED AND COORDINATED TO ACHIEVE THE MAXIMUM RESULTS.
mainOrgs.program3Desc: INVASIVE SPECIES: THE INVASIVE SPECIES PROGRAM IDENTIFIES AND CONTROLS TERRESTRIAL AND AQUATIC INVASIVE SPECIES. THROUGH THIS PROGRAM, THE ORGANIZATION COLLABORATES WITH OTHER ORGANIZATIONS TO STRATEGICALLY ADDRESS INVASIVE SPECIES THREATS IN THE ST. CROIX RIVER BASIN INCLUDING PURPLE LOOSESTRIFE, PHRAGMITES, EURASIAN WATER MILFOIL, ZEBRA MUSSELS, INVASIVE CARP AND MANY OTHER SPECIES.
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
THE ORGANIZATION IS ORGANIZED AS A MINNESOTA NONPROFIT CORPORATION AND HAS BEEN RECOGNIZED BY THE IRS AS EXEMPT FROM FEDERAL INCOME TAXES UNDER SECTION 501(C)(3). THE ORGANIZATION IS ANNUALLY REQUIRED TO FILE A RETURN OF ORGANIZATION EXEMPT FROM INCOME TAX (FORM 990) WITH THE IRS. IN ADDITION, THE ORGANIZATION IS SUBJECT TO INCOME TAX ON NET INCOME THAT IS DERIVED FROM BUSINESS ACTIVITIES THAT ARE UNRELATED TO ITS EXEMPT PURPOSE. THE ORGANIZATION HAS DETERMINED IT IS NOT SUBJECT TO ANY UNRELATED BUSINESS INCOME TAX AND HAS NOT FILED AN EXEMPT ORGANIZATION BUSINESS INCOME TAX RETURN (FORM 990-T) WITH THE IRS.

THE MISSION OF WILD RIVERS CONSERVANCY OF THE ST. CROIX & NAMEKAGON IS TO INSPIRE STEWARDSHIP TO FOREVER ENSURE THE RARE ECOLOGICAL INTEGRITY OF THE ST. CROIX AND NAMEKAGON RIVERWAY. THE CONSERVANCY OF THE ST. CROIX AND NAMEKAGON WORKS THROUGHOUT THE WATERSHED TO PROTECT THE NATIONAL PARK THAT FLOWS THROUGH ITS HEART. WE ARE THE OFFICIAL NON-PROFIT PARTNER FOR THE ST. CROIX NATIONAL SCENIC RIVERWAY.

A TOTAL OF 302 VOLUNTEERS PROVIDED WILD RIVERS CONSERVANCY OF THE ST. CROIX AND NAMEKAGON WITH 5,894 HOURS OF SERVICE DURING 2024.

THE ORGANIZATION PROVIDES AN ELECTRONIC COPY OF THIS FORM 990 TO THE OFFICERS AND DIRECTORS FOR THEIR COMMENTS PRIOR TO FILING.

THE OFFICERS AND DIRECTORS OF THE ORGANIZATION ARE PERIODICALLY REMINDED OF THEIR OBLIGATIONS UNDER THIS POLICY AND ARE REQUIRED TO DISCLOSE ON AN ANNUAL BASIS ANY INTEREST THAT COULD GIVE RISE TO CONFLICTS. THE POLICY REQUIRES DISCLOSURE OF ANY POTENTIAL CONFLICT IN THE MEANTIME, AND RECUSAL FROM PARTICIPATING IN ANY RESULTING ACTION.

THE COMPENSATION FOR THE EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR IS DETERMINED ANNUALLY BY THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS THROUGH A COMMITTEE RECOMMENDATION AND EXECUTIVE COMMITTEE APPROVAL PROCESS. WAGE RATES ARE BASED ON MINNESOTA COUNCIL OF NONPROFIT WAGE AND BENEFITS REPORT.

THE ORGANIZATION'S GOVERNING DOCUMENTS, CONFLICT OF INTEREST POLICY, AND FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ARE AVAILABLE UPON REQUEST AT THE ORGANIZATION'S OFFICE: 1015 N CASCADE ST, OSCEOLA, WI 54020.

Title: Home

URL Source: https://www.nahantmarsh.org/

Markdown Content:
### Who We Are

Nahant Marsh is a 382-acre preserve nestled in southwest Davenport. It is part of a 513-acre wetland complex that is bordered by the Mississippi River, Interstate 280, and Highway 22.

### Education Center Hours

The Nahant Marsh Education Center is open:

Monday through Friday

8:30 a.m. to 4:30 p.m.

Saturdays

9 a.m. to noon

(extended to 3 p.m. from April through October).

### Preserve Hours

Trails are open daily from sunrise to sunset. If the main gate is locked, trails are accessible from the front parking lot. Look for the opening in the fence near the kiosk.

[Click here for a](https://irp.cdn-website.com/2f0b2e22/files/uploaded/Nahant-Marsh-Trail-Map.pdf)[trail map](https://irp.cdn-website.com/2f0b2e22/files/uploaded/Nahant-Marsh-Trail-Map.pdf)[.](https://www.nahantmarsh.org/)

There is no fee to visit the Marsh.

## The future starts with you

#### wHERE NATURE & nURTURE THRIVE

We are in control of how we live on this planet. Consider becoming part of the movement. Protect our rivers year round!

[LEARN MORE](https://www.nahantmarsh.org/our-story)

## Upcoming Events

### Education

Nahant Marsh Education Center offers a wide variety of environmental education programming for people of all ages. Educational programs are conducted both on and off-site and provide programming that focuses on midwest ecosystems, conservation, and stewardship.

Research projects are

mainOrgs.name: Mississippi Park Connection
mainOrgs.missionSummary: Strengthening the connection between people and the Mississippi River through stewardship and community engagement.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Environmental stewardship and community engagement programs focused on the Mississippi River.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Focuses directly on the conservation of the Mississippi River and its resources.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Operates primarily in environmental stewardship and community engagement related to the Mississippi River.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: MPC is a mature organization with nearly 20 years of experience, demonstrating strong financial health and a commitment to enhancing the Mississippi River's ecosystem and community engagement.
mainOrgs.websiteRecentActivity: Offering youth education programs and volunteer opportunities focused on environmental stewardship.
mainOrgs.softFlags: Days cash below tier threshold (63 < 90); Revenue concentrated in Contributions (87%); Program expense ratio below tier threshold (78%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: THE MISSION OF THE MISSISSIPPI PARK CONNECTION (MPC) IS TO STRENGTHEN THE ENDURING CONNECTION BETWEEN PEOPLE AND THE MISSISSIPPI RIVER BY ENRICHING THE LIFE OF THE RIVER AND THE LIVES OF ALL WHO EXPERIENCE THE MISSISSIPPI NATIONAL RIVER AND RECREATION AREA (THE NATIONAL PARK). MPC IS A 501(C)(3) ORGANIZATION. MISSISSIPPI RIVER PADDLE SHARE, LLC, IS WHOLLY OWNED BY MPC.MPC ENGAGES PRIVATE CITIZENS, PUBLIC AND PRIVATE FOUNDATIONS, AND INDUSTRY SOURCES TO SUPPORT THE ACTIVITIES OF THE NATIONAL PARK SERVICE AND ITS PARTNERS TO ENHANCE THE NATIONAL PARK AND MISSISSIPPI RIVER BY: RAISING FUNDS TO SUPPORT THE NATIONAL PARK'S PROGRAMS. INCREASING AWARENESS OF THE NATIONAL PARK AND THE MISSISSIPPI RIVER. PROVIDING COMMUNITY CONNECTION, EXPERTISE, AND INSPIRATION FOR THE NATIONAL PARK.
mainOrgs.program2Desc: ENVIRONMENTAL STEWARDSHIP:MPC'S ENVIRONMENTAL STEWARDSHIP PROGRAMS CONNECT PEOPLE TO THE RIVER THROUGH CONSCIENTIOUS, HANDS-ON STEWARDSHIP ACTIVITIES THAT PROTECT AND RESTORE THE MISSISSIPPI RIVER'S CHERISHED NATURAL AND CULTURAL RESOURCES. MPC EXPANDS THE NATIONAL PARK'S CAPACITY TO CONDUCT ENVIRONMENTAL RESTORATION AND FIELD WORK THAT BENEFIT THE RIVER AND SEEKS OUT BEST STEWARDSHIP PRACTICES OF THE ECOLOGICAL RESOURCES WITHIN THE PARK.
mainOrgs.program3Desc: COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT:MPC'S COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT PROGRAMS NURTURE DEEP RELATIONSHIPS WITH COMMUNITIES AND CO-CREATED PROGRAMS TO DEVELOP AFFINITIES BETWEEN PEOPLE AND THE MISSISSIPPI RIVER. MPC COLLABORATES WITH THE NATIONAL PARK SERVICE TO COORDINATE AND PROMOTE COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT EVENTS YEAR-ROUND, PROVIDING ACCESSIBLE AND POSITIVE EXPERIENCES AT THE PARK FOR THE PUBLIC.
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
THE ORGANIZATION'S ENDOWMENT FUNDS ARE TO BE USED TO SUPPORT PROGRAMS AND PROJECTS THAT BENEFIT THE MISSISSIPPI RIVER AND THE NATIONAL PARK SERVICE. INTENDED USES ON THE ENDOWMENT FUNDS INCLUDE, BUT ARE NOT LIMITED TO, GENERAL OPERATIONS, EDUCATION PROGRAMS, JUNIOR RANGERS, SCIENTIFIC MONITORING, WATER QUALITY, GENERAL STEWARDSHIP, SPECIES PROTECTION, AND HABITAT RESTORATION.

MPC IS CLASSIFIED AS A TAX-EXEMPT ORGANIZATION UNDER MINNESOTA STATUTE 290.05 AND SECTION 501(C)(3) OF THE INTERNAL REVENUE CODE, IS EXEMPT FROM PRIVATE FOUNDATION STATUS UNDER SECTION 509(A)(1) OF THE INTERNAL REVENUE CODE, AND IS SUBJECT TO INCOME TAXES ONLY ON NET UNRELATED BUSINESS INCOME. MANAGEMENT BELIEVES MPC DID NOT HAVE ANY UNRELATED BUSINESS INCOME OR UNCERTAIN TAX POSITIONS.

INVESTMENT MANAGEMENT FEES -34,264.

INVESTMENT MANAGEMENT FEES -34,264.

THE PROFESSIONAL FUNDRAISER IS COMPENSATED FOR CONSULTING FEES FOR CAMPAIGN READINESS AND FEASIBILITY STUDY RELATED TO A POTENTIAL CAPITAL CAMPAIGN RELATED TO THE PLANNED RIVER LEARNING CENTER.

MPC GIVES THE AUDIT FIRM THE NECESSARY INFORMATION TO PREPARE THE 990. A DRAFT OF THE 990 IS REVIEWED BY MANAGEMENT, MANAGEMENT REVIEWS THE 990 WITH THE BOARD EXECUTIVE COMMITTEE AND THEN WITH THE FULL BOARD OF DIRECTORS. THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS HAS FINAL APPROVAL OF THE 990.

BOARD MEMBERS ARE ASKED IF THERE ARE ANY CONFLICTS BEFORE VOTING ON SENSITIVE ISSUES LIKE A GRANT TO AN ORGANIZATION. IF A CONFLICT IS DISCOVERED, THAT BOARD MEMBER IS EXCLUDED FROM VOTING ON THE ISSUE.

THE EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR'S SALARY IS ROUTINELY REVIEWED BY THE FINANCE COMMITTEE. THE SALARY REVIEW INCLUDES A REVIEW OF RECENT SALARY SURVEYS AND COMPARISONS TO POSITIONS IN SIMILARLY SIZED ORGANIZATIONS IN THEIR GEOGRAPHIC LOCATION. RECOMMENDATIONS OF THE FINANCE COMMITTEE ARE FORWARDED TO THE FULL BOARD FOR APPROVAL.

GOVERNING DOCUMENTS ARE REGISTERED WITH THE MINNESOTA SECRETARY OF STATE'S OFFICE, AND ARE AVAILABLE TO THE PUBLIC. FINANCIAL STATEMENTS CAN BE OBTAINED THROUGH FEDERAL, MINNESOTA STATE, OR DUN & BRADSTREET OFFICES. CONFLICT OF INTEREST POLICY MUST BE REQUESTED FROM MISSISSIPPI PARK CONNECTION.

BENEFITS ADMINISTRATION FEES: PROGRAM SERVICE EXPENSES 0. MANAGEMENT AND GENERAL EXPENSES 12,032. FUNDRAISING EXPENSES 0. TOTAL EXPENSES 12,032. CONTRACT SERVICES: PROGRAM SERVICE EXPENSES 310,317. MANAGEMENT AND GENERAL EXPENSES 2,225. FUNDRAISING EXPENSES 0. TOTAL EXPENSES 312,542.

THIS PROCESS HAS NOT CHANGED FROM THE PRIOR YEAR.

mainOrgs.name: Alliance For Water Efficiency Nfp
mainOrgs.missionSummary: Promoting the efficient and sustainable use of water.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Not specifically provided.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Focus on sustainable water usage aligns directly with core water priorities.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Single clear mission centered on water efficiency.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: The Alliance for Water Efficiency has been operational for over 18 years, with a strong current ratio of 5.77 and a revenue trend of 29.3%, indicating stable financial health. It focuses solely on promoting sustainable water use.
mainOrgs.missionStatement: Alliance for Water Efficiency is dedicated to promoting the efficient and sustainable use of water.
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
FIN 48 Footnote from Audited Financial Statements: The Alliance was granted an exemption from federal income taxes by the Internal Revenue Service pursuant to the provisions of Internal Revenue Code Section 501(c)(3). The Alliance qualifies for the charitable contribution deduction under Section 170(b)(1)(A)(vi) and has been classified as an Alliance that is not a private foundation under Section 509(a)(1). The tax-exempt purpose of the Alliance and the nature in which it operates is described above. The Alliance continues to operate in compliance with its tax-exempt purpose. The Alliance's annual information and income tax returns filed with the federal and state governments are subject to examination by the IRS, generally for three years after they were filed.

Membership shall be open to individuals and organizations that have an interest in promoting the mission and objectives of the Alliance; membership shall be effective upon payment of dues and approval of membership application. There shall be one class of members. Members shall have the right to attend the annual meeting of the Alliance and participate in policy discussions. Members shall have no right to vote on resolutions binding the Alliance. All members, voting or nonvoting, of chapters of the Alliance shall be members of the Alliance.

EXECUTIVE REVIEW - The President and CEO, Director of Finance & Operations, and other staff and/or consultants shall work in conjunction with the outside accounting firm engaged to prepare the Form 990 to review its accuracy prior to Board review and submission. It is the duty of the President and CEO to sign and certify that the Form 990 is accurate and complete. Upon resolution by the Board Audit Committee and presentation to the Board, the President and CEO shall ensure the filing the Form 990 by November 15, based on fiscal year ending Dec 31, each year. BOARD REVIEW - The Board Audit Committee shall review and approve Form 990 prior to full Board review and submission. It is the duty of the Audit Committee to report its findings concerning Form 990 to the Board at large prior to submission. The Board shall receive a copy of the IRS Form 990 in advance of filing, and a PDF of the final form shall be posted to the Board's online Handbook for future reference.

The organization has a Conflict of Interest Policy that requires that all Board directors disclose their conflicts of interest in any matter involving the organization or its policies. Every Board director files an annual statement attesting to their receipt of and subscription to the policy, and opportunities to declare potential conflicts of interest are provided at the beginning of every Board meeting. The minutes of the Board meetings record any such disclosures offered. Further, staff for the organization monitors all votes taken by the Board of Directors to ensure that no conflicts occur.

The Board of Directors meets in an Executive Session and the President and CEO's current employment contract (as applicable) is reviewed. Board members and officers receive no compensation other than reimbursement for travel expenses. Compensation for the President and CEO of the organization is determined by a vote of the Board of Directors. Compensation for the organization employees is determined by the President and CEO.

AWE's Annual Report is always posted on the AWE website and contains summary financial information from the yearly audits: https://allianceforwaterefficiency.org/resource-category/annual-report/ . The Annual Report is usually posted by August of the following year once the audit is completed. Also posted on the AWE website is AWE's most recent Strategic Plan, along with Strategic Plans adopted in the past: https://allianceforwaterefficiency.org/resource-type/strategic-plans/ Policies and other governance documents are made freely available to the public upon request.

Consultant and program costs: Program service expenses 208,393. Management and general expenses 0. Fundraising expenses 0. Total expenses 208,393.

The process has not changed from the prior year.

mainOrgs.name: Nine Mile Run Watershed Association Inc
mainOrgs.missionSummary: Restores and protects its watershed ecosystem while supporting resilient solutions for urban environments.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Not provided
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Core focus on watershed restoration and urban environmental health.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Single clear mission centered on watershed protection.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: With 24.6 years of operation and $1.4M in revenue, the organization demonstrates strong stability. Its mission directly aligns with critical water conservation efforts in watershed restoration.
mainOrgs.websiteRecentActivity: Organizing community events and educational programs focused on water equity and resilience.
mainOrgs.softFlags: Months unrestricted below threshold; Revenue concentrated in Contributions (95%); Program expense ratio below tier threshold (79%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: NINE MILE RUN WATERSHED ASSOCIATION RESTORES AND PROTECTS ITS WATERSHED ECOSYSTEM, WHILE WORKING REGIONALLY TO SUPPORT AND IMPLEMENT RESILIENT SOLUTIONS FOR A HEALTHY URBAN ENVIRONMENT.
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
DURING 2010, THE ASSOCIATION RECEIVED A DONATION OF $100,000 WHICH WAS PLACED INTO AN ENDOWMENT FUND HELD BY A THIRD PARTY WHO WAS GRANTED VARIANCE POWER. IF THE DONOR EXPLICITLY GRANTS A THIRD PARTY VARIANCE POWER, THE BENEFICIARY SHALL NOT RECOGNIZE ITS POTENTIAL FOR FUTURE DISTRIBUTIONS FROM THE ASSETS HELD BY THE THIRD PARTY. THEREFORE, THESE INVESTMENTS ARE NOT INCLUDED ON THE BALANCE SHEET. AT DECEMBER 31, 2024, THE ASSETS HELD IN THE ENDOWMENT FUND WERE $224,430 WHICH INCLUDES NET APPRECIATION IN VALUE OF $18,726 LESS FEES OF $2,699 FOR THE YEAR ENDED DECEMBER 31, 2024. DURING THE YEAR ENDED DECEMBER 31, 2024, THERE WERE $0 OF CONTRIBUTIONS TO THE ENDOWMENT.

THE 990 WAS REVIEWED FIRST BY MANAGEMENT AND THEN BY THE ORGANIZATION'S GOVERNING BODY AT A REGULARLY SCHEDULED BOARD OF DIRECTORS MEETING.

AT A BOARD OF DIRECTORS MEETING ANNUALLY, THE POLICY IS REVIEWED AND EXPLAINED TO NEW MEMBERS. ALL OF THE MEMBERS ARE ASKED TO SIGN A NEW FORM DISCLOSING ANY CURRENT OR ANTICIPATED CONFLICTS.

THE FINANCE COMMITTEE PREPARES AND REVIEWS THE ANNUAL BUDGET, INCLUDING OFFICERS' SALARIES, WHICH IS THEN APPROVED BY THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS.

THE FORM 990 IS AVAILABLE ON ITS WEBSITE IN COOPERATION WITH GUIDESTAR.

CONCEPT DESIGN: PROGRAM SERVICE EXPENSES 501,514. MANAGEMENT AND GENERAL EXPENSES 9,218. FUNDRAISING EXPENSES 2,797. TOTAL EXPENSES 513,529.

THE PROCESS HAS NOT CHANGED FROM THE PRIOR YEAR.

mainOrgs.name: Port Royal Sound Foundation
mainOrgs.missionSummary: To conserve the Port Royal Sound for environmental, cultural, and economic well-being.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Education, research, conservation, and organizational stability.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Focus on conservation aligns with water resources and environmental well-being.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Involves multiple related areas, centered around conservation and community engagement.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: With nearly 18 years of operation and stable cash reserves, the organization successfully engages in local water conservation initiatives despite a revenue decline.
mainOrgs.softFlags: Declining revenue trend (-44% over 3 yrs)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: TO CONSERVE THE PORT ROYAL SOUND FOR THE ENVIRONMENTAL, CULTURAL, AND ECONOMIC WELL-BEING OF OUR AREA IN 4 AREAS; EDUCATION, RESEARCH, CONSERVATION, AND ORGANIZATIONAL STABILITY.
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
THE ORGANIZATION IS EXEMPT FROM FEDERAL INCOME UNDER SECTION 501(C)(3) OF THE INTERNAL REVENUE CODE. THE ORGANIZATION FOLLOWS FASB ASC 740, INCOME TAXES, WHICH CLARIFIES THE ACCOUNTING FOR UNCERTAINTY IN INCOME TAXES BY PRESCRIBING THE RECOGNITION THRESHOLD A TAX POSITION IS REQUIRED TO MEET BEFORE BEING RECOGNIZED IN THE FINANCIAL STATEMENTS. IT ALSO PROVIDES GUIDANCE ON DERECOGNITION, CLASSIFICATION, INTEREST AND PENALTIES, ACCOUNTING IN INTERIM PERIODS, DISCLOSURE AND TRANSITION. MANAGEMENT BELIEVES THE ORGANIZATION HAS NO MATERIAL UNCERTAINTIES IN INCOME TAXES. GENERALLY, THE ORGANIZATION'S TAX RETURNS REMAIN OPEN FOR THREE YEARS SUBSEQUENT TO THEIR FILING FOR EXAMINATION BY GOVERNMENT AUTHORITIES.

VOLUNTEERS PROVIDE UNPAID ASSISTANCE IN THE OPERATIONS AND SUPPORT OF THE ORGANIZATION.

THE EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR PROVIDES COPIES OF FORM 990 TO THE BOARD OF TRUSTEES FOR THEIR REVIEW, PRIOR TO FILING THE RETURN.

BOARD MEMBERS ARE REQUIRED TO SIGN CONFLICT OF INTEREST STATEMENTS.

TOP MANAGEMENT'S SALARIES ARE APPROVED BY THE BOARD OF TRUSTEES.

UPON WRITTEN REQUEST.

mainOrgs.name: Clean Lakes Alliance Inc
mainOrgs.missionSummary: Championing lakes and watershed stewardship for collective benefit.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Watershed programming includes restoration and phosphorus reduction initiatives.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Core mission directly focuses on watershed and water quality stewardship.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Key watershed restoration programs and policy advocacy are interrelated.
mainOrgs.notableSignals: Established an agency endowment to support mission.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: CLEAN LAKES ALLIANCE has 15 years of experience, with mature metrics including $1.3M revenue and high current ratio of 31.74, reinforcing their commitment to lake and watershed stewardship.
mainOrgs.websiteRecentActivity: Upcoming events include the Frozen Assets Festival and Volunteer Days.
mainOrgs.softFlags: Months unrestricted below threshold; Declining revenue trend (-11% over 3 yrs); Revenue concentrated in Contributions (110%); Program expense ratio below tier threshold (67%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: OUR MISSION IS TO CHAMPION OUR LAKES AND WATERSHED STEWARDSHIP FOR THE BENEFIT OF ALL.
mainOrgs.program2Desc: WATERSHED PROGRAMMING WATERSHED RESTORATION IS AN ESSENTIAL PART OF CLEAN LAKES ALLIANCE, HELPING TO ACHIEVE THE GOAL OF 50% PHOSPHORUS REDUCTION BY 2025. THE PROGRAMS HAVE INCLUDED YAHARA PRIDE FARMS FINANCIAL ASSISTANCE, CLEAN LAKES GRANTS, STATE OF THE LAKES PROGRESS REPORTING, YAHARA CLEAN COMPACT COLLABORATIONS, DEMONSTRATION PILOTS, AND POLICY ADVOCACY. ON THE GROUND, THESE PROGRAMS CREATE THE FRAMEWORK TO IMPROVE THE QUALITY OF OUR WATERS.

FILES ARE MAINTAINED IN THE CLEAN LAKES ALLIANCE OFFICE FOR ALL GRANTS - INCOMING AND OUTGOING. FILES INCLUDE ORIGINAL GRANT APPLICATIONS, SUBSEQUENT COMMUNICATIONS AND DETAILS OF PAYMENT SCHEDULES, AMONG OTHER THINGS. DETAILS OF CASH RECEIPTS AND PAYMENTS FOR GRANTS ARE RECORDED IN THE CLEAN LAKES ALLIANCE ACCOUNTING SOFTWARE.

THE ORGANIZATION PAID FOR JOHN JAMES TYE III'S ROTARY CLUB OF MADISON DUES.

THE ORGANIZATION IS REPORTING THE NUMBER OF CONTRIBUTIONS RECEIVED IN COLUMN B.

THE PREPARED FORM 990 IS REVIEWED AND APPROVED BY THE MEMBERS OF THE GOVERNING BODY BEFORE THE RETURN IS FILED WITH THE IRS.

ANNUALLY, ALL DIRECTORS AND OFFICERS ARE REQUIRED TO DISCLOSE THEIR INTEREST THAT COULD GIVE RISE TO CONFLICTS. THE MEMBERS OF THE GOVERNING BODY MAKE DETERMINATIONS OF WHETHER A CONFLICT EXISTS AND REVIEW ACTUAL CONFLICTS. ANY PERSON WITH A CONFLICT IS PROHIBITED FROM PARTICIPATING IN THE GOVERNING BODY'S DELIBERATIONS AND DECISIONS IN THE TRANSACTION. WE HAVE A CONFLICT OF INTEREST AGREEMENT THAT GETS SIGNED BY ALL INCOMING MEMBERS.

THE INDEPENDENT MEMBERSHIP OF THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS ANNUALLY CONDUCTS A PERFORMANCE REVIEW OF THE PRESIDENT/CHIEF EXECUTIVE OFFICER. THE PRESIDENT/CHIEF EXECUTIVE OFFICER'S COMPENSATION IS REVIEWED AND COMPARED TO PUBLIC INFORMATION ABOUT COMPENSATION IN POSITIONS AT SIMILAR ORGANIZATIONS. THE BOARD REVIEWS AND APPROVES COMPENSATION FOR THE PRESIDENT/CHIEF EXECUTIVE OFFICER WITH AN OFFICIAL VOTE.

THE ORGANIZATION MAKES ITS GOVERNING DOCUMENTS, CONFLICT OF INTEREST POLICY, AND FINANCIAL STATEMENTS AVAILABLE TO THE PUBLIC UPON REQUEST. THE ORGANIZATION POSTS THE ANNUAL IMPACT REPORT INCLUDING FINANCIALS ON THE ORGANIZATION'S WEBSITE AND ON CANDID/GUIDESTAR PLATFORM.

mainOrgs.missionSummary: Igniting faith communities to protect and restore the Chesapeake Bay watershed.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Advocacy for watershed protection and community outreach.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Focused on protecting and restoring watershed.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Engages in advocacy and community outreach related to water conservation.

mainOrgs.scheduleO:
THE EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR COLLECTED TESTIMONIES AND OP-EDS FOR 2024 BILLS, WRITTEN TESTIMONY FOR MTB ACT/GSE, MARYLAND ADVOCACY WORK, AND WORK ON BOEM HEARINGS & COMMUNITY OUTREACH.

IPC FOLLOWS FASB ASC 740 (FIN 48), INCOME TAXES, WHICH PROVIDES GUIDANCE ON ACCOUNTING FOR UNCERTAIN TAX POSITIONS. FOR THE YEAR ENDED DECEMBER 31, 2024, THERE ARE NO KNOWN ITEMS WHICH WOULD RESULT IN A MATERIAL ACCRUAL RELATED TO WHERE IPC HAS FEDERAL OR STATE ATTRIBUTABLE TAX POSITIONS. THE FEDERAL FORM 990, RETURN OF ORGANIZATION EXEMPT FROM INCOME TAX, IS SUBJECT TO EXAMINATION BY THE IRS GENERALLY FOR THREE YEARS AFTER IT IS FILED. TAX YEARS ENDING DECEMBER 31, 2023, 2022 AND 2021 REMAIN OPEN WITH BOTH FEDERAL AND STATE TAXING AUTHORITIES.

THE ORGANIZATIONS FORM 990 IS PRESENTED TO THE BOARD OF TRUSTEES TO ALLOW THEM THE OPPORTUNITY TO THOROUGHLY REVIEW IT PRIOR TO ITS FILING WITH THE INTERNAL REVENUE SERVICE.

TRUSTEES, OFFICERS AND KEY EMPLOYEES ARE REQUIRED ANNUALLY TO CONFIRM THEIR COMPLIANCE WITH THE ORGANIZATIONS CONFLICT OF INTEREST POLICIES. THE BOARD OF TRUSTEES PERIODICALLY REVIEWS AND MONITORS COMPLIANCE AT ITS REGULARLY SCHEDULED MEETINGS.

THE ORGANIZATIONS BOARD OF TRUSTEES REVIEWS AND APPROVES THE COMPENSATION PAID TO THE EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR.

THE ORGANIZATION MAKES ITS GOVERNING DOCUMENTS, CONFLICT OF INTEREST POLICY AND FINANCIAL STATEMENTS AVAILABLE TO THE PUBLIC UPON REQUEST.

mainOrgs.scheduleO:
THE ORGANIZATIONS BOARD OF DIRECTORS IS A SELF - PERPETUATING BOARD. THE BOARD ACCEPTS RESIGNATION AND APPOINTS NEW BOARD MEMBERS FROM TIME TO TIME

THE BOARD REVIEWS THE ANNUAL FORM 990 AFTER IT HAS BEEN COMPLETED BY THE OUTSIDE ACCOUNTANT. THOSE REVIEWING THIS FORM INCLUDE THE PRESIDENT, VICE-PRESIDENT AND TREASURER OF THE BOARD. INFORMATION NEEDED TO COMPLETE THE FORM 990 WAS OBTAINED FROM THE BOOKS AND RECORDS OF THE ENTITY AND BY ANSWERS TO QUESTIONS RECEIVED FROM VARIOUS PERSONNEL OF THE ENTITY.

THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS REGULARLY MONITORS AND ENFORCES THE CONFLICT OF INTEREST POLICY. THE POLICY REQUIRES ALL BOARD MEMBERS TO DISCLOSE ANY PERSONAL, FINANCIAL, OR OTHER INTERESTS THAT COULD INFLUENCE THEIR DECISIONS RELATED TO THE ORGANIZATION. DISCLOSURES MUST BE MADE PROMPTLY UPON RECOGNITION AND ARE EVALUATED BY THE BOARD (EXCLUDING THE INTERESTED INDIVIDUAL) TO DETERMINE IF A CONFLICT EXISTS. IF A CONFLICT IS FOUND, THE AFFECTED PERSON IS BARRED FROM PARTICIPATING IN DISCUSSIONS, DELIBERATIONS, OR VOTES ON THE MATTER.

EMPLOYEE COMPENSATION IS DETERMINED BY THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS AFTER REVIEWING FACTORS SUCH AS AVERAGE COMPENSATION LEVELS FOR EMPLOYEES AT SIMILAR ORGANIZATIONS (COMPARABILITY DATA). ALL COMPENSATION DECISIONS MADE BY THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS ARE DOCUMENTED IN MINUTES OF THE BOARD.

ALL GOVERNING DOCUMENTS, POLICY MANUALS, FINANCIAL STATEMENTS AND COPIES OF FORM 990 ARE MADE AVAILABLE TO ANYONE WHO REQUESTS THIS INFORMATION IN ADDITION TO BEING REVIEWED AND MONITORED BY THE BOARD.

TOP MANAGEMENT COMPENSATION IS DETERMINED BY WITH CONSULTATION WITH BOARD TREASURER AND CHAIRMAN. WE HAVE LOOKED AT COMPARABILITY DATA AND I THINK ITS OBVIOUS IT IS A GOOD DEAL BELOW OTHER COMPARABLE SALARIES FOR THIS FIELD.

VARIOUS 79689. 67597. 8092. 4000.

Restoring life to the Chicago River goes beyond the plants and wildlife - a beautiful river lined with lush greenery and public walkways brings life to the community and connects local communities to nature.

mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Offers education, conferences, and networking within the water sector.
mainOrgs.notableSignals: Hosts large annual conference and multiple networking events.

mainOrgs.missionStatement: Dedicated to providing water education, training and leadership to protect public health and the environment.
mainOrgs.program2Desc: Conferences - The association has an annual conference, with over 1700 attendees in 2024, which provides continuing education on water and wastewater topics. It enhances member awareness of issues related to the industry and encourages networking. Additionally, CEU's (continuing education credits) are provided that allow state operators and engineers to meet mandatory continuing education requirements.
mainOrgs.program3Desc: Networking - The association hosts a variety of networking events throughout the year (24 in 2024) designed to help members connect with others in the industry.
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
The NC Safewater Endowment Fund was formed for the purpose of promoting safe water, increasing education through knowledge and understanding of safe water, and preserving and enhancing the water environment. The primary means for accomplishing this purpose is to provide scholarship funds to individuals who seek assistance in order to complete a course of study emphasizing the protection of public health by providing safe drinking water and/or protecting the quality and integrity of the water environment. In addition, the NC Safewater Endowment Fund awards funds to assist educators in providing enhanced water environment education to registered students and/or the public. The board considers specific donor restrictions on contributions of $25,000 or more.

The Association is exempt from federal income taxes under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code and is not a private foundation. Accordingly, no income taxes have been provided in the accompanying financial statements. Management has evaluated the effect of the guidance provided by GAAP on accounting for the uncertainty of income taxes. Management believes the Association continues to satisfy the requirements of a tax-exempt entity as of December 31, 2024. Management has evaluated all other tax positions that could have a significant effect on the financial statements and determined the Association had no uncertain income tax positions as of December 31, 2024.

Fundraising event expenses $4417 Rental expense reported on line VIII.06b $11312

Fundraising event expenses $4417 Rental expense reported on line VIII.06b $11312

No Water, No Beer is an annual event held to promote safe, clean water. Proceeds from the event are evenly distributed as general support grants to non-profits who share our mission. They are vetted for 501(c)(3) status and local community impact. There are no reporting requirements. NC WEA and NC AWWA also raise funds throughout the year for Water for People, a mission aligned 501(c)(3). The net proceeds from funds collected are granted to Water for People for general support and there are no reporting requirements. Each scholarship has specific eligibility criteria that recipients must meet. Scholarship awards are paid directly to the recipient's chosen educational institution. A scholarship recipient tracker is used to keep in contact with recipients, track payments, provide recipients with award reminders and follow-ups, and ensure additional requirements of the scholarship are met.

In 2024, the two related organizations, as disclosed on Schedule R, entered into a management agreement in which American Water Works Association North Carolina Section (NC AWWA), an affiliate tax exempt organization with identical board members and officers, agreed to carryout shared operations and collective work with North Carolina Water Environment Association, Inc. (NC WEA) under the trade name NC One Water. Revenues and expenses shall be attributed directly to NC AWWA or NC WEA and any revenues and expenses that benefited the shared operations will be allocated annually at the conclusion of each fiscal year, on a basis that reflects the relative benefit of the transaction to NC AWWA and NC WEA. NC WEA agrees to pay NC AWWA an amount equal to the NC WEA portion of all shared costs. See Schedule R, Part VII.

The association, along with its related organization NC American Water Works Association, has over 2300 members. These include national organization members, national individual members, and state level members.

Prior to filing, the Form 990s are signed by the appropriate board member of the respective organization in service for the year the tax return is prepared, reviewed and approved by the Finance Committee, and then distributed to the full Board for review.

When the new Executive Director was hired in 2024, the salary level and benefits package was reviewed and approved by a Selection Committee which consisted of independent board members and officers. The Selection Committee established the salary level and benefits package with reliance on documented comparability data to ensure it does not exceed comparable levels of compensation for nonprofit directors in the region of the county in which the Executive Director works. Minutes of their deliberations were documented contemporaneously, and the records are retained on file.

The three most recent Form 990s (including financial information) will be provided to the public upon request. The Bylaws, Articles of Incorporation, Exemption Letter, and all current policies are available on the organization website.

The organization shares almost all of its revenues and expenses with its partner organization, American Water Works Association North Carolina Section. The two organizations have very similar missions, although they are each affiliated with a different national organization. Very few activities are attributable to only one of the two organizations. Accordingly, nearly all operations are conducted jointly. Revenues and expenses are allocated to the respective organizations based on the purpose of the transactions. In most cases, revenues and expenses are shared between the two organizations, as most transactions benefit both organizations.

UPON SATISFACTORY REVIEW BY ALL OFFICERS, THE PREPARER WILL THEN SUBMIT THE COMPLETED REPORT. ALL MEMBERS ARE ENTITLED TO REVIEW THIS COMPLETED REPORT UPON REQUEST.

A CONFLICT OF INTEREST POLICY IS IN EFFECT.

THE OFFICERS DETERMINE THE SALARY FOR THE EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR.

THERE IS NO OFFICER COMPENSATION.

NO DOCUMENTS ARE AVAILABLE TO THE PUBLIC.

CONTINUED FROM LINE 28 ENVIRONMENTAL LAW CLINIC TO CONDUCT WEEKLY REVIEWS OF PUBLIC NOTICES FROM THE PA DEPT OF ENVIRONMENTAL PROTECTION, TO IDENTIFY PERMIT APPLICATIONS AND APPROVALS THAT MAY IMPACT THE THREE RIVERS WATERSHED. JOINED OTHER ORGANIZATIONS IN SUBMITTING LEGAL AND TECHNICAL COMMENTS ON PROPOSED DISCHARGE PERMITS OR PERMIT REVISIONS AND OTHER PROPOSED PROJECTS THAT THREATEN THE WATER QUALITY AND RECREATIONAL VALUE OF THE THREE RIVERS. PART III - ORGANIZATIONS PRIMARY EXEMPT PURPOSE - TO ENSURE THAT THE ALLEGHENY, OHIO AND MONONGAHELA RIVERS ARE PROTECTED AND OUR RIVER AND WATER QUALITY LAWS ARE ENFORCED.

WE DO THAT THROUGH AN EFFECTIVE MIX OF BOOTS-ON-THE-WATER STEWARDSHIP AND DATA-DRIVEN ADVOCACY DESIGNED TO PROTECT THE PUBLIC'S RIGHT TO CLEAN WATER.
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
A COPY OF THE 990 WAS PROVIDED TO ALL VOTING BOARD MEMBERS.

THE ORGANIZATION'S CONFLICT OF INTEREST POLICY IS REVIEWED WITH EACH BOARD MEMBER DURING THEIR INITIATION PROCESS UPON APPOINTMENT, AT WHICH TIME THEY MUST DISCLOSE ANY INTEREST THAT COULD GIVE RISE TO CONFLICTS. THE POLICY MAY ALSO BE VISITED IN INTERIM WHEN NEEDED IF THERE IS AN OCCASION WHEREIN A CONFLICT OF INTEREST MAY ARISE.

EACH YEAR, THE BOARD CHAIR AND MEMBERS OF THE EXECUTIVE COMMITTEE, COLLECTIVELY THE "COMPENSATION REVIEW TEAM": 1) COMPLETE AND RECOMMEND TO THE BOARD FOR APPROVAL A WRITTEN EVALUATION OF THE EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR'S PERFORMANCE RELATIVE TO BOARD APPROVED GOALS AND THEN SHARE WITH THE EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR, 2) PERFORM AN APPROPRIATE LEVEL OF REVIEW OF PEER COMPENSATION LEVELS FOR THE PURPOSE OF COMPARISON TO CHARLESTON WATERKEEPER'S COMPENSATION AWARDS, 3) REVIEW AND RECOMMEND TO THE BOARD FOR APPROVAL ANY YEAR-END BONUS COMPENSATION FOR THE EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR THAT MAY OR MAY NOT BE AWARDED, 4) REVIEW AND RECOMMEND TO THE BOARD FOR APPROVAL THE EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR'S COMPENSATION FOR THE COMING YEAR, 5) WORK WITH THE EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR TO PREPARE GOALS AND ADDITIONAL PERSONAL TARGETS FOR THE NEXT YEAR THAT WILL BE SUBMITTED TO THE BOARD FOR APPROVAL.

THE ORGANIZATION PROVIDES THESE DOCUMENTS UPON WRITTEN REQUEST, AS WELL AS ON SEVERAL ONLINE PLATFORMS INCLUDING: THE ORGANIZATION'S WEBSITE, THE SOUTH CAROLINA PUBLIC CHARITY SEARCH, AND ON GUIDESTAR'S CHARITY NAVIGATOR.

THE ORGANIZATION PROVIDES THESE DOCUMENTS UPON WRITTEN REQUEST.

mainOrgs.missionSummary: Sustain healthy watersheds for communities and economies, improving water quality through education and partnerships.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Workshops and events focused on watershed education and soil health.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Core focus on watershed health and water quality improvement.

mainOrgs.scheduleO:
No review was or will be conducted.

No documents available to the public.

Disposition of Pontoon in 2023 = -$12500

MWA Hosted the Following Events: On March 14, 2024, the Going Green for Ag workshop was hosted with 70 people in attendance. Event partners included ADM:regenerations, Allen County Soil and Water Conservation District, Conservation Cropping Systems Initiative, and Purdue University Extension. Key speakers were: 1.Barry Fisher, President of Fisher Soil Health LLC, spoke on maximizing cover crop success. 2.Rodney Rulon, Rulon Enterprises, discussed the economics of a systems approach. On April 20, 2024, a Hoosier Riverwatch Basic Training workshop was hosted in collaboration with Jackie Buck of the St. Joe River Watershed Initiative. The in-stream training section of the workshop had to be postponed due to high water. On May 20, 2024, the in-stream training section of the April 20th Hoosier Riverwatch Basic Training workshop was held. From June 18-22, 2024, MWA participated in educating Allen County residents in sewer system management and soil health at the Allen County Fair in partnership with the Allen County Soil and Water Conservation District. The Allen County SWCD had their education trailer at the fair for the entire week. On June 24, 2024, a Hoosier Riverwatch in-stream training was held for volunteers with the Little River Wetlands Project (LRWP). MWA hosted the training with LRWP so that their volunteers understood how to monitor water quality in streams flowing through LRWP properties. On August 7, 2024, MWA hosted the Day at the Lake tour in partnership with Allen County SWCD. 33 attendees were chartered to Put-In-Bay, OH on Lake Erie where they participated in a hands-on Science Cruise courtesy of The Ohio State Universitys Stone Laboratory, listened to a presentation on harmful algal blooms by Dr. Brian Alford, and enjoyed an informative tour of South Bass Island. On September 24, 2024, MWA co-hosted a Womens Learning Circle with the St. Joseph River Watershed Initiative. Ten attendees met at the ACRES Land Trust Office along the Cedar Creek in Huntertown, IN. The focus of the workshop was native plants and their impact on riparian habitat and water quality.

Welcome to Our Website!

Welcome to the Belle Fourche River Watershed Partnership's (BFRWP) Website!The website is here to provide you with information about how you can participate in the Partnership's activities. The Partnership is continually seeking innovative ways to improve conservation in the watershed while promoting economic sustainability and growth.

## Announcements

**Improved Irrigation in Western South Dakota with USDA NRCS’s Regional Conservation Partnership Program (RCPP)**

**Applications will be accepted for the BFRWP RCPP Project until January 23, 2026.**

_NATURAL RESOURCES CONSERVATION SERVICE (NRCS), Huron, South Dakota (SD), December 19, 2025 – The USDA Natural Resources Conservation Service (NRCS) in SD have announced a call for applications for the Belle Fourche River Watershed Partnership (BFRWP) Regional Conservation Partnership Program (RCPP) Project._

The applications are accepted until January 23, 2026. Producers are encouraged to contact the NRCS Belle Fourche office at (605) 892-3368 or the NRCS Sturgis office at (605) 347-4952 before January 23, 2026.

[NEWS RELEASE - Improved Irrigation in Western SD with USDA NRCS’s Regional Conservation Partnership Program (RCPP)](https://www.nrcs.usda.gov/state-offices/south-dakota/news/improved-irrigation-in-western-sd-with-usda-nrcss-regional)

## Learn More About

Our Coastal Network

The IOOS Association is a nonprofit organization comprising [11 regions](https://ioosassociation.org/regions/) of the [U.S. Integrated Ocean Observing System (IOOS)](https://ioos.noaa.gov/). Together with federal and nonfederal partners, we provide critical information that serves and protects the entire U.S. coastline. This collaborative network gives coastal communities, recreational ocean users, ocean economy industries, resource managers, scientists, and decision makers a big-picture view of the changing ocean and Great Lakes.

mainOrgs.name: Center For Water Security And Cooperation
mainOrgs.missionSummary: Advances water security through understanding, evaluating, and innovating water law and governance.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: International Water Security Challenge and Building Legal Understanding and Engagement programs.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Focus on legal frameworks for water security aligns directly with water conservation.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Two primary programs related to water law and governance.
mainOrgs.notableSignals: Established 10.5 years with independent board oversight for compensation decisions.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: The Center for Water Security and Cooperation has a solid revenue of $427K and maintains a strong program expense ratio of 0.8%. With 10.5 years of experience, they are well-established in advancing water security globally.
mainOrgs.softFlags: Revenue concentrated in Contributions (100%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: To advance water security by understanding, evaluating and innovating in water law and governance.
mainOrgs.program2Desc: The International Water Security Challenge (IWSC) program encompasses CWSC's work on setting legal benchmarks, assessing legal frameworks, and building legal and institutional frameworks for water security worldwide. This includes research on the impacts of law on the ability of water and wastewater systems to anticipate and manage the impacts of floods and droughts, realization of the human rights to water and sanitation and WASH standards in Africa, legal and service provision data collection, assessment of existing legal frameworks governing water resources and pollution control, and other means necessary to achieve water security. Also included are education and research initiatives that encourage deeper understanding of the rule of law in water security. Additionally included is coalition building in critical watersheds to inform and empower elected leaders to develop shared principles and shared framework for governing shared resources.
mainOrgs.program3Desc: The Building Legal Understanding and Engagement (BLUE) phone program provided technical assistance and education on water resource management, international water law, international criminal law, human rights, international water diplomacy, and water and sanitation law in the United States, countries around the world, and at the United Nations.
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
The 990 is provided for open notice and comment from board members, who have a period to provide comments or to ask any questions.

Any and all decisions that might create a conflict of interest are addressed by the officers of the corporation and, if necessary, presented to the governing board.

Compensation was reviewed by an independent board committee formed in 2022 and made up of three independent directors. The compensation committee reviewed executive compensation in other organizations and raised the pay for fiscal year 2022-2023 and onwards. The full board voted on the compensation, with all independent board members voting in favor.

The organization makes its governing documents available to the public upon request.

A PERSON HAVING A CONFLICT OF INTEREST SHALL NOT PARTICIPATE IN THE MEETING EXCEPT TO DISCLOSE MATERIAL ISSUES OF FACT AND TO RESPOND TO QUESTIONS. REMAINING MEMBERS WILL VOTE TO DETERMINE IF A CONFLICT OF INTEREST EXISTS. ANY PROCEEDINGS RESULTING FROM CONFLICTS OF INTEREST ARE DOCUMENTED IN THE MEETING MINUTES OR AS OTHERWISE APPROPRIATE.

THE ORGANIZATION MAKES ITS GOVERNING DOCUMENTS, CONFLICT OF INTEREST POLICY, AND FINANCIAL STATEMENTS AVAILABLE TO THE PUBLIC UPON REQUEST.

THE MINNESOTA FARM BUREAU FEDERATION (EIN: 41-0417230) PAYS ALL OF THE SALARIES. mainOrgs.websiteTextExcerpt:
Title: MAWRC – Minnesota Agricultural Water Resource Center

URL Source: https://www.mawrc.org/

Markdown Content:
### Minnesota Farmers: Naturally Resourceful

Minnesota farmers are leaders in the production of corn, soybeans, wheat, sugar beets, potatoes, milk, pork, beef and poultry products. They are also leaders in conservation.

Programs developed and administered by farmers provide a research and education foundation to continuously seek areas of improvement in farming practices and tracking improvements.

[![Image 1: Discovery Farms Minnesota](https://mawrc.org/wp-content/uploads/2016/03/discoverfarms_logo-300x132.png)](http://discoveryfarmsmn.org/)[](http://www.discoveryfarmsmn.org/)

**[Discovery Farms Minnesota](http://discoveryfarmsmn.org/)**is a farmer-led program combining water quality information and farming system information to help characterize the relationship between agricultural management and water quality.

The Minnesota Agricultural Water Resource Center is a non-profit research and education organization dedicated to assisting Minnesota farmers in addressing water quality concerns.

Our members and supporters include agricultural organizations representing more than 50,000 farmers and those who advise them. Please visit the website for our research program, Discovery Farms Minnesota, to learn more about Minnesota farmers and their commitment to environmental stewardship.

Title: Great Plains Tribal Water Alliance

URL Source: https://www.tribalwateralliance.org/

Markdown Content:
![Image 1](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/5f4444e36e3bfc6da676483a/8d5328b4-bd4b-477c-9858-cb0bbe7733cd/50554800-A637-35EA-67C611FCBE48E41D-1.jpg)

### GREAT PLAINS TRIBAL WATER ALLIANCE

The Great Plains Tribal Water Alliance is the leader in Tribal water resiliency in the Great Plains region.

We are dedicated to the protection and preservation of Tribal water rights.

## Make a Donation

Support our mission by contributing today.

![Image 2](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/5f4444e36e3bfc6da676483a/991f8449-89e3-470b-be0e-86652262bbe6/02+IMG_1956bcom+S.jpg)

The Great Plains Tribal Water Alliance is fighting uranium mining in the Black Hills and we need your help!

The Craven Canyon is an important spiritual place for the Lakota and other Indigenous Tribes.

There are traditional properties dating back 7,000 years or more. The South Dakota board of minerals and environment is considering approval of uranium exploration at this important place. Within the zone of impact of uranium exploration, there are 70 important cultural sites.

Red Canyon Creek and Craven Canyon has been degraded by open pit mines and remains vulnerable to further pollution.

There is a hearing for approval of uranium mining in Craven Canyon, tentatively scheduled for February 26, 2026. The GPTWA will present expert testimony in an effort to protect this sacred area

mainOrgs.name: Pennsylvania Organization For Watersheds And Rivers
mainOrgs.missionSummary: Empowering communities to protect and restore rivers and streams through education and advocacy.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Outreach and education for community watershed organizations, insurance access, and advocacy for water resource management.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Focused entirely on watershed protection and water quality.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Offers multiple related programs aimed at watershed organizations.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: POWR has a mature presence with over 31 years of experience. Its strong financial health, with a high current ratio and sufficient cash reserves, supports its mission of watershed protection and education.
mainOrgs.websiteRecentActivity: POWR announces 2026 Pennsylvania Sojourn Grant Awards and new capacity building tools for watershed organizations.
mainOrgs.softFlags: Months unrestricted below threshold; Declining revenue trend (-30% over 3 yrs); Revenue concentrated in Contributions (91%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: PENNSYLVANIA ORGANIZATION FOR WATERSHEDS AND RIVERS (POWR) PROVIDES THE TOOLS, TRAINING, EDUCATION AND RESOURCES THAT EMPOWER COMMUNITY WATERSHED ORGANIZATION LEADERS AND VOLUNTEERS TO PROTECT AND RESTORE RIVERS AND STREAMS, ADVOCATE FOR SOUND WATER RESOURCE MANAGEMENT AND POLICIES, AND FACILITATE STEWARDSHIP AND ENJOYMENT OF PENNSYLVANIA'S WATERWAYS.
mainOrgs.program2Desc: OUTREACH AND EDUCATION: WE OFFER TOOLS AND RESOURCES THAT ENABLE COMMUNITY WATERSHED ORGANIZATIONS (CWOS) TO REMAIN ACTIVE AND VIABLE. THESE INCLUDE WEBINARS ON TOPICS RANGING FROM INSURANCE, RETAINING VOLUNTEERS, BOARD DEVELOPMENT, CLEANUPS, RIPARIAN BUFFER DEVELOPMENT, SUCCESSION PLANNING, AND FUNDING. WE OFFER NETWORKING AND EDUCATIONAL OPPORTUNITIES AT REGIONAL AND STATEWIDE WORKSHOPS AND CONFERENCES. WE HOST A ROBUST WEBSITE THAT CONNECTS CWOS TO ONE ANOTHER AS WELL AS AN EXPANDED PROFESSIONAL NETWORK OF PROVIDERS OF SERVICES, PROMOTES THEIR VOLUNTEER OPPORTUNITIES AND STEWARDSHIP EVENTS, AND SPOTLIGHTS CWO ACCOMPLISHMENTS. FOR MORE INFORMATION, PLEASE VISIT OUR WEBSITE AT WWW.PAWATERSHEDS.ORG.
mainOrgs.program3Desc: INSURANCE: WE PROVIDE NON-PROFIT WATERSHED ORGANIZATION MEMBERS ACCESS TO REDUCED RATES FOR GENERAL LIABILITY INSURANCE COVERAGE THAT WOULD BE PROHIBITIVELY EXPENSIVE THROUGH OTHER AVENUES. BY ACCESSING THIS PROGRAM, POWR'S MEMBER ORGANIZATIONS CAN HOST MORE ACTIVITIES. THE SAVINGS STRETCH THEIR RESOURCES AVAILABLE FOR THE PROTECTION, RESTORATION, AND ENJOYMENT OF PENNSYLVANIA'S WATER RESOURCES, AS WELL AS ENABLING THEM TO CONDUCT PROGRAMS THAT FOSTER STEWARDSHIP, COMMUNICATION, LEADERSHIP, AND ACTION. FOR MORE INFORMATION, PLEASE VISIT OUR WEBSITE AT WWW.PAWATERSHEDS.ORG.
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
POWR QUALIFIES AS A TYPE 1 SUPPORTING ORGANIZATION UNDER SECTION 509(A)(3). INCOME TAX BENEFITS ARE RECOGNIZED FOR INCOME TAX POSITIONS TAKEN OR EXPECTED TO BE TAKEN IN A TAX RETURN, ONLY WHEN IT IS DETERMINED THAT THE INCOME TAX POSITION WILL MORE-LIKELY-THAN-NOT BE SUSTAINED UPON EXAMINATION BY TAXING AUTHORITIES. MANAGEMENT HAS ANALYZED TAX POSITIONS TAKEN FOR FILING WITH THE INTERNAL REVENUE SERVICE AND ALL STATE JURISDICTIONS WHERE IT OPERATES. MANAGEMENT BELIEVES THAT INCOME TAX FILING POSITIONS WILL BE SUSTAINED UPON EXAMINATION AND DOES NOT ANTICIPATE ANY ADJUSTMENTS THAT WOULD RESULT IN A MATERIAL ADVERSE EFFECT ON POWR'S FINANCIAL CONDITION, RESULTS OF OPERATIONS OR CASH FLOWS. ACCORDINGLY, MANAGEMENT HAS NOT RECORDED ANY RESERVES, OR RELATED ACCRUALS FOR INTEREST OR PENALTIES FOR UNCERTAIN INCOME TAX POSITIONS AT DECEMBER 31, 2023 AND DECEMBER 31, 2022. POWR'S POLICY IS TO CLASSIFY ANY INCOME TAX RELATED INTEREST AND PENALTIES TO INTEREST EXPENSE AND OTHER EXPENSES, RESPECTIVELY. POWR IS SUBJECT TO ROUTINE AUDITS BY TAXING JURISDICTIONS: HOWEVER, THERE ARE CURRENTLY NO AUDITS FOR ANY TAX PERIODS IN PROGRESS.

PERIODIC DISCUSSIONS AND SCHEDULED REPORTING.

PENNSYLVANNIA ENVIRONMENTAL COUNCIL (PEC) IS THE SOLE MEMBER OF PENNSYLVANNIA ORGANIZATION FOR WATERSHEDS (POWR) AND APPOINTS BOARD MEMBERS.

THE ORGANIZATION HAS ITS FORM 990 PREPARED BY AN OUTSIDE ACCOUNTING FIRM AND HAS ESTABLISHED THE FOLLOWING REVIEW PROCESS TO ENSURE THAT THE INFORMATION REPORTED IS COMPLETE AND ACCURATE: THE PRESIDENT AND THE TREASURER REVIEW THE FORM 990 AND PROVIDE A FINAL COPY OF THE FORM 990 TO EACH OF THE BOARD MEMBERS BEFORE IT IS FILED WITH THE IRS.

CONFLICT OF INTEREST DISCLOSURE FORMS ARE REQUIRED TO BE COMPLETED ANNUALLY. TRANSACTIONS POTENTIALLY INVOLVING CONFLICTS OF INTEREST ARE IDENTIFIED BY MANAGEMENT AND GIVEN SPECIAL ATTENTION BY THE BOARD.

POWR MAKES ITS BYLAWS, FORM 1023, FORM 990, AND AUDITED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS AVAILABLE TO THE PUBLIC UPON REQUEST.

THERE WAS NO CHANGE FROM THE PRIOR YEAR.

mainOrgs.missionStatement: ACARE WAS ORGANIZED FOR THE FOLLOWING MAIN PURPOSES:1. TO EDUCATE AND TRAIN THE NEXT GENERATION OF AFRICA'S FRESH WATER RESEARCHERS, SCIENTISTS, AND POLICY MAKERS ON THE AFRICAN GREAT LAKES;2. STRENGTHEN UNDERSTANDING OF THE IMPACTS OF BIOLOGICAL, PHYSICAL, CHEMICAL, ECONOMIC, POLITICAL, SOCIAL, AND TECHNICAL FACTORS ON THE AFRICAN FRESHWATER RESOURCES; AND3. ENHANCE GLOBAL COLLABORATION BETWEEN FRESHWATER LEARNING INSTITUTIONS, ORGANIZATIONS, AND ENTITIES TO ADDRESS THE CHALLENGES FACING ALL FRESHWATER RESOURCES WITH A PARTICULAR FOCUS ON THE AFRICAN GREAT LAKES.

mainOrgs.name: Partners For Clean Streams Inc
mainOrgs.missionSummary: Promotes clean rivers, streams, and lakes, and encourages local ownership of natural resources.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Supports water action initiatives and collaboration for water quality in the Maumee Area of Concern.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Core focus on maintaining clean waterways and environmental stewardship.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Two main programs focused on water quality and community science related to local water bodies.
mainOrgs.notableSignals: Established partnerships and online tools for community engagement.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: PARTNERS FOR CLEAN STREAMS has been active for over 19 years, with a strong program expense ratio and a healthy current ratio, indicating good financial health. Their commitment to community involvement and high-quality water data enhances their mission of promoting clean waterways.
mainOrgs.websiteRecentActivity: Programs like 'Clean Your Streams Day' and 'Get the Lead Out' actively promote clean waterways.
mainOrgs.softFlags: Months unrestricted below threshold; Revenue concentrated in Contributions (97%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: PARTNERS FOR CLEAN STREAMS STRIVES FOR ABUNDANT OPEN SPACE AND A HIGH QUALITY NATURAL ENVIRONMENT; ADEQUATE FLOOD WATER STORAGE CAPACITIES AND FLOURISHING WILDLIFE; STAKEHOLDERS WHO TAKE LOCAL OWNERSHIP IN THEIR RESOURCES; AND RIVERS, STREAMS AND LAKES THAT ARE CLEAN, CLEAR AND SAFE.
mainOrgs.program2Desc: PARTNERS FOR CLEAN STREAMS (PCS) CONTINUED TO SUPPORT THE MAUMEE AREA OF CONCERN (AOC) ADVISORY COMMITTEE ACTIVITIES THROUGH A GRANT FROM THE OHIO EPA. WE PLANNED AND ORGANIZED MEETINGS, COMMITTEES, AND ACTIVITIES TO FACILITATE COLLABORATION AND PROGRESS. PCS MAINTAINED A SOPHISTICATED ONLINE DATABASE AND DECISION-MAKING TOOL (DMDS), WHILE ALSO UPDATING AOC SOCIAL MEDIA, NEWSLETTERS, AND OUR WEBSITE TO KEEP STAKEHOLDERS INFORMED. ADDITIONALLY, PCS COLLABORATED WITH MULTIPLE PARTNERS TO DEVELOP POTENTIAL RESTORATION PROJECTS IN THE MAUMEE AOC AND PROVIDED GUIDANCE ON RESTORATION EFFORTS IN THE NORTHWEST OHIO REGION.
mainOrgs.program3Desc: PARTNERS FOR CLEAN STREAMS (PCS) COMMUNITY WATER ACTION IN TOLEDO (CWAT) PROGRAM IS A COLLABORATIVE INITIATIVE FOCUSED ON COMMUNITY SCIENCE IN NORTHWEST OHIO AND THE LAKE ERIE BASIN, AIMING TO COLLECT HIGH-QUALITY WATER DATA AND EMPOWER INDIVIDUALS AND ORGANIZATIONS TO ADVOCATE FOR LOCAL WATERWAYS. AS PART OF THE CLEVELAND WATER ALLIANCE'S LAKE ERIE VOLUNTEER SCIENCE NETWORK (LEVSN), CWAT UNITES PARTNERS TO ENHANCE SAMPLING EFFORTS, SHARE RESOURCES, AND DELIVER ROBUST DATA TO ADDRESS ONGOING WATER QUALITY ISSUES AND TO ENSURE SCIENTIFIC RIGOR AND DATA COMPARABILITY.

mainOrgs.name: Chesapeake Stormwater Network Inc
mainOrgs.missionSummary: Advocates for sustainable stormwater management in the Chesapeake Bay.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Education, technical assistance, and advocacy for stormwater reform and implementation.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Directly addresses stormwater management critical to water quality in the Chesapeake Bay.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Engages in multiple related activities focused on stormwater issues.
mainOrgs.notableSignals: Connects thousands of individuals across the Chesapeake Bay.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: Chesapeake Stormwater Network is a mature organization with 16.7 years of operation. Despite a recent revenue decline of 13.6%, it maintains strong cash reserves and a high current ratio, indicating financial stability for ongoing mission success.
mainOrgs.softFlags: Declining revenue trend (-14% over 3 yrs); Revenue concentrated in Contributions (100%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: SEE SCHEDULE O
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
The Chesapeake Stormwater Network advocates for reform of federal, state, and local laws, permits, regulations and design manuals to promote more sustainable stormwater management in the Chesapeake Bay. The interactive network aligns and integrates the efforts of thousands of individuals working on the stormwater problem across the Bay. The CSN seeks to improve on the ground implementation of more sustainable stormwater management and environmental site design practices in each of 1300 communities and seven states in the Chesapeake Bay Watershed.

The network aligns and integrates the efforts of thousands of individuals working in the stormwater problem across the Bay and focuses on the improved stormwater manuals, permits, programs, training, and technical assistance to meet its mission. The key functions of the network include: -Educate the Bay advocacy community about the stormwater problem and its solutions, and create a coalition to create change at the local, state, and federal level. -Articulate a simple and unified approach to sustainable stormwater practices that can be understood and applied by all stormwater interest in the Bay. -Link the many stormwater interests in the Chesapeake Bay together into a single integrated network and provide them with the best research, engineering and land planning tools. -Import the best stormwater solutions developed elsewhere in the world to communities in the Chesapeake Bay. -Work at the state level to ensure stormwater laws, permits, regulations and design manuals are reformed to require that sustainable stormwater practices are applied at every development site. -Ensure that each are backed up by a stringent compliance and enforcement tools that provide real accountability that practices are fully protecting the Chesapeake Bay. -Work at the local level with the progessive early adopter communities to change the culture and codes that are currently impeding sustainable stormwater practices. -Work directly with progressive early adopters and design consultants to finance, design, build and monitor the next generation of effective sustainable stormwater practices. -Challenge hesitant communities to adopt sustainable stormwater practices through a combination of education, local legislation, ms4 permit enforcement and litigation, when needed. -Work with partners to institute a Bay-wide training program for designers and plan reviewers to test and certify their ability to effectiviely implement sustainable stormwater practices on every development site. -Advocate for an expanded Chesapeake Bay Stormwater research program at the state and federal level to fill in key knowledge gaps that impede sustainable stormwater practices. -Provide direct technical support to communities to restore streams, forest and wetlands damaged by past development, and work with them to invest in effective watershed restoration practices.

The Form 990 is initially reviewed by the Treasurer for accuracy and completeness. After the initial review, the Form 990 is distributed to the full board for approval prior to submission with the IRS.

Conflict of interest disclosure forms are completed by the full board and staff at the annual board retreat and any potential conflicts are discussed at full board meetings, per the procedures outlined in the CSN conflict of interest policy.

A sub committee of the Board conducts an annual performance review of the Executive Director, as well as an analysis of how current compensation compares to peers in nonprofit and watershed management fields. The Executive Director provides a self-assessment to the subcommittee, which meets independently to make a written recommendation for Executive Director goals, compensation and job description. The full board discusses the recommendation at a regular board meeting, and votes on a final recommendation. The final action is documented in writing, and is signed by the Executive Director, Board President and Treasurer.

Governing documents, financial statements and the Form 990 are available upon request.

mainOrgs.missionStatement: AMAZON FRONTLINES BUILDS POWER WITH INDIGENOUS PEOPLES TO DEFEND THEIR RIGHTS TO LAND, LIFE AND CULTURAL SURVIVAL IN THE AMAZON RAINFOREST.
mainOrgs.program2Desc: BUILDING AUTONOMY AND SCALING PARTNERSHIPS: AMAZON FRONTLINES IS EXPANDING OUR PARTNERSHIPS WITH INDIGENOUS COMMUNITIES TO ACHIEVE A MUCH WIDER IMPACT IN PROTECTING THE UPPER AMAZON. AS WE SCALE OUR FRONTLINE MODEL WITH NEW ORGANIZATIONS ACROSS THE REGION, IT'S CRITICAL THAT WE STAY TRUE TO OUR CORE MISSION OF ENSURING THAT OUR INDIGENOUS PARTNERS ARE LEADING, LEARNING, AND OWNING THE PROCESSES THAT DIRECTLY RESPOND TO THEIR OWN VISIONS FOR THE WELL-BEING OF THEIR COMMUNITIES AND TERRITORIES. OUR PARTNERSHIP DEPARTMENT WORKS TOGETHER WITH THE LEADERSHIP AND STAFF OF REGIONAL INDIGENOUS ORGANIZATIONS TO BUILD THEIR ORGANIZATIONAL CAPACITY THROUGH TAILORED TRAINING IN PROGRAM MANAGEMENT, COMMUNICATIONS AND ACCOUNTING, AS WELL AS SUPPORTING THEM IN ACCESSING GLOBAL NETWORKS AND FUNDING TO STRENGTHEN THEIR FIGHT TO PROTECT THEIR CLIMATE-CRITICAL TERRITORIES.THROUGH OUR GRANTMAKING FACILITY WE DIRECTLY PROVIDE INDIGENOUS LEADERS AND ORGANIZATIONS WITH THE RESOURCES NEEDED TO SUPPORT A WIDE ARRAY OF ACTIVITIES, RANGING FROM CONVENING EVENTS AND COMMUNITY ASSEMBLIES CRUCIAL FOR ORGANIZATIONAL STRENGTHENING AND COMMUNITY COHESION, TO THE CONSTRUCTION OF SCHOOLS IN INDIGENOUS COMMUNITIES AND THE DEVELOPMENT OF COMMUNICATION MATERIALS AND TERRITORIAL DEFENSE TRAINING PROGRAMS, ALL OF WHICH CONTINUED IN 2024. WE CONTINUE MAKING GRANTS FOR HUMANITARIAN AID IN RESPONSE TO REGIONAL CONFLICTS AND ENVIRONMENTAL CRISES. FOR EXAMPLE, WE HAVE BEEN ABLE TO RAPIDLY REGRANT EMERGENCY FUNDS TO INDIGENOUS SIONA COMMUNITIES IN RESPONSE TO THE ONGOING ARMED CONFLICT IN COLOMBIA'S PUTUMAYO PROVINCE WHICH IS CURRENTLY DISPLACING AND THREATENING THE LIVES OF SIONA COMMUNITIES.
mainOrgs.program3Desc: BUILDING COMMUNITY RESILIENCE: IN EFFORTS TO UPLIFT INDIGENOUS WOMEN AS LEADERS IN THEIR COMMUNITIES AND DEVELOP ECONOMIC ALTERNATIVES TO FOSSIL FUEL EXTRACTION, WE EMPOWER ASPIRING WOMEN ENTREPRENEURS FROM ACROSS THE REGION THROUGH OUR WOMEN'S LEADERSHIP AND ENTREPRENEURSHIP SCHOOL, OFFERING WORKSHOPS ON LEADERSHIP AND ORGANIZATIONAL CAPACITY BUILDING FOR WOMEN-LED ASSOCIATIONS, INTRODUCTIONS TO BASIC IT, COMPUTER SKILLS, ACCOUNTING, FINANCES, E-COMMERCE AND DIGITAL COMMUNICATIONS.SINCE 2023, WE HAVE SUPPORTED INDIGENOUS NATIONALITIES, CURRENTLY THE WAORANI, KOFAN AND SIEKOPAI, TO DEVELOP AND IMPLEMENT THEIR OWN INTERCULTURAL EDUCATION MODELS. THESE PROCESSES INVOLVE ENTIRE COMMUNITIES AND NATIONS, FROM ELDERS TO PARENTS TO INDIGENOUS AUTHORITIES, UNIFYING A COLLECTIVE STRUGGLE FOR THE CULTURAL SURVIVAL OF THEIR PEOPLES, AND THE PASSING DOWN OF THEIR ANCESTRAL FOREST KNOWLEDGE TO INDIGENOUS YOUTH, WHO ULTIMATELY WILL BE THE NEXT GENERATION OF LEADERS FIGHTING ON THE FRONTLINES OF THE CLIMATE CRISIS TO DEFEND THE AMAZON RAINFOREST.

mainOrgs.name: Sustainable Ocean Alliance Inc
mainOrgs.missionSummary: Empowering the next generation to preserve ocean health and sustainability.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Ocean Solutions Program provides grants and support for youth-led ocean projects.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Focused on ocean health with implications for marine conservation and sustainability.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Programs address both direct support for projects and marketing for awareness.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: With 7.8 years of experience and $5.3M in revenue, SUSTAINABLE OCEAN ALLIANCE INC effectively empowers youth to tackle ocean conservation issues, showcasing a solid growth trend and prudent financial management.
mainOrgs.websiteRecentActivity: Thousands of young leaders activated across 115 countries for ocean restoration.
mainOrgs.softFlags: Revenue concentrated in Contributions (90%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: SUSTAINABLE OCEAN ALLIANCE EMPOWERS THE NEXT GENERATION TO BECOME LEADERS IN PRESERVING THE HEALTH AND SUSTAINABILITY OF OUR OCEAN.
mainOrgs.program2Desc: THE OCEAN SOLUTIONS PROGRAM PROVIDES FINANCIAL GRANTS, FELLOWSHIPS AND MENTORSHIP TO PROJECT LEADERS WHO ARE UNDER 36 AND ADDRESSING CRITICAL OCEAN ISSUES WITH GRASSROOTS, COLLABORATIVE APPROACHES. THE OCEAN SOLUTION PROGRAM ALSO INVESTS IN EARLY STAGE STARTUP COMPANIES DEVELOPING INNOVATIVE SOLUTIONS TO SUSTAIN AND RESTORE THE HEALTH OF OUR OCEAN.
mainOrgs.program3Desc: PROGRAM MARKETING: STRATEGIC PROMOTION AND COMMUNICATION AIMED SPECIFICALLY TO INCREASE AWARENESS OF THE GLOBAL MOVEMENT TO SAVE OUR OCEAN. THIS INCLUDES EDUCATION PROGRAMS, PROGRAM LAUNCHES, AND TARGETED MARKETING TO DRIVE PARTICIPATION AND ENGAGEMENT.
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
EACH GRANT APPLICATION IS REVIEWED BY 2 SOA STAFF AND SCORED IN 5 CORE CRITERIA (MISSION, ALIGNMENT, IMPACT, FEASIBILITY, SCALABILITY/REPLICABILITY, FINANCIALS) AND 5 SECONDARY CRITERIA (SOA ENGAGEMENT/STRENGTH OF RELATIONSHIP, COMMUNICATION SKILLS, MARKETABILITY/STORYTELLING, ORIGINALITY, SCIENTIFIC RIGOR). THOSE GRANTS SCORING HIGHLY, PARTICULARLY IN SCIENTIFIC RIGOR, ARE PASSED ALONG TO A SCIENTIST FOR REVIEW. LAST YEAR, THE PANEL CONSISTED OF 3 MEMBERS AT NOAA, UCSB, AND RUTGERS. EACH SELECTED GRANTEE MEETS WITH SOA STAFF. THEN THEY SUBMIT A FORM PROVIDING THEIR BANK ACCOUNT INFORMATION, PHOTO ID, AND AFFIRM THEIR GOOD LEGAL STANDING (NO SANCTIONS, LITIGATION, OR OTHER LEGAL ISSUES). THEIR INFORMATION IS RUN THROUGH THE US STATE DEPT LIST OF SANCTIONED ENTITIES (OFAC). THE FINAL STEP IS A GRANT AGREEMENT SIGNED BY GRANTEE AND SOA PROGRAM OFFICER. EACH GRANTEE IS ISSUED THEIR FUNDS IN TWO INSTALLMENTS: THE SECOND IS CONTINGENT UPON SATISFACTORY COMPLETION OF THEIR INTERIM FINANCIAL AND PROGRESS REPORTS. EACH GRANTEE SUBMITS A FINAL REPORT AS WELL. AS GRANTS SUBMIT FINAL REPORTS AND THEIR IMPACT IS EVALUATED, WE ACTIVELY EVALUATE THE OVERALL COMPOSITION AND BALANCE OF THE ENVIRONMENTAL ISSUES ADDRESSED AND OUR "ROI" THROUGH THESE GRANTS.

AS GRANTS SUBMIT FINAL REPORTS AND THEIR IMPACT IS EVALUATED, WE ACTIVELY EVALUATE THE OVERALL COMPOSITION AND BALANCE OF THE ENVIRONMENTAL ISSUES ADDRESSED AND OUR "ROI" THROUGH THESE GRANTS.

AS PER OUR TRAVEL POLICY, ANY FLIGHT EXTENDING LONGER THAN 6 HOURS MAY BE UPDRADED TO BUSINESS OR FIRST CLASS AS LONG AS THE COST DOES NOT EXCEED $1,000 USD.

KIMBERLY VALLEE RECEIVED $60,000 SEVERANCE PAYMENT DURING THE YEAR.

SUSTAINABLE OCEAN ALLIANCE HAS BEEN ADDED A NEW PROGRAM UNDER ECOPRENEUR NETWORK IN 2024.

THE FORM 990 WAS PREPARED BY AN INDEPENDENT TAX ACCOUNTANT, WHICH WAS THEN REVIEWED BY THE HEAD OF FINANCE AND OPERATIONS; ADJUSTMENTS ARE MADE, AS NECESSARY. A COMPLETE COPY OF THE FORM 990 WAS THEN PROVIDED TO ALL MEMBERS OF THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS AND CEO PRIOR TO FILING WITH THE INTERNAL REVENUE SERVICE.

THE HEAD OF FINANCE AND OPERATIONS DISTRIBUTES ANNUALLY TO ALL DIRECTORS, OFFICERS, AND KEY EMPLOYEES (AS IDENTIFIED BY THE CORPORATION) A FORM SOLICITING THE DISCLOSURE OF ALL CONFLICTS OF INTEREST. A DIRECTOR OR OFFICER WHO LEARNS ABOUT A POTENTIAL CONFLICT OF INTEREST DISCLOSES PROMPTLY TO THE SECRETARY OF THE CORPORATION THE MATERIAL FACTS SURROUNDING ANY POTENTIAL CONFLICT OF INTEREST, INCLUDING SPECIFIC INFORMATION CONCERNING THE TERMS OF ANY CONTRACT OR TRANSACTION WITH THE CORPORATION. ALL EFFORTS SHOULD BE MADE TO DISCLOSE ANY SUCH CONTRACT OR TRANSACTION AND HAVE IT APPROVED BY DISINTERESTED MEMBERS OF THE BOARD BEFORE THE ARRANGEMENT IS ENTERED INTO. AN EMPLOYEE OF THE CORPORATION WITH A POTENTIAL CONFLICT OF INTEREST PROMPTLY AND FULLY DISCLOSES THE POTENTIAL CONFLICT TO THE EMPLOYEE'S SUPERVISOR. THE EMPLOYEE THEREAFTER REFRAINS FROM PARTICIPATING IN DELIBERATIONS AND DISCUSSIONS, AS WELL AS ANY DECISIONS. THE CHAIR OF THE BOARD IS ULTIMATELY RESPONSIBLE FOR DETERMINING THE PROPER WAY TO HANDLE CORPORATE DECISIONS WHICH INVOLVE UNRESOLVED EMPLOYEE CONFLICTS OF INTEREST. DUE TO CHANGES IN KEY STAFFING IN 2020, THE CONFLICT OF INTEREST FORMS WERE NOT GATHERED, HOWEVER, NO CONFLICTS WERE DISCOVERED OR NOTED DURING THE YEAR. IN 2021, SOA RESUMED NORMAL PROCEDURES.

THE BOARD APPROVES, IN ADVANCE, COMPENSATION PACKAGES FOR OFFICERS AND KEY EMPLOYEES OF THE CORPORATION, HAVING OBTAINED AND CONSIDERED APPROPRIATE DATA CONCERNING COMPARABLE COMPENSATION PAID TO SIMILAR OFFICERS IN LIKE CIRCUMSTANCES. THE BOARD SETS FORTH THE BASIS FOR ITS DECISIONS WITH RESPECT TO COMPENSATION IN THE MINUTES OF THE MEETING AT WHICH THE DECISIONS ARE MADE, INCLUDING THE CONCLUSIONS OF THE EVALUATION AND THE BASIS FOR DETERMINING THAT THE INDIVIDUAL'S COMPENSATION IS REASONABLE IN LIGHT OF THE EVALUATION AND THE COMPARABILITY DATA. THE LAST COMPENSATION REVIEW TOOK PLACE IN DECEMBER 2024.

THE ORGANIZATION MAKES ITS GOVERNING DOCUMENTS, CONFLICT OF INTEREST POLICY, AND FINANCIAL STATEMENTS AVAILABLE UPON REQUEST.

mainOrgs.name: Santa Ana Watershed Association Of Rcds
mainOrgs.missionSummary: SAWA protects and improves the Santa Ana River watershed ecosystem.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Invasive species removal and habitat restoration.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Core focus on watershed conservation and species protection.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Central focus on invasive species and habitat restoration.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: With 22.8 years of experience and $3.7M in revenue, SAWA effectively implements watershed protection, though faces declining revenue trends. Strong financial metrics show sustainability.
mainOrgs.websiteRecentActivity: SAWA has removed nearly 5,000 acres of invasive plants and continues habitat restoration and wildlife management services.
mainOrgs.hardRedFlags: Days cash on hand critically low (32); Board below tier minimum (5 < 7)
mainOrgs.softFlags: Months unrestricted below threshold; Declining revenue trend (-18% over 3 yrs); Revenue concentrated in Program Services (95%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: The Santa Ana River Watershed Association SAWA develops, coordinates and implements natural resource programs that support a sustainable ecosystem and social benefits from the San Bernardino Mountains to the Pacific Ocean. SAWA is committed to the protection and improvement of areas within the Santa Ana River watershed. Major FOCI of SAWA are the removal of invasive species, which is a significant problem throughout the watershed, native habitat enhancement and the protection of endangered and threatened species.
mainOrgs.program2Desc: Invasive plant and animal removal-habitat restoration, primarily through Arundo control, is the current central focus of the watershed program. The endangered species work and other wildlife monitoring is necessary for compliance with regulatory permits. SAWA conducted on-going maintenance and control of non-native species on over 6,957 acres of habitat. In addition, .8 acres of habitat were actively planted, restored and managed by SAWA.
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
The Santa Ana Watershed Association SAWA holds a conservation easement and property interest to ensure the perpetual protection of the conservation values of property within which mitigation responsibilities are carried out. The durability of the propertys conservation easement is dependent on the SAWAs ability to enforce its terms. The following guidelines and procedures help assure that appropriate steps are taken to document any violation and to help SAWA develop a strategy to resolve it so that conservation values are preserved and protected: Maintain the conservation purposes of the conservation easement and protect the lands conservation values in perpetuity, in keeping with the documented intent of the original agreements. Conduct routine property inspections and respond swiftly to all suspected violations. Maintain perspective and keep SAWAs response proportional to the severity and circumstances of the violation. Protect SAWAs legal rights and financial investment in the conserved property. Assess responsibility for violations and act promptly to resolve the issues. Comply with all applicable laws.

Before the acceptance of an easement, the easement is appraised. The easement is recorded on the balance sheet at fair value plus ancillary costs on the date the easement is recorded. The fair value is recorded as contribution revenue on the income statement on the date the easement is recorded. Expenses related to the easement are recorded as program cost.

Endowment for Mockingbird Canyon Conservation Easement upkeep and maintenance continuity.

The organization has contemporaneously documented the meetings held by each committee and makes recommendations to the governing body.

The 990 was provided to the Board Members for review and comment prior to it being filed with the Internal Revenue Service.

Executive Director monitors and discloses as deemed necessary.

Administrative committee determines compensation and makes a recommendation to the board. The entire board approves the executive's compensation.

They are made available upon request.

The Organization's audit and finance committee assumes responsibility for oversight of the audit and the selection of the independent accountant. The audit and auditor's are approved by the entire board of directors.

## SAWA is committed to protecting and improving natural areas of the Santa Ana River Watershed through the removal of invasive species, enhancement of native habitat and protection of endangered and threatened species.

## MAKE A DIFFERENCE

## What We Do

With a veteran staff of permitted and licensed habitat restoration specialists and biologists, the Santa Ana Watershed Association—a 501(c)(3) non-profit organization—has removed nearly 5,000 acres of Arundo and other invasive plants throughout the watershed.

SAWA provides long-term habitat restoration and wildlife management services for a variety of clients, including local, state and federal agencies, conservation organizations and private citizens.
