IN 2023, BISCAYNE BAY WATERKEEPER (D/B/A MIAMI WATERKEEPER) BUILT ON OUR REPUTATION BY NAVIGATING CHALLENGES, SEIZING OPPORTUNITIES, AND CONTINUING OUR UNWAVERING COMMITMENT TO SAFEGUARDING OUR PRECIOUS WATERWAYS AND FINDING LOCAL RESILIENCY SOLUTIONS TO GLOBAL CHALLENGES. WE UPDATED OUR MISSION AND VISION TO BETTER REFLECT THE WORK WE DO AND THOSE WE SERVE. THE MISSION OF MIAMI WATERKEEPER IS NOW TO PROTECT SOUTH FLORIDA'S WATERS BY ADVOCATING FOR RESILIENT SOLUTIONS GROUNDED IN SCIENCE, ROOTED IN NATURE, AND DRIVEN BY COMMUNITY. WE HAVE A VISION FOR A RESILIENT SOUTH FLORIDA THAT IS A GLOBAL LEADER IN CLEAN WATER SOLUTIONS, WHERE THRIVING COMMUNITIES AND NATURE COEXIST. SINCE 2010, MIAMI WATERKEEPER HAS DEFENDED THE RIGHT TO USE AND ENJOY CLEAN WATER. WE ARE RECOGNIZED IN THE COMMUNITY AS A TRUSTED SOURCE OF INFORMATION AND A LEADING VOICE AND ADVOCATE FOR BISCAYNE BAY AND ITS SURROUNDING WATERSHED. OUR WORK IS EFFECTIVE BECAUSE WE EMPLOY A MULTI- FACETED APPROACH THAT INCLUDES SCIENTIFIC RESEARCH, ADVOCACY, COMMUNITY EDUCATION AND OUTREACH, AND WHEN NECESSARY, LEGAL ACTION. THROUGH OUR EFFORTS, WE PROTECT A HEALTHY WATERSHED AND A VIBRANT COASTAL CULTURE IN SOUTH FLORIDA FOR GENERATIONS TO COME. MIAMI WATERKEEPER FOCUSES ON THREE MAIN AREAS: CLEAN WATER, ECOSYSTEM PROTECTION, AND SEA LEVEL RISE RESILIENCY. FIRST, WE TACKLE ISSUES OF POLLUTION TO ENSURE CLEAN WATER THROUGHOUT THE BISCAYNE BAY WATERSHED, FROM THE EVERGLADES TO OUR CORAL REEFS. SECOND, WE PROTECT THE ECOSYSTEMS THAT DEPEND ON THAT CLEAN WATER, INCLUDING CORALS, SEAGRASSES, AND MANGROVES. FINALLY, WE FOCUS ON SEA LEVEL RISE RESILIENCY, WHICH IMPACTS ALL AREAS OF OUR WORK. WE EMPLOY A MULTIDISCIPLINARY APPROACH TO EACH FOCUS AREA: SCIENCE AND RESEARCH EVERYTHING WE DO STARTS WITH SCIENCE. WE ANALYZE EXISTING RESEARCH AND LEAD LARGE-SCALE NOVEL RESEARCH PROJECTS TO FILL IN KNOWLEDGE GAPS. WE ALSO VISIT OVER 30 LOCATIONS ACROSS MIAMI-DADE AND BROWARD COUNTIES EVERY WEEK TO COLLECT AND ANALYZE WATER QUALITY FROM YOUR FAVORITE WATERWAYS AND BEACHES, SO THAT WE CAN INFORM THE PUBLIC IN REAL-TIME ON WHERE IT'S SAFE TO SWIM AND ENJOY THE WATER. IN 2023, WE ENHANCED OUR WATER QUALITY TESTING CAPACITY WITH THE ADDITION OF AERIAL AND UNDERWATER DRONES TO OUR POLLUTION REPORTING AND RESPONSE PROGRAM. THIS UPGRADE NOT ONLY AUGMENTED OUR ABILITY TO DETECT POLLUTION BUT ALSO EMPOWERED US TO VISUALLY DOCUMENT POLLUTION INCIDENTS AND HOLD THOSE RESPONSIBLE ACCOUNTABLE. ONE OF THE SIGNIFICANT MILESTONES OF 2023 WAS THE CONFIRMATION OF THE DEVASTATING IMPACT OF THE PORTMIAMI PHASE III DREDGING PROJECT ON OUR CORAL REEFS BY THE FEDERAL GOVERNMENT. OUR RESEARCH AND ADVOCACY, DATING BACK TO 2014, LAID THE GROUNDWORK FOR THIS CRUCIAL REVELATION, SHEDDING LIGHT ON THE TRAGIC LOSS OF LIKELY MILLIONS OF CORALS AND THE DEGRADATION OF 278 ACRES OF REEF HABITAT. THIS REPORT NOT ONLY UNDERSCORES THE URGENCY OF OUR MISSION BUT VALIDATES OUR RESEARCH AND CATALYZES EFFORTS FOR THE MITIGATION AND PROTECTION OF OUR REEFS. WE ARE COMMITTED TO CONTINUING TO WORK TOGETHER TO FIND RESILIENT SOLUTIONS THAT ARE GROUNDED IN SCIENCE, ROOTED IN NATURE, AND DRIVEN BY COMMUNITY. POLICY AND LEGAL ADVOCACY BACKED BY SCIENCE, WE DEVELOP POLICY GOALS TO PROTECT OUR ENVIRONMENT AND COMMUNITY. FROM GRASSROOTS TO GRASSTOPS, WE ENGAGE THE PUBLIC, GARNER THEIR SUPPORT, AND WORK WITH ELECTED OFFICIALS AND GOVERNMENT TO INSTILL LEGISLATION THAT ENSURES CLEAN WATER AND CLIMATE RESILIENCY. WHEN NECESSARY, WE PURSUE LITIGATION AGAINST VIOLATIONS OF KEY ENVIRONMENTAL LAWS, LIKE THE CLEAN WATER ACT AND THE ENDANGERED SPECIES ACT. ONE OF OUR "WATER WINS" FROM 2023 INCLUDES THE NUCLEAR REGULATORY COMMISSION (NRC) PROPOSING UPDATES TO ITS RULES FOR EVALUATING ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACTS AT ALL NUCLEAR POWER PLANTS - AN OUTCOME DIRECTLY INFLUENCED BY OUR EFFORTS AT SOUTH FLORIDA'S TURKEY POINT NUCLEAR POWER PLANT. THESE CHANGES NOW REQUIRE NRC STAFF TO WEIGH CLIMATE CHANGE AND SEA LEVEL RISE WHEN THEY RE-LICENSE PLANTS FOR LONGER OPERATING TERMS. ANOTHER "WATER WIN" FROM 2023 WAS THE RESULT OF MIAMI WATERKEEPER RAISING CONCERNS ABOUT THE POTENTIAL FOR INCREASED CANCER RISK FROM EATING FLORIDA SEAFOOD AND DISPROPORTIONATELY HARMING TRIBAL COMMUNITIES AND THE FISHING COMMUNITIES WHO DEPEND ON IT. IN 2023, THE EPA AGREED WITH US, FINDING FLORIDA'S STANDARDS OUTDATED. THEY ORDERED STRICTER RULES TO PROTECT OUR WATERS, AND ON DECEMBER 1ST, THE EPA PROPOSED NEW RULES BASED ON THE LATEST SCIENCE. IF APPROVED, THE PROPOSED RULES WILL PROTECT THE HEALTH OF FLORIDIANS AND IMPROVE THE QUALITY OF THE WATER IN WHICH WE FISH AND RECREATE. EDUCATION AND OUTREACH OUR WORK ALWAYS COMES BACK TO THE COMMUNITY. WE MAKE IT EASY FOR THE PUBLIC TO BE HEARD THROUGH OUR ADVOCACY PLATFORM; WE PROVIDE TRAINING ON HOW TO OBSERVE AND REPORT POLLUTION THROUGH OUR 1,000 EYES ON THE WATER PROGRAM; WE DEVELOP HIGH SCHOOLERS INTO ENVIRONMENTAL LEADERS THROUGH OUR JUNIOR AMBASSADOR PROGRAM; WE EDUCATE YOUTH THROUGH FLOATING CLASSROOMS AND YOUTH ENVIRONMENTAL ACTION (YEA) DAYS; AND WE ENGAGE VOLUNTEERS IN HABITAT RESTORATIONS, KAYAK CLEANUPS, AND SHORE-BASED MARINE DEBRIS CLEANUPS. IN 2023, WE SAW RECORD NUMBERS FOR OUR PROGRAMS THE INCREASING ENTHUSIASM FROM YOUNG LEADERS LOOKING TO GET INVOLVED WITH OUR ORGANIZATION CONTINUES TO GROW, WITH OVER 90 APPLICANTS FOR THE JUNIOR AMBASSADOR PROGRAM - SO WE ACCEPTED OUR LARGEST COHORT EVER WITH 35 JUNIOR AMBASSADORS AND SEVEN (7) JA LEADERSHIP BOARD MEMBERS FOR THE 2023-2024 ACADEMIC YEAR. ADDITIONALLY, THROUGH OUR COMMUNITY CLEANUP INITIATIVES, WE REMOVED A STAGGERING 6,996 POUNDS OF MARINE DEBRIS FROM BEACHES, BAYS, RIVERS, AND COASTAL AREAS THIS IS AN IMPRESSIVE INCREASE FROM THE 2,362 POUNDS OF TRASH REMOVED THE PREVIOUS YEAR. BY MOBILIZING AND EDUCATING RESIDENTS, OUR CLEANUP EFFORTS HAVE EXPANDED IN BOTH SCOPE AND IMPACT. FINALLY, WE ARE PIONEERING INNOVATIVE DESIGNS TO COMBAT HEAT, IMPROVE WATER QUALITY, AND MITIGATE FLOODING WHILE IMPROVING HABITAT - SHOWCASING THE POWER OF COLLECTIVE ACTION IN BUILDING A MORE SUSTAINABLE AND RESILIENT FUTURE FOR OUR COMMUNITIES AND ECOSYSTEMS. THIS WORK FOCUSES ON ONGOING COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT AND IS AIDED BY AN INTERACTIVE ONLINE COMMUNITY INPUT TOOL THAT SOLICITS NOMINATIONS OF SITES FOR REVITALIZATION WITH GREEN INFRASTRUCTURE IN UNDERUTILIZED PUBLIC SPACES, SUCH AS PARKING LOTS, SWALES, AND CANAL BANKS. ORGANIZATIONAL RECOGNITION ELEVATE PRIZE IN 2023, EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR & WATERKEEPER, RACHEL SILVERSTEIN, PH.D., WAS ANNOUNCED AS A WINNER OF THE ELEVATE PRIZE. BEYOND THE UNRESTRICTED FUNDING PROVIDED TO MIAMI WATERKEEPER BY THE ELEVATE PRIZE FOUNDATION, RACHEL JOINED AN INCREDIBLE COHORT OF 12 NONPROFIT LEADERS WHO HAVE BEEN RECEIVING TAILORED RESOURCES FROM THE ELEVATE TEAM FOR TWO YEARS. THE ELEVATE PRIZE IS AN ANNUAL AWARD AND SIGNATURE PROGRAM OF THE ELEVATE PRIZE FOUNDATION THAT RECOGNIZES LEADERS FROM ACROSS THE GLOBE FOR THEIR IMPACT -- PROVIDING FUNDING, GREATER VISIBILITY, AND TAILORED RESOURCES TO HELP EXPAND AND AMPLIFY THEIR WORK. THE ELEVATE PRIZE FOUNDATION WORKS TO MAKE GOOD FAMOUS BECAUSE, "WHEN WE AMPLIFY THE VISIBILITY OF THOSE CREATING SOCIAL IMPACT, EVERYONE CAN BE INSPIRED TO BE PROBLEM-SOLVERS AND AGENTS FOR CHANGE." THE ELEVATE PRIZE FOUNDATION WANTS TO INCREASE THE VISIBILITY AND AWARENESS OF SOCIAL ENTREPRENEURS WHO ARE LITERALLY CHANGING THE WORLD. THE TEAM IS CREATING A PLATFORM WHERE AUDIENCES CAN FIND, FOLLOW, AND CELEBRATE THEM, JUST AS THEY CAN FOR ATHLETES, ACTORS, AND REALITY TV STARS. WE ARE HONORED TO HAVE RACHEL BE NAMED AN ELEVATE PRIZE WINNER AND INCLUDED IN THE COHORT OF INCREDIBLE CHANGE-MAKERS FROM ACROSS THE GLOBE. KNIGHT FOUNDATION ALSO IN 2023, MIAMI WATERKEEPER LAUNCHED A NEW ERA OF GROWTH AND IMPACT, ACCELERATED BY A 5 MILLION INVESTMENT OVER SIX YEARS FROM THE JOHN S. AND JAMES L. KNIGHT FOUNDATION. THE INVESTMENT WILL ALLOW US TO EXPAND OUR ABILITY TO INFORM AND ENGAGE RESIDENTS IN SOUTH FLORIDA IN DEVELOPING CLIMATE RESILIENCY SOLUTIONS BY LEVERAGING CUTTING-EDGE TECHNOLOGIES AND DIGITAL TOOLS. RECOGNIZING MIAMI'S POTENTIAL TO SERVE AS AN INCUBATOR FOR GLOBAL CLIMATE SOLUTIONS, WE AIM TO IMPLEMENT AND SCALE EFFECTIVE SOLUTIONS. WE PIONEER INNOVATIVE, MULTIDISCIPLINARY APPROACHES THAT CAN BE REPLICATED IN OTHER REGIONS GRAPPLING WITH SIMILAR CLIMATE-RELATED ISSUES, INSPIRING AND GUIDING COMMUNITIES WORLDWIDE TOWARD SUSTAINABLE AND RESILIENT FUTURES.

AMY CLEMENT KENNY BROAD DIRECTOR TRUSTEE HUSBAND AND WIFE MARIA SCHWEDEL DAVID SCHWEDEL TRUSTEE TRUSTEE HUSBAND AND WIFE

THE PREPARED FORM 990 IS REVIEWED AND APPROVED BY THE GOVERNING BODY BEFORE THE RETURN IS FILED WITH THE IRS.

THE GOVERNING BODY REVIEWS AND SIGNS A CONFLICT-OF-INTEREST POLICY ON AN ANNUAL BASIS.

THE BOARD MEETS ANNUALLY TO DISCUSS AND DETERMINE CEO COMPENSATION. THEY CONSIDER COMPENSATION OF OTHER SIMILARLY-SIZED ORGANIZATIONS LOCALLY AND OTHER WATERKEEPER ORGANIZATION NATIONALLY.

THE BOARD AND CEO MEET TO DISCUSS AND DETERMINE OFFICER COMPENSATION. THEY CONSIDER COMPENSATION OF OTHER SIMILARLY-SIZED ORGANIZATIONS LOCALLY AND OTHER WATERKEEPER ORGANIZATION NATIONALLY.

mainOrgs.missionStatement: SEE SCHEDULE O - 01. GENERAL EXPLANATION ATTACHMENT
mainOrgs.program2Desc: IN 2024, THE SECOND LARGEST PROGRAM AREA WAS COMMUNITY OUTREACH AND STEWARDSHIP PROJECTS, WHICH INCLUDES OUR TRASH CLEANUPS AND 14 IN-STREAM TRAXH TRAPS. WE MOBILIZED 2,123 VOLUNTEERS TO REMOVE 54 TONS OF TRASH AND RECYCLABLES FROM THE WATERSHED, AND RECYCLED AN ADDITIONAL 3,644 LBS. ADDITIONALLY, WE LOGGED 186 YOUTH VOLUTEERS IN THE WATER WARRIORS PROGRAMS. WE ALSO EDUCATED 9,165 STUDENTS AND TEACHERS ONBOARD OUR FLOATING CLASSROOMS PROGRAM. WE PROVIDED 4,182 FLOATING CLASSROOM SCHOLARSHIPS TO STUDENTS FROM TITLE 1 SCHOOLS. 19 INTERNS AND 2 FELLOWS GAINED CAREER EXPERIENCE IN WATER SCIENCE AND COMMUNITY OUTREACH. WE ADDED 1,540 NEW MEMBERS AND HELD 85 COMMUNITY OUTREACH EVENTS TO SHARE OUR MISSION.
mainOrgs.program3Desc: IN 2024, THE THIRD LARGEST PROGRAM AREA WAS OUR ADVOCACY WORK. WE WROTE AND DELIVERED PUBLIC COMMENT LETTERS AND TESTIMONIES TO AGENCIES AND THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY TO PROTECT ENVIRONMENTAL RESOURCES. WE HELD TOWN HALL MEETINGS TO EDUCATE THE PUBLIC.

THE STAFF WORKS WITH THE FINANCE COMMITTEE OF THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS TO PRESENT THE FORM 990 TO THE FULL BOARD OF DIRECTORS FOR REVIEW AND APPROVAL.

CRK IS COMMITTED TO AVOIDING ACTUAL AND PERCEIVED CONFLICTS OF INTEREST. OUR POLICY IS TO RESPOND TO AND RESOLVE ANY ACTUAL OR PERCEIVED CONFLICTS OF INTEREST THAT MAY ARISE. TO THAT END, ANY PERSON ACTING ON BEHALF OF CRK IS EXPECTED TO SERVE THE BEST INTERESTS OF THE ORGANIZATION AND SHALL IMMEDIATELY DISCLOSE POTENTIAL CONFLICTS OF INTERESTS TO THE AUDIT COMMITTEE. THE ORGANIZATION REQUIRES THAT THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS, OFFICERS AND THE EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR DISCLOSE ANY POTENTIAL CONFLICTS OF INTEREST.

IT IS THE POLICY OF CRK TO ESTABLISH RATES OF PAY THAT ARE COMPETITIVE IN THE NONPROFIT COMMUNITY PARTICULARLY AS THEY ARE REFLECTED FOR ORGANIZATIONS OF COMPARABLE SIZE, MISSION AND LOCATION. SALARY INCREASES ARE CONSIDERED ANNUALLY, THE ORGANIZATION DOES NOT GRANT AUTOMATIC SALARY INCREASES. INCREASES ARE DETERMINED BASED ON BUDGET CONSIDERATIONS AND IN CONJUNCTION WITH A PERFORMANCE REVIEW. THE CONSIDERATION OF SALARY INCREASES INCLUDES THE EMPLOYEE?S FULFILLMENT OF CURRENT JOB RESPONSIBILITIES AND MAINTAINING AN ATTITUDE OF PROFESSIONALISM.

IT IS THE POLICY OF CRK TO ESTABLISH RATES OF PAY THAT ARE COMPETITIVE IN THE NONPROFIT COMMUNITY PARTICULARLY AS THEY ARE REFLECTED FOR ORGANIZATIONS OF COMPARABLE SIZE, MISSION AND LOCATION. SALARY INCREASES ARE CONSIDERED ANNUALLY, THE ORGANIZATION DOES NOT GRANT AUTOMATIC SALARY INCREASES. INCREASES ARE DETERMINED BASED ON BUDGET CONSIDERATIONS AND IN CONJUNCTION WITH A PERFORMANCE REVIEW. THE CONSIDERATION OF SALARY INCREASES INCLUDES THE EMPLOYEE?S FULFILLMENT OF CURRENT JOB RESPONSIBILITIES AND MAINTAINING AN ATTITUDE OF PROFESSIONALISM.

THE ORGANIZATION PUBLISHES AN ANNUAL REPORT, AUDIT, FINANCIAL STATEMENTS AND BY THE NUMBERS EACH YEAR AND MAKES THE GOVERNING DOCUMENTS, CONFLICT OF INTEREST POLICY AVAILABLE TO THE PUBLIC.

OUR MISSION IS TO EDUCATE, ADVOCATE AND SECURE THE PROTECTION AND STEWARDSHIP OF THE CHATTAHOOCHEE RIVER, INCLUDING ITS LAKES, TRIBUTARIES AND WATERSHED, IN ORDER TO RESTORE AND CONSERVE THEIR ECOLOGICAL HEALTH FOR THE PEOPLE AND WILDLIFE THAT DEPEND ON THE RIVER SYSTEM AND IN RECOGNITION OF THE IMPORTANT ECOSYSTEM FUNCTIONS PROVIDED THROUGHOUT THE REGION AND PLANET.

mainOrgs.name: Guy Harvey Ocean Fund Inc
mainOrgs.missionSummary: The Guy Harvey Foundation collaborates on marine conservation and education through research and stakeholder engagement.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Scientific research and educational programs focused on ocean conservation.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Core focus on marine conservation and enhancement of fish populations.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Engages in related programs of scientific research and education for marine environments.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: The Guy Harvey Foundation has established itself over 5 years with a revenue of $2.9M and a high current ratio of 19.32, but shows a concerning revenue trend decline of 36.7%. The organization excels in marine conservation.
mainOrgs.softFlags: Months unrestricted below threshold; Revenue concentrated in Contributions (87%); Program expense ratio below tier threshold (70%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: THE GUY HARVEY FOUNDATION (GHF) COLLABORATES WITH LOCAL AND INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATIONS TO CONDUCT SCIENTIFIC RESEARCH AND HOST EDUCATIONAL PROGRAMS AIMED AT BETTER UNDERSTANDING AND CONSERVING THE OCEAN ENVIRONMENT. (CONTINUED ON SCHEDULE O) THE GHF ALSO FUNDS INSPIRED SCIENTIFIC RESEARCH AND INNOVATIVE EDUCATIONAL PROGRAMS DEVELOPED BY UNIVERSITIES, COLLEGES, INSTITUTES, AND NONPROFIT ORGANIZATIONS THAT PROMISE TO BRING NEW IDEAS AND INITIATIVES TO MARINE CONSERVATION AND THAT ENCOURAGE CONSERVATIONS AND BEST MANAGEMENT PRACTICE FOR SUSTAINABLE MARINE ENVIRONMENT.
mainOrgs.program2Desc: SCIENCE AND RESEARCH: THE GUY HARVEY FOUNDATION (GHF) RESEARCH PROGRAMS ARE DEDICATED TO EXPANDING THE SCIENTIFIC KNOWLEDGE NECESSARY FOR EFFECTIVE CONSERVATION OF FISH POPULATIONS AND MAINTENANCE OF FISH BIODIVERSITY. THE HIGH-QUALITY SCIENTIFIC INFORMATION PROVIDED BY GHF IS CRITICAL TO THE UNIVERSALLY RECOGNIZED URGENT NEED TO DEVELOP AND IMPLEMENT EFFECTIVE MEASURES FOR THE CONSERVATION AND ENHANCEMENT OF FISH RESOURCES WORLDWIDE. RESEARCH PROGRAMS INCLUDE: (1) SATELLITE TAGGING SHORTFIN MAKO SHARKS, (2) SATELLITE AND CONVENTIONAL TAGGING DOLPHINFISH IN THE NW ATLANTIC OCEAN, CARIBBEAN SEA, AND EASTERN TROPICAL PACIFIC, (3) TAGGING AND TRACKING BILLFISH IN THE EASTERN TROPICAL PACIFIC OCEAN, AND (4) SATELLITE TAGGING OF WHALE SHARKS OFF THE YUCATAN PENINSULA. (CONTINUED ON SCHEDULE O)1. SATELLITE TAGGING SHORTFIN MAKO SHARKS IN THE NW ATLANTIC OCEAN AND WESTERN CARIBBEAN / GULF OF MEXICO. THE GUY HARVEY FOUNDATION AND GUY HARVEY RESEARCH INSTITUTE (GHRI) AT NOVA SOUTHEASTERN UNIVERSITY DEPLOYED FIVE SATELLITE TRACKING TAGS (SPOT TAGS) ON SHORTFIN MAKO SHARKS IN THE NW ATLANTIC OCEAN AND WESTERN CARIBBEAN / GULF OF MEXICO AS PART OF A MULTIYEAR STUDY TO UNDERSTAND THE MIGRATION PATTERNS AND FISHING MORTALITY FOR THIS SPECIES. THIS RESEARCH PROJECT PROVIDED THE FIRST FISHERIES-INDEPENDENT FISHING MORTALITY RATE FOR THIS ECONOMICALLY AND ECOLOGICALLY IMPORTANT SPECIES, WHICH WAS 10 TIMES HIGHER THAN PREVIOUSLY THOUGHT. THIS DISCOVERY LED THE INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATION THAT MANAGES MAKO SHARKS TO DOWNGRADE THEIR STATUS TO OVERFISHED WITH OVERFISHING OCCURRING. THIS CHANGE OF STATUS, IN TURN, PROMPTED THE U.S. GOVERNMENT (NOAA) TO INCREASE PROTECTIONS FOR MAKO SHARKS IN FEDERAL WATERS, INCLUDING MANDATING THE RELEASE OF LIVE MAKO SHARKS IN THE COMMERCIAL FISHERY AND AN INCREASED MINIMUM SIZE IN THE RECREATIONAL FISHERY.2. SATELLITE AND CONVENTIONAL TAGGING DOLPHINFISH IN THE NW ATLANTIC OCEAN, CARIBBEAN SEA, AND EASTERN TROPICAL PACIFIC. THE FOUNDATION PARTNERED WITH THE DOLPHINFISH RESEARCH PROGRAM TO DEPLOY POP-UP SATELLITE TRACKING TAGS (PSAT TAGS) AND GEOLOCATING TAGS ON ATLANTIC AND PACIFIC DOLPHINFISH AND TO ENGAGE RECREATIONAL ANGLERS IN A TAG AND RECAPTURE CITIZEN SCIENCE STUDY. THIS HIGHLY REPRODUCTIVE FISH HAS LONG BEEN THOUGHT TO BE IMMUNE TO OVERFISHING BUT ANECDOTAL EVIDENCE IS SHOWING A DECLINE IN THE NUMBERS AND SIZE OF DOLPHINFISH AROUND THE WORLD AND SPECIFICALLY IN THE SOUTHEAST UNITED STATES. THE SATELLITE TAGGING STUDY IS HELPING RESEARCHERS BETTER UNDERSTAND THE SEASON MIGRATIONS OF THESE FISH WHILE THE TAG AND RECAPTURE STUDY IS PROVIDING INVALUABLE DATA ABOUT THE INCREDIBLY FAST GROWTH RATE OF THESE FISH AND HOW OFTEN THEY ARE CAUGHT. THESE DATA ARE FUNDAMENTAL TO CONSERVING THIS COMMERCIALLY AND RECREATIONALLY IMPORTANT SPECIES.3. TAGGING AND TRACKING BILLFISH IN THE EASTERN TROPICAL PACIFIC OCEAN. THE FOUNDATION PARTNERED WITH NOVA SOUTHEASTERN UNIVERSITY AND TROPIC STAR LODGE IN PANAMA ON A COMPREHENSIVE STUDY OF THE BILLFISH AND OTHER LARGE VERTEBRATES THAT INHABIT THE EASTERN TROPICAL PACIFIC OCEAN. THIS IS THE FOURTH YEAR OF A FIVE-YEAR STUDY TO DEPLOY SATELLITE POP-UP TAGS AND ACCELEROMETER TAGS ON BLUE MARLIN, BLACK MARLIN, AND SAILFISH TO BETTER UNDERSTAND THEIR MIGRATION PATTERNS AND HOW THESE SPECIES RECOVER FROM THE CATCH AND RELEASE PROCESS. THERE IS A SIGNIFICANT AMOUNT OF COMMERCIAL FISHING IN THIS REGION, SO FISHERIES MANAGERS NEED TO KNOW WHERE THESE BILLFISH ARE TRAVELLING. ADDITIONALLY, THE RECREATIONAL FISHERY IS VERY VALUABLE TO THE REGION, SO IT IS IMPORTANT TO UNDERSTAND THE EFFECTS THAT THE CATCH AND RELEASE PROCESS HAS ON THESE ANIMALS. DATA COLLECTED FROM THE DEPLOYMENT OF BAITED REMOTE UNDERWATER VIDEO (BRUV) CAMERAS IS IN THE PROCESS OF BEING SCIENTIFICALLY PUBLISHED.4. SATELLITE TAGGING OF WHALE SHARKS OFF THE YUCATAN PENINSULA. THE FOUNDATION PARTNERED WITH CH'OOJ AJAUIL AND THE GUY HARVEY RESEARCH INSTITUTE TO DEPLOY SMART POSITION AND TEMPERATURE (SPOT) TRANSMITTING TAGS ON WHALE SHARKS IN A LONG-TERM MOVEMENT ECOLOGY STUDY. WHALE SHARKS ARE A COSMOPOLITAN SPECIES THAT AGGREGATE ANNUALLY IN LARGE NUMBERS OFF MEXICO ATTRACTING NATURALISTS, SCIENTISTS, AND TOURISTS TO THE SITE. WHALE SHARKS ARE THREATENED BY POLLUTION, BOAT STRIKES AND THE SHARK FIN TRADE. THEIR ANNUAL MIGRATION SUPPORTS AN IMPORTANT SEASONAL ECO-TOURISM ATTRACTION AND ALLOWS ABILITY TO STUDY MULTIPLE SHARKS OVER MULTI YEAR PERIODS TO BETTER UNDERSTAND THEIR MIGRATORY BEHAVIOR OUTSIDE OF THE ANNUAL AGGREGATION PERIOD. THEIR TRACKS ARE MADE PUBLICLY AVAILABLE TO SUPPORT EDUCATIONAL AWARENESS OF THIS IMPORTANT SPECIES AND OVERALL AID IN THEIR CONSERVATION.
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
THE GUY HARVEY FOUNDATION SUPPORTS THE CENTER FOR SPORTFISHING POLICY ('CENTER'). THE CENTER IS THE NATION'S LEADING ADVOCATE FOR SALTWATER RECREATIONAL ANGLERS. THE CENTER ORGANIZES, FOCUSES, AND ENGAGES RECREATIONAL FISHING STAKEHOLDERS TO SHAPE FEDERAL MARINE FISHERIES MANAGEMENT POLICIES. THE FOUNDATION ALSO ENGAGED AN ADVOCACY FIRM TO DEVELOP RELATIONSHIPS WITH STAKEHOLDERS, ADVOCATE FOR STATE APPROPRIATIONS, AND ADVANCE THE FOUNDATION'S EDUCATION INITIATIVES.

THE GUY HARVEY FOUNDATION PARTNERS WITH LEADING ORGANIZATIONS TO CONDUCT CUTTING-EDGE SCIENTIFIC RESEARCH AIMED AT BETTER UNDERSTANDING THE MARINE ENVIRONMENT AND PROVIDING THE INFORMATION NECESSARY TO HELP ENSURE PROPER FISHERIES MANAGEMENT. THE FOUNDATION'S EDUCATIONAL AND SCIENTIFIC RESEARCH PROGRAMS CONDUCTED IN FOREIGN REGIONS INCLUDE SATELLITE TAGGING, DOLPHINFISH RESEARCH, SHARK GENOME RESEARCH, GRAND CAYMAN STINGRAY SURVEYS, EASTERN TROPICAL PACIFIC SEASCAPE, AND CORAL REEF RESTORATION. SEE FORM 990, PART III, LINE 4B.

SCHEDULE I, PART II: THE FOUNDATION SUPPORTS THE UNIVERSITY OF FLORIDA FOUNDATION VIA A PARTNERSHIP WITH FLORIDA SEA GRANT. FLORIDA SEA GRANT IS A UNIVERSITY-BASED PROGRAM THAT SUPPORTS RESEARCH, EDUCATION AND EXTENSION TO CONSERVE COASTAL RESOURCES FOR RESIDENTS AND VISITORS AND ENHANCE ECONOMIC OPPORTUNITIES FOR THE PEOPLE OF FLORIDA. THE FSG/GUY HARVEY FELLOWSHIP IS OPEN TO ANY GRADUATE STUDENT WHO IS ENROLLED FULL-TIME AT ONE OF FLORIDA'S INSTITUTIONS OF HIGHER EDUCATION AND IS CONDUCTING RESEARCH ON THE BIOLOGY, ECOLOGY, HABITAT OR MANAGEMENT OF SUSTAINABLE MARINE FISHERIES. FELLOWS WILL BE EXPECTED TO SHARE THEIR PROGRESS, INCLUDING PARTICIPATING AT PUBLIC AND SCIENTIFIC EVENTS TO IMPROVE SCIENCE COMMUNICATION SKILLS WHEN AVAILABLE. THE FOUNDATION SUPPORTS THE CENTER FOR SPORTFISHING POLICY. THE CENTER FOR SPORTFISHING POLICY IS THE NATION'S LEADING ADVOCATE FOR SALTWATER RECREATIONAL ANGLERS. THE CENTER ORGANIZES, FOCUSES, AND ENGAGES RECREATIONAL FISHING STAKEHOLDERS TO SHAPE FEDERAL MARINE FISHERIES MANAGEMENT POLICIES. THE GUY HARVEY FOUNDATION COLLABORATED WITH THE MARINE TECHNOLOGY SOCIETY (MTS) TO EXPAND THE REACH AND IMPACT OF ITS OCEAN EXCHANGE PROGRAM, SPONSORING THE GUY HARVEY FOUNDATION COLLEGIATE AWARDS. OCEAN EXCHANGE IS A GLOBAL ECOSYSTEM WHOSE MISSION IS TO ACCELERATE THE ADOPTION OF INNOVATIVE SOLUTIONS FOR HEALTHY OCEANS AND THE SUSTAINABLE BLUE ECONOMY BY PROVIDING MONETARY AWARDS, CONNECTING INNOVATORS WITH EXPERTS, AND PROMOTING BREAKTHROUGH TECHNOLOGIES THROUGH A GLOBAL ECOSYSTEM. EACH YEAR, OCEAN EXCHANGE HOSTS AN EVENT TO RECOGNIZE AND HELP ADVANCE INNOVATION IN TECHNOLOGIES AIMED AT THE HEALTH OF OUR OCEANS AND THE BLUE ECONOMY. AT THE EVENT, WINNERS OF MONETARY AWARDS ARE CHOSEN BASED ON THE IMPACT, LEVEL OF INNOVATION, AND THE ABILITY TO EXECUTE THE PRESENTED SOLUTION. THE GUY HARVEY FOUNDATION COLLEGIATE AWARDS ARE PRESENTED TO THE SOLUTIONS THAT ADVANCE UNDERSTANDING OF THE OCEAN AND HELP MINIMIZE OUR IMPACT ON THESE RESOURCES, EVEN WHILE USING THEM FOR HUMAN BENEFIT, RESULTING IN MORE RESILIENT BODIES OF WATER INCLUDING HEALTHY MARINE LIFE AND COASTLINES. SCHEDULE I, PART III: IN 2024, THE FOUNDATION LAUNCHED ITS BACK-TO-SCHOOL CAMPAIGN TO EMPOWER EDUCATORS WITH THE TOOLS THEY NEED TO TEACH MARINE SCIENCE AND INSPIRE THE NEXT GENERATION OF OCEAN STEWARDS. THE FOUNDATION PROVIDED ESSENTIAL RESOURCES, CURRICULUM, AND HANDS-ON ACTIVITIES THAT BRING THE WONDERS OF THE OCEAN INTO CLASSROOMS. BY HELPING TEACHERS ACCESS THESE MATERIALS, THE FOUNDATION NOT ONLY ENHANCED STUDENTS' UNDERSTANDING OF MARINE ECOSYSTEMS BUT ALSO FOSTERED A LIFELONG PASSION FOR OCEAN CONSERVATION. SCHEDULE I, PART II & III: GRANTEES MUST SUBMIT REGULAR REPORTS DETAILING PROGRAMMATIC PROGRESS AND BUDGETARY COMPLIANCE. GHF STAFF REVIEW THESE REPORTS AND COMMUNICATE CLOSELY WITH THE GRANTEE. AS THE GRANT'S TERM COMES TO AN END, GRANTEES OFTEN MUST PROVIDE PROGRAMMATIC MATERIALS SUCH AS A RESEARCH PAPER AS WELL AS FINANCIAL DOCUMENTS LIKE RECEIPTS FROM A SCHOOL FIELD TRIP TO AN AQUARIUM. GHF STAFF COLLECT AND ANALYZE ALL MATERIALS TO ENSURE COMPLIANCE.

PART IV, LINE (1), COLUMN (D): ANNUAL LICENSING - THE FOUNDATION ENTERED INTO A SERVICE MARK LICENSE AGREEMENT EFFECTIVE JANUARY 1, 2019, WHICH ALLOWS THE FOUNDATION TO USE THE LICENSED INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY OF GUY HARVEY, INC. FOR THE PURPOSE OF PROMOTING PUBLIC AWARENESS OF MARINE AND OCEAN WILDLIFE CONSERVATION. THE LICENSED PROPERTY HAS BEEN CREATED, PROMOTED, COMMERCIALIZED, AND USED BY GUY HARVEY, INC., IN THE UNITED STATES AND AROUND THE WORLD, IN WAYS THAT ATTACH MATERIAL VALUE TO THE PROPERTY. UNDER THE TERMS OF THE AGREEMENT, ROYALTY FEES OF $300,000 ARE DUE ANNUALLY.

BOARD MEMBERS DR. GUY HARVEY AND JESSICA HARVEY HAVE A FAMILY RELATIONSHIP.

FORM 990 IS REVIEWED IN DETAIL WITH MANAGEMENT PRIOR TO FILING. THE FORM IS THEN MADE AVAILABLE TO THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS.

ALL MEMBERS OF THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS ARE REQUIRED TO ANNUALLY ACKNOWLEDGE THEIR UNDERSTANDING OF, AND AGREEMENT TO, GUY HARVEY FOUNDATION'S CONFLICT OF INTEREST POLICY. THIS CONFLICT-OF-INTEREST POLICY IS DESIGNED TO HELP DIRECTORS, OFFICERS, AND EMPLOYEES OF THE GUY HARVEY FOUNDATION IDENTIFY SITUATIONS THAT PRESENT POTENTIAL CONFLICTS OF INTEREST AND TO PROVIDE GUY HARVEY FOUNDATION A PROCEDURE THAT, IF OBSERVED, WILL ALLOW A TRANSACTION TO BE TREATED AS VALID AND BINDING EVEN THOUGH A DIRECTOR, OFFICER, OR EMPLOYEE HAS OR MAY HAVE A CONFLICT OF INTEREST WITH RESPECT TO THE TRANSACTION. IN THE EVENT THERE IS AN INCONSISTENCY BETWEEN THE REQUIREMENTS AND PROCEDURES PRESCRIBED BY THE POLICY AND THOSE IN FEDERAL OR STATE LAW, THE LAW SHALL CONTROL. AS SET FORTH IN THE BYLAWS, DIRECTORS SHALL BE RESPONSIBLE FOR ENSURING THAT NO INTERESTED PERSON, AS DEFINED IN THE CONFLICT-OF-INTEREST POLICY, OR ANY FAMILY MEMBER OF THE INTERESTED PERSON, WHO HAS A CONFLICT OF INTEREST WITH REGARDS TO ANY BUSINESS DEALINGS OR CONTRACTS OF GUY HARVEY FOUNDATION EXERCISES INFLUENCE AND/OR PARTICIPATES IN DECISIONS RELATED TO THOSE BUSINESS DEALINGS OR CONTRACTS. FURTHER, ANY FINANCIAL ARRANGEMENTS, BUSINESS DEALINGS AND CONTRACTS ENTERED BY GUY HARVEY FOUNDATION WILL BE ON "FAIR MARKET" TERMS AS DETERMINED BY INDUSTRY STANDARDS AND LOCAL CONVENTION. THE GOVERNING BOARD OF DIRECTORS HAS IDENTIFIED A QUALIFIED CERTIFIED PUBLIC ACCOUNTANT (CPA) TO ENSURE FINANCIAL ACCURACY AND COMPLIANCE.

A COMMITTEE OF THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS REVIEWS THE COMPENSATION OF THE CEO. THE COMMITTEE USES ITS OWN OBSERVATIONS OF THE CEO AND RELIES ON COMMENTS AND FEEDBACK FROM STAFF IN ORDER TO EVALUATE OVERALL PERFORMANCE. THE COMMITTEE ALSO REVIEWS COMPARABLE DATA OF OTHER SIMILAR ORGANIZATIONS TO ENSURE THE COMPENSATION IS WITHIN REASONABLE RANGE. THE COMMITTEE THEN MAKES A RECOMMENDATION TO THE FULL BOARD OF DIRECTORS WHO THEN VOTE ON THE COMPENSATION OF THE CEO.

FINANCIAL STATEMENTS, GOVERNING DOCUMENTS, AND CONFLICT OF INTEREST POLICIES ARE NOT REQUIRED DISCLOSURES PURSUANT TO INTERNAL REVENUE CODE IRC SEC. 6104. THESE DOCUMENTS ARE NOT MADE AVAILABLE TO THE PUBLIC AT THIS TIME.

mainOrgs.name: Coastal Quest
mainOrgs.missionSummary: Accelerate solutions for resilient coastal communities, nature, and climate.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Protected areas, coastal communities, and clean coastal water initiatives.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Focus on climate resilience with significant interactions with water quality and ecosystem conservation.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Three related programs addressing coastal resilience, protection, and water quality.
mainOrgs.notableSignals: Mature organization with strong revenue growth and low program expense ratio.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: Coastal Quest has been active for over 26 years, with $3.5M in revenue and a strong current ratio of 5.41, indicating solid financial health. Their focus on building resilient coastal communities aligns with their efforts in water conservation and climate action.
mainOrgs.websiteRecentActivity: Upcoming California Wildfire Conference and California Plastic Crisis Conference Series occur in October 2023.
mainOrgs.softFlags: Months unrestricted below threshold; Revenue concentrated in Contributions (95%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: Our Mission is to accelerate solutions, building resilient coastal communities for people, nature, and the climate.
mainOrgs.program2Desc: Resilient Protected Areas and Parks. To address the global climate crisis, nations are committing to protect, restore, and reduce impacts on natural areas. In the United States, an executive order was recently signed promising to protect 30 percent of U.S. land and 30 percent of oceans by 2030. In California, a similar executive order was signed. This 30 by 30 commitment galvanized governments to act quickly to develop nature-based solutions to address climate change that can sequester carbon, reduce pollution, restore wildlife habitat, and benefit communities. There is an opportunity to work on public-private partnership to strengthen management, diversity, equity, and inclusion in coastal protected areas and parks. Simultaneously, this will help states and nations reach 30 by 30 targets, prepare protected areas and parks for climate change, and ensure increased access by priority communities.
mainOrgs.program3Desc: Resilient Coastal Communities and Governments. Limited funding sources exist worldwide to help communities build capacity to respond to public health and on-the-ground impacts of climate change. Many existing climate resilience-focused funding sources lack flexibility, focus support on infrastructure and capital projects, and often do not allow for the allocation of funds to early phases of planning. Developing and implementing solutions will require joint action and collaboration across jurisdictions, agencies, and partners. Highlighting a need to increase funding for coastal resilience inclusive, science-based planning and piloting. There is an opportunity to pilot and implement public- private partnerships and blended funding mechanisms to support coastal resilience planning, projects at all scales, from local to national.

We monitor grant funds by understanding the terms, using a dedicated account, tracking expenditures, reconciling budgets regularly, implementing internal controls, and maintaining detailed records.

Coastal Quest leases office space from single member LLC of Board Member, Executive Director. The LLC provides a guarantee for a $350,000 line of credit from a bank to Coastal Quest.

OTHER PROGRAM SERVICES 4: Clean Coastal Water. Coastal water quality agencies struggle to meet clean water standards, impacting the health of coastal waters and wellbeing of residents and visitors alike. National, state, and local water quality agencies have limited ability to enforce current rules and laws and capacity to make changes needed to update outdated standards on emerging issues and take into account climate change. There are opportunities to strengthen capacity and enhance water quality management actions for contaminants of emerging concern, nutrient pollution, sea level rise, and wastewater/stormwater treatment all to improve coastal water quality for people and nature. THE ORGANIZATION IS A QUALIFIED TAX EXEMPT ORGANIZATION UNDER CODE SECTION 501(C)(3) OF THE INTERNAL REVENUE CODE AND IS EXEMPT FROM INCOME TAXES. IN ADDITION, THE ORGANIZATION IS SUBJECT TO INCOME TAX ON NET INCOME THAT IS DERIVED FROM BUSINESS ACTIVITIES THAT ARE UNRELATED TO THEIR EXEMPT PURPOSE.

SPECIAL EVENT EXPENSES 501,133.

SPECIAL EVENT EXPENSES 501,133.

THE ORGANIZATION USES A CONSISTENT APPROACH WHEN EVALUATING SUPPORT FOR OTHER ORGANIZATIONS, PROGRAMS OR INDIVIDUALS WITHIN COASTAL COMMUNITIES. ORGANIZATIONS AND PROGRAMS MUST CLEARLY BENEFIT THE COMMUNITY AND HAVE A SIMILAR MISSION. INDIVIDUALS APPLY FOR DIRECT SUPPORT VIA A STANDARD QUESTIONNAIRE WITH ANSWERS BEING EVALUATED ANONYMOUSLY TO DETERMINE NEED.

THE TOTALS REPORTED IN COLUMN (B) REPRESENT THE TOTAL NUMBER OF CONTRIBUTIONS RECEIEVED FOR EACH CATEGORY DURING THE CALENDAR YEAR ENDED DECEMBER 31, 2024.

A CERTIFIED PUBLIC ACCOUNTANT REVIEWS THE FORM 990 WITH THE ORGANIZATION'S CFO. THE CFO THEN REVIEWS THE FORM 990 WITH THE FULL BOARD OF DIRECTORS PRIOR TO FILING.

POTENTIAL CONFLICTS OF INTEREST ARE REVIEWED AND DETERMINED AT THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS LEVEL. THE ORGANIZATION'S BYLAWS INCLUDE A CONFLICT-OF-INTEREST REQUIREMENT. ALL BOARD OF DIRECTORS ARE REQUIRED TO SIGN AN ANNUAL CONFLICT-OF-INTEREST DOCUMENT. IF A CONFLICT ARISES, THE PERSON WITH THE CONFLICT IS NOT ALLOWED TO VOTE ON THE TRANSACTION. THERE WERE NO INCIDENCES OF ANY CONFLICT OF INTEREST IN 2024.

THE ORGANIZATION'S EXECUTIVE COMMITTEE REVIEWS AND APPROVES THE COMPENSATION FOR THE ORGANIZATION'S CEO. THE PROCESS FOR DETERMINING AND ESTABLISHING THE CEO'S COMPENSATION INCLUDES USE OF DATA SUCH AS FORMS 990 OF OTHER ORGANIZATIONS FOR COMPARABLE COMPENSATION INFORMATION FOR SIMILARLY QUALIFIED PERSONS IN FUNCTIONALLY COMPARABLE POSITIONS AT SIMILARLY SITUATED ORGANIZATIONS. PERSONS WITH A CONFLICT OF INTEREST REGARDING THE COMPENSATION ARRANGEMENT AT ISSUE ARE NOT INVOLVED. THE ORGANIZATION MAINTAINS CONTEMPORANEOUS DOCUMENTATION AND RECORDKEEPING FOR DELIBERATIONS AND DECISIONS REGARDING THE COMPENSATION ARRANGEMENT. THIS PROCESS WAS LAST DONE AND DOCUMENTED IN JUNE 2025. COMPENSATION FOR THE ORGANIZATION'S CFO IS REVIEWED BY THE ORGANIZATION'S CEO AND THIS PROCESS WAS LAST DONE AND DOCUMENTED IN JUNE 2025.

THE ORGANIZATION MAKES ITS GOVERNING DOCUMENTS, CONFLICT OF INTEREST POLICY, AND FINANCIAL STATEMENTS AVAILABLE TO THE PUBLIC UPON REQUEST, INCLUDING THE ORGANIZATION'S BY-LAWS, PERSONNEL POLICY, SAFETY PROCEDURES AND OPERATIONAL DOCUMENT, LICENSE AND PERMIT DOCUMENTS, VOLUNTEER GUIDELINES AND PROCEDURES.

mainOrgs.name: Fishchoice Inc
mainOrgs.missionSummary: Reduce seafood industry's environmental impact by promoting sustainable sourcing.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Online tool for tracking global fishery improvements.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Some connection to water resource stewardship through sustainable seafood sourcing.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Two main programs: online tracking tool and education at industry conferences.
mainOrgs.notableSignals: Tracks over 90% of global fishery improvement projects.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: With 17 years of experience and $2.4M in revenue, FISHCHOICE INC focuses on sustainable seafood sourcing but shows a 30.6% negative revenue trend, warranting attention.
mainOrgs.softFlags: Months unrestricted below threshold; Declining revenue trend (-31% over 3 yrs); Revenue concentrated in Contributions (99%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: FISHCHOICE INC IS ORGANIZED AS A PUBLIC CHARITY TO DECREASE THE ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACTS OF THE SEAFOOD INDUSTRY BY CREATING TOOLS TO INCREASE THE AMOUNT OF SEAFOOD PURCHASED FROM SUSTAINABLE SOURCES AND DECREASE THE AMOUNT OF SEAFOOD PURCHASED FROM SOURCES THAT DEGRADE THE ENVIRONMENT.
mainOrgs.program2Desc: FISHCHOICE HAS DEVELOPED AN ONLINE TOOL TO TRACK THE IMPROVEMENTS OF FISHERIES GLOBALLY. OVER 90% OF GLOBAL FISHERY IMPROVEMENT PROJECTS THAT ARE RUN BY VARIOUS NGO'S AROUND THE GLOBE ARE TRACKED IN ONE PLACE, FISHERYPROGRESS.ORG. THIS ENABLES GOVERNMENTS, NGO'S AND INDUSTRY TO EFFICIENTLY PUSH FOR IMPROVEMENTS THAT LEAD TO MORE SUSTAINABLE FISHERIES. FISHERYPROGRESS.ORG IS OVERSEEN BY AN ADVISORY COMMITTEE AND MANAGED DAY TO DAY BY FISHCHOICE.
mainOrgs.program3Desc: ORGANIZATION PERSONNEL HAVE ATTENDED NUMEROUS SEAFOOD INDUSTRY CONFERENCES IN ORDER TO EDUCATE BUYERS AND SUPPLIERS OF SEAFOOD PRODUCTS ABOUT FISHCHOICE AND HOW THE ORGANIZATION'S WEBSITE CAN BE USED BY THE SEAFOOD INDUSTRY TO INCREASE PURCHASES OF SUSTAINABLE SEAFOOD PRODUCTS AND DECREASE DEMAND PRESSURES ON FISHES AND AQUACULTURE THAT ARE NOT SUSTAINABLE. KNOWLEDGE GLEANED AT THESE CONFERENCES ALLOWS FISHCHOICE TO DEFINE POLICIES AND PROCEDURES IN A MANNER COMPATIBLE WITH OTHER NONPROFIT ENTITIES OPERATING IN THE SAME OR SIMILAR NATIONAL AND GLOBAL ENVIRONMENT SPACE.
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
FISHCHOICE DOES NOT HAVE ANY COMMITTEES WITH AUTHORITY TO ACT ON BEHALF OF THE GOVERNING BODY.

THE FORM 990 IS REVIEWED AND APPROVED BY THE ENTIRE GOVERNING BODY BEFORE IT IS FILED WITH THE IRS.

ALL OFFICERS AND DIRECTORS OF THE ORGANIZATION ARE REQUIRED TO SIGN A CONFLICT OF INTEREST STATEMENT. IN CONNECTION WITH ANY ACTUAL OR POTENTIAL CONFLICT OF INTEREST, ALL OFFICERS AND DIRECTORS MUST DISCLOSE THE EXISTENCE OR ANY FINANCIAL INTEREST TO THE BOARD ON AN ANNUAL BASIS, IF APPLICABLE. ANY ACTUAL OR POTENTIAL CONFLICT OF INTEREST IS REVIEWED BY THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS TO DETERMINE IF A CONFLICT OF INTEREST EXISTS. INDIVIDUALS WITH THE POTENTIAL OR ACTUAL CONFLICT OF INTEREST ARE EXCLUDED FROM DELIBERATIONS AND VOTING ON THE MATTER.

FISHCHOICE'S BOARD OF DIRECTORS, EXCLUDING THE PRESIDENT/CEO, REVIEWS AND APPROVES THE COMPENSATION OF THE PRESIDENT/CEO ON AN ANNUAL BASIS. COMPARABILITY DATA IS USED DURING THE REVIEW PROCESS TO ENSURE COMPENSATION LEVELS ARE REASONABLE.

FISHCHOICE MAKES IT GOVERNING DOCUMENTS AND FINANCIAL STATEMENTS AVAILABLE TO THE PUBLIC UPON REQUEST.

OTHER FEES: PROGRAM SERVICE EXPENSES 760,532. MANAGEMENT AND GENERAL EXPENSES 190,134. FUNDRAISING EXPENSES 0. TOTAL EXPENSES 950,666.

## Home

![Image 1](https://fishchoice.com/sites/default/files/styles/homepage_simplecrop/public/Untitled%20design%20%2832%29.png?itok=IZLIhuJC&sc=f4cf4a9e4b3b5e98298cc4514037f8fb)

Sustainable Seafood Made Easy

Whether you’re new to the world of sustainable seafood or an old salty pro, FishChoice creates online tools that give you and your business the power to make progress on seafood sustainability. Sign up for a free account or log in to get the most from your FishChoice experience!

About Us

Taking action on sustainability starts with having clear, easy-to-access information. FishChoice develops online tools that put actionable information directly in the hands of industry leaders, resource managers, policymakers, donors, and more.

We are a passionate team of ocean advocates who recognize that environmental and social aspects of sustainability are inextricably linked. And to make informed, responsible decisions that protect the health of our oceans and communities that depend on them, decision-makers need credible and transparent information to take the right actions on all dimensions of sustainability.

Title: Colorado River Land Trust

URL Source: https://www.coloradoriverlandtrust.org/

Published Time: Fri, 18 Apr 2025 13:43:40 GMT

Markdown Content:
* * *

## **PROTECT TEXAS**

They say everything is bigger in Texas, but the truth is, Texas is still growing. Our state now boasts five of the fifteen largest cities in the country. There was a time when the vast majority of Texas land was part of a farm, ranch, or forest, but today Texas is losing its open country faster than any other state in the nation—especially within the Colorado River watershed.

Right now, the land, water, and wildlife we care about are at risk.

![Image 1: Patterson Ranch](https://www.coloradoriverlandtrust.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/01/patterson_ranch_with_vehicle.jpg)

* * *

## **Texas Earned.**

_“Everyday you wake up, look in the mirror, and ask yourself, what kind of Texan are you going to be today? Texas expects more out of us.”_

Marcus Luttrell

## With its wide-open landscapes, eternal skies and abundant wildlife, Texas is one-of-a-kind.

We believe in our responsibility as Texans to protect our own backyard and conserve our most valuable resources—our land, water, and traditions. Join us and help protect Texas.

![Image 2: icon-texas](https://www.coloradoriverlandtrust.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/01/icon-texas.svg)

## Who We Are

We protect Texas today and for future generations.

![Image 3: icon-land](https://www.coloradoriverlandtrust.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/01/icon-land.svg)

##

mainOrgs.name: Harpeth Conservancy
mainOrgs.missionSummary: Protecting clean water and ecosystems for Tennessee's rivers through science and community stewardship.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Clean Water Protection and Outreach, Education & Recreation programs.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Focuses directly on protecting water quality and river ecosystems in Tennessee.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Engages in two interrelated programs focused on water protection and community involvement.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: Harpeth Conservancy is a mature organization with 26 years of dedicated service and a revenue of $1.5M. Its strong expertise in water conservation ensures effective stewardship and impactful community programs.
mainOrgs.websiteRecentActivity: Highlighted ongoing efforts for the Harpeth River and legislative priorities for 2026.
mainOrgs.softFlags: Revenue concentrated in Contributions (123%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: TO RESTORE AND PROTECT CLEAN WATER AND HEALTHY ECOSYSTEMS FOR RIVERS IN TENNESSEE BY EMPLOYING SCIENTIFIC EXPERTISE AND COLLABORATIVE RELATIONSHIPS TO DEVELOP, PROMOTE, AND SUPPORT BROAD COMMUNITY STEWARDSHIP AND ACTION.
mainOrgs.program2Desc: THE CLEAN WATER PROTECTION PROGRAM ADVANCES THE REGULATORY AND POLICY SOLUTIONS NECESSARY TO PROTECT AND IMPROVE WATER QUALITY IN TENNESSEE. THIS PROGRAM WORKS TO ESTABLISH SCIENTIFICALLY BASED POLICY THAT IMPROVES WATER QUALITY, INCLUDING WATER WITHDRAWAL PERMITS THAT LEAVE SUFFICIENT FLOW FOR WILDLIFE, NUTRIENT POLLUTION LIMITS THAT RESTORE NATURAL WATER CHEMISTRY, AND STORMWATER REGULATIONS THAT REDUCE EROSION. WE ARE ACTIVELY PURSUING AND DEFENDING REGULATORY SYSTEMS THAT PROTECT THE HEALTH OF HUMANS AND WILDLIFE BY WORKING WITH STATE AGENCIES AND LOCAL GOVERNMENTS, ORGANIZING STATEWIDE COALITIONS, AND BUILDING LOCAL CITIZEN ENGAGEMENT. THE EFFORT TO SET LIMITS ON THE AMOUNT OF NUTRIENT POLLUTION ENTERING THE HARPETH IS THE MOST ADVANCED IN THE STATE, AND WE STRIVE TO MAKE THIS A MODEL FOR RIVERS THROUGHOUT THE SOUTHEASTERN US.
mainOrgs.program3Desc: THE OUTREACH, EDUCATION, & RECREATION PROGRAM CREATES OPPORTUNITIES TO FOSTER AWARENESS OF ISSUES AFFECTING FRESHWATER RESOURCES AND APPRECIATION FOR THE INCREDIBLE DIVERSITY OF SPECIES FOUND IN THE REGION. THIS PROGRAM WORKS WITH CIVIC ORGANIZATIONS, COMMUNITY GROUPS, AND INDIVIDUALS TO BUILD THE LOCAL SUPPORT AND INVOLVEMENT NECESSARY TO ACHIEVE OUR MISSION OF PROTECTING RIVERS AND CLEAN WATER. THROUGH EDUCATIONAL EVENTS WITH LOCAL YOUTH PROGRAMS, AWARENESS CAMPAIGNS, AND PROVIDING INFORMATION ON WAYS INDIVIDUALS CAN HELP, WE HOPE TO INSPIRE PERSONAL ACTIVISM AND DEVELOP COMMUNITY LEADERS. WE VIEW RECREATION AS AN AVENUE TO BUILDING STRONG ENVIRONMENTAL STEWARDS, AND WORK TO PROMOTE ACCESS TO RIVERS BY DEVELOPING BLUEWAYS AND PARTNERING WITH LOCAL OUTFITTERS TO DEMONSTRATE RESPONSIBLE USE OF OUR RESOURCES.
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
STAFF ENGAGED IN LOBBYING ON A LIMITED BASIS (57 HOURS FOR THE REPORTING PERIOD) TO PROVIDE INFORMATION FOR PROPOSED LEGISLATION THAT WOULD DIRECTLY EFFECT THE MISSION OF THE ORGANIZATION.

THE ORGANIZATION FOLLOWS FASB ASC 740-10, ACCOUNTING FOR UNCERTAINTY IN INCOME TAXES, AS IT RELATES TO THE UNCERTAIN TAX POSITIONS. FOR ALL TAX POSITIONS TAKEN BY THE ORGANIZATION, MANAGEMENT BELIEVES IT IS CLEAR THAT THE LIKELIHOOD IS GREATER THAN 50% THAT THE FULL AMOUNT OF THE TAX POSITION TAKEN WILL BE ULTIMATELY REALIZED. THEREFORE, MANAGEMENT BELIEVES THAT NO LIABILITY FOR UNRECOGNIZED TAX BENEFITS SHOULD BE RECORDED RELATED TO UNCERTAIN TAX POSITIONS TAKEN ON RETURNS FOR THE THREE MOST RECENT YEARS FILED, OR EXPECTED TO BE TAKEN IN THE ORGANIZATIONS'S CURRENT YEAR TAX RETURN. THE ORGANIZATION IDENTIFIES ITS MAJOR TAX JURISDICTIONS AS THE U.S. FEDERAL AND THE STATE OF TENNESSEE. HOWEVER, THE ORGANIZATION IS NOT CURRENTLY UNDER AUDIT NOR HAS THE ORGANIZATION BEEN CONTACTED BY EITHER OF THESE JURISDICTIONS. AS OF MARCH 31, 2025, THE ORGANIZATION HAS ACCRUED NO INTEREST OR PENALTIES RELATED TO UNCERTAIN TAX POSITIONS.

DONATION OF SERVICES FOR SILENT AUCTION 10,738.

THE ORGANIZATION ACTS AS A FISCAL SPONSOR FOR THE LIVING LAND, THEREBY COLLECTING DONATIONS/GRANTS DESIGNATED FOR THE ORGANIZATION. THE ORGANIZATION ALSO PAYS EXPENSES ON BEHALF OF THIS ORGANIZATION BASED ON THEIR GUIDANCE.

ORGANIZATION'S PROCESS TO REVIEW FORM 990 THE DRAFT OF THE 990 IS REVIEWED BY THE FINANCIAL ADMINISTRATION STAFF TEAM AND SENT TO THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS' FINANCE COMMITTEE FOR REVIEW BEFORE SUBMITTAL TO THE IRS.

HARPETH CONSERVANCY'S BY-LAWS, SECTION III.F. SPECIFY THE PROCESS THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS FOLLOWS TO REVIEW POTENTIAL CONFLICTS OF INTEREST WITH MEMBERS OF THE BOARD. HARPETH CONSERVANCY ALSO HAS A CONFLICT OF INTEREST POLICY IN ITS PERSONNEL HANDBOOK AND A CONFLICT OF INTEREST POLICY FORM THAT ALL INCOMING STAFF MUST REVIEW AND SIGN INDICATING THAT THEY UNDERSTAND THE ORGANIZATION'S INTERNAL CONFLICT OF INTEREST POLICY FOR STAFF.

THE COMPENSATION PROCESS FOR THE CEO AND PRESIDENT AND ALL FULL-TIME EMPLOYEES ARE REVIEWED ANNUALLY. SUPERVISORS REVIEW STAFF THEY MANAGE. THE UPCOMING CALENDAR YEAR COLA SET BY THE SOCIAL SECURITY ADMINISTRATION IS BUILT INTO THE ANNUAL BUDGET FOR ALL STAFF AS A STANDARD PROCESS UNLESS LARGER SALARY INCREASES ARE PROPOSED OR THERE ARE BUDGETARY CONSTRAINTS. THE PROPOSED ANNUAL STAFF COMPENSATION INCLUDING BENEFITS FOR THE COMING FISCAL YEAR IS REVIEWED AND RECOMMENDED BY THE BOARD FINANCE COMMITTEE FOR BOARD APPROVAL DURING THE BUDGET PROCESS. FOR THE CEO AND PRESIDENT, THE BOARD'S GOVERNANCE COMMITTEE MANAGES AN ANNUAL REVIEW. THE PROCESS ALSO INCLUDES A COMPARISON OF COMPARABLE SALARIES IN THE REGION AND NATIONWIDE. SIGNIFICANT CHANGES TO SALARY AND COMPENSATION FOR THE PRESIDENT AND CEO ARE REVIEWED BY THE FINANCE COMMITTEE BEFORE REVIEW AND APPROVAL BY THE BOARD.

THE ORGANIZATION'S GOVERNING DOCUMENTS ARE MADE AVAILABLE TO THE GENERAL PUBLIC THROUGH THE ORGANIZATION'S WEBSITE, PUBLIC WEBSITES SUCH AS CANDID AND GIVING MATTERS AND THE DOCUMENTS ARE AVAILABLE UPON REQUEST.

mainOrgs.name: Friends Of The Teton River Inc
mainOrgs.missionSummary: Protects and improves clean water and healthy streams in the Teton River watershed.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Community education and watershed monitoring.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Core focus on watershed protection, water quality, and fisheries.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Programs related to education and watershed health.
mainOrgs.notableSignals: 25 years active with strong revenue growth and high current ratio.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: With over 25 years of experience, FTR has demonstrated a strong commitment to watershed health, showcasing effective community education and scientific monitoring programs. Financially stable with positive revenue trends.
mainOrgs.websiteRecentActivity: Recent projects include stream bank restoration and the Teton Creek Corridor Project, enhancing ecosystem and community engagement.
mainOrgs.softFlags: Months unrestricted below threshold; Revenue concentrated in Contributions (99%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: FRIENDS OF THE TETON RIVER (FTR) USES SOUND SCIENCE AND COLLABORATION TO PROTECT AND IMPROVE CLEAN WATER, HEALTHY STREAMS AND A THRIVING FISHERY IN THE TETON RIVER WATERSHED. FTR IMPLEMENTS COMMUNITY-DRIVEN RESTORATION PROJECTS AND EDUCATION PROGRAMS TO BENEFIT WATER RESOURCES IN THE TETON RIVER WATERSHED.
mainOrgs.program2Desc: COMMUNITY EDUCATION: FTR'S COMMUNITY EDUCATION PROGRAM PROVIDES OPPORTUNITIES FOR COMMUNITY MEMBERS OF ALL AGES TO LEARN & MAKE INFORMED DECISIONS ABOUT WATER. IN FY2024, FTR CONTINUED TO ENGAGE K-12 STUDENTS IN CLASSROOM, ONLINE, AND FIELD SCIENCE PROGRAMS; IMPLEMENTED COST SHARE & COMMUNITY EDUCATION PROGRAMS FOR DRINKING WATER TESTING AND PROTECTION, HOSTED A SERIES OF EDUCATIONAL FIELD TOURS, COORDINATED AGRICULTURAL AND RESPONSIBLE RECREATION EDUCATION INITIATIVES.
mainOrgs.program3Desc: WATERSHED MONITORING: FTR CONDUCTS RESEARCH ON & MONITORS TROUT POPULATIONS, WATER QUALITY, RECREATION, SOIL HEALTH & WATER QUANTITY. THIS RESEARCH IS DESIGNED TO MEASURE RESTORATION PROGRAM IMPACTS, PRIORITIZE FUTURE RESTORATION WORK, TRACK TRENDS & IDENTIFY THREATS TO WATERSHED HEALTH. IN FY2024, FTR COLLECTED STREAM TEMPERATURE, FLOW AND FISHERIES DATA; MONITORED GROUNDWATER, SOIL MOISTURE, AND SOIL HEALTH; AND COLLECTED RIVER USE DATA.
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
FRIENDS OF THE TETON RIVER (FTR) USES SOUND SCIENCE AND COLLABORATION TO PROTECT AND IMPROVE CLEAN WATER, HEALTHY STREAMS AND A THRIVING FISHERY IN THE TETON RIVER WATERSHED. FTR IMPLEMENTS COMMUNITY-DRIVEN RESTORATION PROJECTS AND EDUCATION PROGRAMS TO BENEFIT WATER RESOURCES IN THE TETON RIVER WATERSHED.

FORM 990 IS REVIEWED BY MEMBERS OF THE FTR FINANCE COMMITTEE AND THE EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR INDEPENDENTLY. IT IS THEN DISCUSSED AT A FINANCE COMMITTEE MEETING, AND A RECOMMENDATION IS PREPARED. THE 990 AND FINANCE COMMITTEE RECOMMENDATIONS ARE PRESENTED TO THE FULL BOARD OF DIRECTORS FOR APPROVAL BY VOTE PRIOR TO FILING.

THE CONFLICT OF INTEREST POLICY IS REVIEWED ON AN ANNUAL BASIS AND ENFORCED BY THE EXECUTIVE COMMITTEE OF THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS, AND IT IS A POINT OF ORDER BEFORE VOTING OCCURS.

SALARIES ARE REVIEWED ANNUALLY BY THE COMPENSATION AND FINANCE COMMITTEES AS PART OF THE BUDGETING PROCESS AND ARE COMPARED TO OTHER SALARIES OF COMPARABLE-SIZED ORGANIZATIONS IN THE REGION.

AVAILABLE UPON REQUEST

## EXPLORE OUR WORK

[![Image 3: Friends of the Teton River](https://www.tetonwater.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/07/StoryMap-Image.jpg)](https://buxtonrestoration.tetonwater.org/)

### STREAM BANK RESTORATION

FTR’s river restoration projects address watershed issues in a holistic way—to benefit fish, wildlife and ecosystem function—for generations to come. Click on the link below to visit the digital story map and learn about restoring unstable stream banks at the Buxton River Park! This Story is representative of the work we do throughout the watershed to address multiple project goals and community needs.

![Image 4: Friends of the Teton River](https://www.tetonwater.org/wp-content/uploads/2018/07/teetongreek-opt2-e1529424937613.jpg)

### TETON CREEK CORRIDOR PROJECT

This project builds on over a decade of stream restoration and conservation work in the beautiful Teton Creek corridor, just outside of Driggs. With your help, it will be an ecological and recreational asset to our community, for all to steward and enjoy.

## Camrin Dengel

mainOrgs.name: Ozarks Environmental Services
mainOrgs.missionSummary: Support water quality protection in the Upper White River watershed.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Supports the mission of the Upper White River Basin Foundation.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Core focus on protecting water quality in a specific watershed.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Single mission oriented towards watershed protection.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: OZARKS ENVIRONMENTAL SERVICES has been established for 9 years with $2.4M revenue. Its strong current ratio indicates good financial health while focusing on water quality in the Upper White River watershed.
mainOrgs.websiteConsistencySignal: 990 emphasizes watershed protection; website primarily focuses on operation and maintenance of water systems.
mainOrgs.softFlags: Revenue concentrated in Program Services (100%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: TO SUPPORT UPPER WHITE RIVER BASIN FOUNDATION'S MISSION OF PROTECTING WATER QUALITY IN THE UPPER WHITE RIVER WATERSHED OF SOUTHWEST MISSOURI AND NORTHWEST ARKANSAS.
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
THE ORGANIZATION HAS ANALYZED TAX POSITIONS TAKEN AND CONCLUDED THAT AS OF DECEMBER 31, 2024, THERE ARE NO UNCERTAIN POSITIONS TAKEN, OR EXPECTED TO BE TAKEN, THAT WOULD REQUIRE DISCLOSURE IN THE FINANCIAL STATEMENTS. THE ORGANIZATION IS SUBJECT TO ROUTINE AUDITS BY TAXING JURISDICTIONS; HOWEVER, THERE ARE CURRENTLY NO AUDITS FOR ANY TAX PERIODS IN PROGRESS. THE FOUNDATION DOES NOT BELIEVE IT LIKELY THAT CHANGES WILL OCCUR WITHIN THE NEXT FISCAL YEAR THAT WILL HAVE A MATERIAL IMPACT ON THE FINANCIAL STATEMENTS.

OES WAS FORMED AS A SUPPORTING ORGANIZATION OF UPPER WHITE RIVER BASIN. THE TWO ENTITIES HAVE COMMON BOARD MEMBERS AND DAVID CASELETTO IS THE PRESIDENT OF BOTH ORGANIZATIONS.

OZARKS ENVIRONMENTAL SERVICES (THE ORGANIZATION") RECEIVED IRS STATUS AS A SUPPORTING ORGANIZATION FOR THE UPPER WHITE RIVER BASIN FOUNDATION (THE "FOUNDATION"). THE FOUNDATION IS A 501(C)3 WATERSHED CONSERVATION ORGANIZATION, DEDICATED TO PROTECTING WATER QUALITY IN THE UPPER WHITE RIVER WATERSHED OF SOUTHWEST MISSOURI AND NORTHWEST ARKANSAS. THE FOUNDATION ENGAGES IN MANY DIFFERENT ACTIVITIES TO HELP PROTECT THE UPPER WHITE RIVER WATERSHED'S WATERWAYS AND WATER SOURCES FROM EXCESSIVE POLLUTION AND DEGRADATION, INCLUDING EDUCATING THE PUBLIC ABOUT NEW TECHNOLOGIES AND PRACTICES FOR DECENTRALIZED WASTEWATER TREATMENT. THE ORGANIZATION WAS FORMED BY THE FOUNDATION SPECIFICALLY TO ADDRESS THE ISSUE OF THERE BEING A LACK OF QUALIFIED TECHNICIANS TO PERFORM THE NECESSARY MAINTENANCE WORK ON THE ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT SYSTEMS. THE ORGANIZATION SUPPORTS AND FURTHERS THE FOUNDATION'S TAX-EXEMPT PURPOSE OF PROTECTING THE WATER QUALITY OF THE UPPER WHITE RIVER WATERSHED BY PERFORMING MAINTENANCE WORK ON ADVANCED WASTEWATER TREATMENT SYSTEMS AND DRINKING WATER SYSTEMS IN COMMUNITIES NOT SERVED BY TRADITIONAL MUNICIPAL SYSTEMS.

THE INITIAL REVIEW OF THE 990 IS PERFORMED BY MANAGEMENT. AFTER APPROVAL, THE RETURN IS PROVIDED TO THE BOARD.

COMPENSATION OF THE ORGANIZATION'S PRESIDENT IS REVIEWED USING COMPARATIVE SALARIES AND COMPENSATION INFORMATION AND IS APPROVED BY THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS. DECISIONS ARE DOCUMENTED IN BOARD MINUTES.

GOVERNING DOCUMENTS, CONFLICT OF INTEREST POLICY AND FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ARE MADE AVAILABLE TO THE PUBLIC UPON REQUEST.

mainOrgs.missionSummary: Preserve and protect the San Dieguito River Valley's natural and cultural resources through collaborative efforts.

mainOrgs.notableSignals: Strong community engagement through educational and recreational programs.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: The San Dieguito River Valley Land Conservancy is an emerging organization with substantial revenue growth and a mission focused on water resource stewardship. With a strong emphasis on education and recreation, it demonstrates commitment to the conservation of local watersheds.

mainOrgs.softFlags: Revenue concentrated in Contributions (95%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: TO PRESERVE, PROTECT, AND SHARE THE NATURAL AND CULTURAL RESOURCES OF THE SAN DIEGUITO RIVER VALLEY THROUGH COLLABORATIVE EFFORTS TO ACQUIRE LANDS, COMPLETE TRAILS, RESTORE HABITATS, ESTABLISH EDUCATIONAL PROGRAMS, CREATE INTERPRETIVE CENTERS, ENCOURAGE RECREATION, AND MOBILIZE PUBLIC SUPPORT.
mainOrgs.program2Desc: EDUCATION: SDRVC EDUCATED HUNDREDS OF PRE-K THROUGH HIGH SCHOOL STUDENTS THROUGH OUR ONLINE NATURE PROGRAMS - POLLINATOR'S PARADISE, WATERSHED EXPLORERS AND COASTAL WETLANDS FIELD ECOLOGY PROJECT.
mainOrgs.program3Desc: RECREATION: SDRVC PROVIDED MANY RECREATIONAL OPPORTUNITIES TO CITIZENS TO ENJOY THE COAST TO CREST TRAIL AND OTHER COMMUNITY TRAILS WITHIN THE SAN DIEGUITO RIVER VALLEY INCLUDING THE C2C TRAIL CHALLENGE.
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
MANAGEMENT AGREEMENTS WITH THE SAN DIEGUITO RIVER PARK JOINT POWERS AUTHORITY ARE IN PLACE TO MANAGE AND MONITOR THE PROPERTIES FOR THE CONSERVANCY.

THE ASSETS IN THE BERNARDO MOUNTAIN MITIGATION FUND AND SDRVC QUASI-ENDOWMENT FUND ARE MAINTAINED BY THE RANCHO SANTA FE FOUNDATION. THE FUNDS ARE FOR THE LONG TERM PRESERVATION OF THE ORGANIZATION AND ARE INVESTED TO GENERATE RETURNS TO BE USED FOR OPERATIONS.

THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS RECEIVES A TREASURER'S REPORT THAT INCLUDES THE FORM 990. THE TREASURER SPENDS DAYS REVIEWING THE FINACIAL REPORTS FOR YEAR END WITH THE BOOKKEEPER IN ORDER TO PROVIDE THE CPA WITH GOOD INFORMATION. THE 990 IS THEN DRAFTED AND PRESENTED TO THE FINANCE COMMITTEE FOR APPROVAL. UPON APPROVAL, THE 990 IS MADE AVAILABLE TO THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS.

BOARD MEMBERS ARE COUNSELED AT ELECTION AND QUARTERLY OF THEIR OBLIGATIONS TO IDENTIFY AND PUBLICLY DISCLOSE IF THERE ARE ANY CONFLICT OF INTEREST ISSUES. THE EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR IS CHARGED WITH THE RESPONSIBILITY TO CALL TO THE ATTENTION OF THE SDRVC PRESIDENT AND BOARD POTENTIAL CONFLICTS OF INTEREST. THE PRESIDENT AND HUMAN RESOURCES COMMITTEE MONITORS POTENTIAL STAFF-LEVEL CONFLICTS OF INTEREST.

COMPARABILITY DATA FROM OTHER FORM 990'S OF SIMILAR LAND CONSERVANCY'S ARE REVIEWED BY THE HIRING COMMITTEE AND THE BOARD IN ORDER TO SET THE SALARY FOR THE EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR.

ALL RECORDS OF THE SAN DIEGUITO RIVER VALLEY LAND CONSERVANCY ARE OPEN TO MEMBERS OF THE PUBLIC. GOVERNING DOCUMENTS, POLICIES, AND FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ARE IDENTIFIED AS BEING AVAILABLE ON THE SDRVC WEB PAGE AND ARE SUMMARIZED THERE. THE FINANCIAL STATUS OF THE ORGANIZATION IS REGULARLY REPORTED ON THE ORGANIZATIONS NEWSLETTER, CURRENTS, AND IS POSTED AT GUIDESTAR. FORM 990 DATA IS ALSO CARTED AND PUBLISHED ON BETTERGIVING AT THE SAN DIEGO FOUNDATION.

mainOrgs.name: Rogue River Watershed Council
mainOrgs.missionSummary: Enhance resilience in Rogue River watersheds through ecological restoration and community engagement.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Community engagement and water quality improvement.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: The mission focuses directly on watershed health and ecological restoration.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Engagement and water quality improvement programs are closely related to the core mission.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: With 17.7 years of active service and $2.7M in revenue, the Council effectively enhances Rogue River watersheds through targeted engagement and restoration programs.
mainOrgs.websiteRecentActivity: Fundraising event with the Medford Rogues for river restoration on June 11.
mainOrgs.softFlags: Months unrestricted below threshold; Revenue concentrated in Contributions (100%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: ENHANCE RESILIENCE IN ROGUE RIVER WATERSHEDS THROUGH ECOLOGICAL RESTORATION AND ENGAGEMENT WITH COMMUNITY PARTNERS.
mainOrgs.program2Desc: ENGAGEMENT - THE COUNCIL USES A VARIETY OF STRATEGIES TO COMMUNICATE AND ENGAGE WITH COMMUNITY MEMBERS ABOUT STREAM PROCESSES, FUNCTIONING WATERSHEDS, RESILIENT COMMUNITIES, AND COUNCIL ACTIVITIES. ENGAGEMENT STRATEGIES INCLUDE ACTIVE SOCIAL MEDIA ACCOUNTS, A REGULARLY UPDATED WEBSITE, SHORT VIDEO PRODUCTIONS, AND VIDEO LIBRARY, AS WELL AS SHORT NEWSLETTERS (EVERY OTHER MONTH) AND LENGTHIER NEWSLETTERS (BIANNUAL). ENGAGEMENT ACTIONS SUPPLEMENT AND PROMOTE THE COUNCIL'S RESTORATION EFFORTS.
mainOrgs.program3Desc: WATER QUALITY IMPROVEMENT - THE COUNCIL WORKS TO ADDRESS ISSUES THAT POLLUTE AND WARM THE STREAMS IN OUR REGION. THE COUNCIL HAS DEVELOPED A COLLABORATIVE WATER QUALITY IMPROVEMENT PROGRAM THAT WILL HELP ACHIEVE THE GOAL OF CLEANER WATER. SPECIFICALLY, THE INTENT OF THE PROGRAM IS TO ADDRESS PERSISTENT WATER QUALITY PROBLEMS THAT INCLUDE HIGH TEMPERATURE, LOW DISSOLVED OXYGEN LEVELS, SEDIMENTATION, BACTERIA PRESENCE, AND NUTRIENT LOADING. CONTINUED ON SCHEDULE O. PROJECTS SUCH AS SIDE-CHANNEL RECONNECTION, STREAMSIDE REHABILITATION, CONVERTING FROM FLOOD TO SPRINKLER IRRIGATION, AND REDUCING STORMWATER RUNOFF IN URBAN AREAS PROVIDE WATER QUALITY BENEFITS INCLUDING DECREASED STREAM BANK EROSION, INCREASED SHADING, AND DECREASED NUTRIENT AND BACTERIA ENTERING THE STREAM. THE COUNCIL ALSO WORKS WITH DRINKING WATER PROVIDERS TO STRATEGICALLY LOCATE ECOLOGICAL RESTORATION PRACTICES AND ON FARM AGRICULTURE IMPROVEMENTS TO BENEFIT SOURCE WATER PROTECTION EFFORTS.
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
THE FORM 990 IS PREPARED BY INDEPENDENT ACCOUNTANT, AND REVIEWED BY MANAGMENT. THE FORM IS THEN PRESENTED TO THE BOARD FOR REVIEW AND APPROVAL PRIOR TO FILING.

THE BOARD MONITORS AND ENFORCES COMPLIANCE WITH THIS POLICY BY REVIEWING ANNUAL CONFLICT OF INTERESTS STATEMENTS AND TAKING OTHER ACTIONS AS NECESSARY FOR EFFECTIVE OVERSIGHT.

THE COUNCIL MAKES ITS GOVERNING DOCUMENTS, CONFLICT OF INTEREST POLICY, AND FINANCIAL STATEMENTS AVAILABLE UPON REQUEST. THE FINANCIAL STATEMENTS AND FORM 990 ARE ALSO AVAILABLE ON THE COUNCIL'S WEBSITE.

ENVIRONMENTAL DESIGN, CONSTRUCTION AND RESTORATION: PROGRAM SERVICE EXPENSES 1,927,649. MANAGEMENT AND GENERAL EXPENSES 0. FUNDRAISING EXPENSES 0. TOTAL EXPENSES 1,927,649. OTHER: PROGRAM SERVICE EXPENSES 0. MANAGEMENT AND GENERAL EXPENSES 4,636. FUNDRAISING EXPENSES 0. TOTAL EXPENSES 4,636.

mainOrgs.name: Watsonville Wetlands Watch
mainOrgs.missionSummary: Advocates for wetland issues, educates the public, restores habitats, and preserves wetlands in Pajaro Valley.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Environmental education for over 6,000 K-12 students; supports local policy initiatives.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Focuses intensively on wetland conservation and education.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Primarily focuses on education and advocacy related to wetlands.
mainOrgs.notableSignals: Engages community members and students actively in environmental issues.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: Watsonville Wetlands Watch, with over 26 years of experience, manages $1.5M in revenue and has strong program expense ratios, highlighting effective use of resources for wetlands advocacy and education.
mainOrgs.websiteRecentActivity: Upcoming Spring Native Plant Sale on May 2nd, 2026.
mainOrgs.softFlags: Days cash below tier threshold (63 < 90); Months unrestricted below threshold; Revenue concentrated in Contributions (99%); Program expense ratio below tier threshold (76%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: WATSONVILLE WETLANDS WATCH ADVOCATES FOR WETLAND ISSUES, EDUCATES IN SCHOOLS AND IN THE PUBLIC ARENA, RESTORES DEGRADED HABITATS, AND PRESERVES WETLANDS OF THE PAJARO VALLEY, ESPECIALLY INVOLVING MEMBERS OF THE WATSONVILLE COMMUNITY AND THE STUDENTS OF THE PAJARO VALLEY UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT.
mainOrgs.program2Desc: Environmental Education Programs: Watsonville Wetlands Watch provides outdoor and classroom based experiential environmental education programs for over 6,000 students in K-12, as well colleges and universities that increase knowledge, understanding, and appreciation of the environment and unique habitats of the Pajaro Valleys wetlands and provide meaningful ways for youth to contribute to local environmental and sustainability solutions. Our education programs are operated out of the Fitz Wetlands Educational Resource Center, an environmental education center and community center on the campus of Pajaro Valley High School. We run a volunteer docent program and offer free tours and lectures to the community that encourage the publics appreciation and increased understanding of the value of wetlands in the Pajaro Valley.
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
During the 2024 calendar year, Watsonville Wetlands Watch conducted limited support for local policy initiatives and City of Watsonville Council decisions related to land-use policy and projects. Our work included the time spent by 1 staff person to support organizational decision making, analysis of issues, and promotion of land-use policies and practices that benefit local wetlands.No other work was done during the 2024 calendar year related to political campaigns and lobbying activity.

THE ORGANIZATION HAS TWO TYPES OF ENDOWMENT FUNDS TO PROVIDE INCOME FOR THE ORGANIZATIONS ACTIVITIES, PROGRAMS AND OPERATIONS, AND TO PROVIDE INCOME FOR SCHOLARSHIPS AND STIPENDS MADE.

MANAGEMENT HAS EVALUATED THE ORGANIZATIONS TAX POSITIONS AND CONCLUDED THEY TOOK NO UNCERTAIN TAX POSITIONS REQUIRING AN ADJUSTMENT TO THE FINANCIAL STATEMENTS TO COMPLY WITH THE PROVISIONS ISSUED BY THE FINANCIAL ACCOUNTING STANDARDS BOARD.

STAFF REVIEWS BEFORE FILING; FINANCE COMMITTEE AND BOARD REVIEW AFTER FILING.

DONE ON AN AS-NEEDED BASIS VIA DISCUSSION AT MEETINGS OF THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS AS APPROPRIATE.

Compensation for the Executive Director is considered by the Personnel Committee prior to a recommendation to the Board of Directors. Review of this compensation is made with an effort to reflect competitive compensation with similar positions in the region, funding permitted.

Key employee salary and corresponding pay increases are set up by the Executive Director. This is determined based on comparable data such as the Non-profit compensation report. The board of directors and personnel committee review this data during the annual budgeting process. Changes in salary as recommended are incorporated into the budget process and adopted by the Board of Directors annually.

DOCUMENTS ARE MADE AVAILABLE UPON REQUEST. THE ORGANIZATION ALSO CREATES AN ANNUAL REPORT OF OPERATIONS AND FINANCES. THIS IS PUBLISHED ON THEIR WEBSITE FOR THE PUBLIC AND DISTRIBUTED VIA MAIL TO SUPPORTERS.

NET DONATED USE OF BUILDING = -$4232

mainOrgs.name: San Antonio River Foundation
mainOrgs.missionSummary: Inspires stewardship and conservation of the San Antonio River and its native ecology.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Educational field trips and public art events focusing on the river's history and ecology.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Directly focused on river conservation and education.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Includes educational activities and community engagement initiatives.
mainOrgs.notableSignals: Engaged over 120,000 students and participants through programs.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: With 22.6 years of experience, the San Antonio River Foundation has a strong revenue trend of 48.7% and a mature organization structure, enabling effective river conservation education.
mainOrgs.softFlags: Revenue concentrated in Contributions (83%); Staff shrinkage ≥20% YoY
mainOrgs.missionStatement: THROUGH ITS OUTREACH, WHETHER THROUGH EDUCATIONAL FIELD TRIPS OR PUBLIC ART, THE SAN ANTONIO RIVER FOUNDATION'S GOAL IS TO INSPIRE STEWARDSHIP AND BROADER PUBLIC CONSERVATION OF THE 240 MILES OF THE SAN ANTONIO RIVER AND ITS NATIVE ECOLOGY. SIGNIFICANT TIME AND VOLUNTEER RESOURCES ARE DEDICATED YEAR-ROUND TOWARD BRINGING INFORMATIVE PROGRAMS AND ENGAGING EVENTS WHICH HIGHLIGHT THE HISTORY, CULTURE, AND ECOLOGY OF THE RIVER AND THE COMMUNITIES IT HELPS SHAPE. ADDITIONALLY, THE RIVER FOUNDATION RAISES FUNDS TO SUPPORT EDUCATIONAL PROGRAMMING AT CONFLUENCE PARK. COLLECTIVELY, FOUNDATION PROGRAMS AT CONFLUENCE PARK HAVE SERVED MORE THAN 120,000 STUDENTS AND PARTICIPANTS FROM ACROSS THE ENTIRE METRO COMMUNITY, AS WELL AS SURROUNDING COUNTIES, RECONNECTING EACH WITH FUNDAMENTAL VALUES OF NATURE AND THE RIVER.
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
ENDOWMENT FUNDS ARE USED TO SUPPORT THE EDUCATION MISSION OF THE FOUNDATION, WHICH IS TO PROVIDE EDUCATIONAL ACTIVITIES INVOLVING CONFLUENCE PARK ALONG THE SAN ANTONIO RIVER AND TO SUPPORT REPAIRS AND MAINTENANCE OF CONFLUENCE PARK.

MANAGEMENT IS NOT AWARE OF ANY TAX POSITIONS THAT WOULD HAVE A SIGNIFICANT IMPACT ON ITS FINANCIAL POSITION. ITS FEDERAL TAX RETURNS FOR THE LAST FOUR YEARS REMAIN SUBJECT TO EXAMINATION.

THE FORM 990 IS PREPARED BY THE ORGANIZATION'S INDEPENDENT PUBLIC ACCOUNTING FIRM. IT IS THEN REVIEWED BY THE EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR, THE FINANCE COMMITTEE, AND THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS BEFORE SIGNING AND FILING WITH THE INTERNAL REVENUE SERVICE.

THE BOARD, AT LEAST ANNUALLY, REVIEWS ANY KNOWN CONFLICTS OF INTEREST.

EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR POSITION- RESEARCH IS COMPLETED BY THE BOARD REGARDING OTHER SAN ANTONIO NONPROFIT ORGANIZATIONS THROUGH ASSOCIATION OF FUNDRAISING PROFESSIONALS, TEXAS NONPROFIT ORGANIZATION, AND GUIDESTAR. SALARIES ARE DETERMINED BY COMPARING NONPROFIT SIZE AND OVERALL BUDGET AND EXPERIENCE OF THE INDIVIDUAL CANDIDATE. KEY EMPLOYEES- SPECIAL EVENTS COORDINATOR, PARK PROGRAMMING COORDINATOR AND ADMINISTRATIVE ASSISTANT: RESEARCH IS COMPLETED REGARDING OTHER SAN ANTONIO NONPROFIT ORGANIZATIONS THROUGH ASSOCIATION OF FUNDRAISING PROFESSIONALS, TEXAS NONPROFIT ORGANIZATION,AND GUIDESTAR. SALARIES ARE DETERMINED BY COMPARING NONPROFIT SIZE AND OVERALL BUDGET AND EXPERIENCE OF THE INDIVIDUAL CANDIDATE.

THE FORM 990 IS AVAILABLE UPON FORMAL WRITTEN REQUEST TO OUR ADMINISTRATIVE OFFICES.

THE ORGANIZATION'S GOVERNING DOCUMENTS, INCLUDING IT'S CONFLICT OF INTEREST POLICY AND AUDITED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS, ARE AVAILABLE UPON FORMAL WRITTEN REQUEST TO OUR ADMINISTRATIVE OFFICES.

THE AUDITED FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ARE REVIEWED BY THE FINANCE COMMITTEE PRIOR TO PRESENTATION TO THE BOARD.

mainOrgs.name: Animas-La Plata Operation Maintenance And Replacement Associ
mainOrgs.missionSummary: Protect and operate Animas-La Plata Project assets efficiently for water management.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Water capture, storage, and release management.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Directly focuses on project water management and conservation.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Single clear mission of managing water resources for conservation.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: With nearly 15 years of operation, the organization manages $2.2M in revenue with strong program expense ratios, focusing on vital water resource stewardship.

mainOrgs.missionStatement: To protect, preserve, and operate all assets of the Animas-La Plata Project while capturing, storing, and releasing the Project water supply in the most efficient and cost effective manner for the members of the Association.
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
J. Andrew Green & Associates, Inc. provided governmental affairs services by monitoring and providing information regarding state legislative activities that could have an impact on the operations, maintenance, and repair of the Animas-La Plata Project

The Association is exempt from income tax as provided under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code, except on net income derived from unrelated business activities. The Association adopted accounting requirements that prescribe when to recognize and how to measure the financial statement effects of income tax positions taken or expected to be taken on its income tax returns, including the position that the Association continues to qualify to be treated as a tax-exempt Association for both federal and state income tax purposes. These rules require management to evaluate the likelihood that, upon examination by relevant taxing jurisdictions, those income tax positions would be sustained. Based on that evaluation, if it were more than 50% probable that a material amount of income tax would be imposed at the entity level upon examination by the relevant taxing authorities, a liability would be recognized in the accompanying statement of financial position along with any interest and penalties that would result from that assessment. If the Association has unrelated business income, the federal Exempt Association Business Income Tax Return (Form 990T) would be subject to examination by the Internal Revenue Service for three years after filing. Should any penalties and interest be incurred, they would be recognized as management and general expenses. Based on the results of management's evaluation, the requirements did not have a material effect on the Association's financial statements. Accordingly, no liability is recognized in the accompanying statement of financial position for uncertain income tax positions.

Compensation of the general manager is determined using comparable data from member organizations, which is then reviewed and approved by the board annually.

Section A. Governing Body and Management, Line 1b Although each member derives a benefit from the Association as a result of the use of project water, the Association was formed to ease the burden on the federal government and each member entity. Additionally, each member has an equal vote.

Each member of the Association has an independent federal contract with the Bureau of Reclamation for the development, operation, maintenance and use of the project water. The members of the Association with current voting capacity are the City of Durango, La Plata Conservancy District, Navajo Nation, San Juan Water Commission, Southern Ute Indian Tribe, State of Colorado, and Ute Mountain Ute Tribe.

The Board includes a representative from each Member Organization. When terms come due, the Board approves another representative from the Member Organization to take over the position for a set term

A draft of the Form 990 is presented to the board for review and approval prior to signing and filing the return.

Conflicts of interest are discussed at board meetings as necessary. Additionally, each member signs a statement disclosing any potential conflicts of interest on an annual basis.

Compensation of the general manager is determined using comparable data from member organizations, which is then reviewed and approved by the board annually.

The Association's governing documents, policies and financial statements are available upon request.

The Organization adopted the provisions of FASB Accounting Standards Codification (ASC) 740-10, Accounting for Uncertainty in Income Taxes, on July 1, 2009, which had no significant financial statement impact to the Organization. The Organization recognizes the tax benefit from uncertain tax positions only if it is more likely than not that the tax position will be sustained on examination by the taxing authorities, based on the technical merits of the position. The tax benefit is measured based on the largest benefit that has a greater than 50% likelihood of being realized upon ultimate settlement. The Organization's income tax returns are subject to possible examination by the taxing authorities until the expiration of the related statutes of limitations. The Organization would recognize interest and penalties related to income tax matters in operating expenses. Management has concluded that there were no uncertain tax positions as of June 30, 2024. The Organization is no longer subject to income tax examinations by tax authorities for the years ending prior to June 30, 2021.

mainOrgs.name: California Ocean Science Tr
mainOrgs.missionSummary: Accelerating progress toward a healthy and productive ocean future for California.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Solutions-oriented science, graduate education, and science advice to policymakers.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Focuses on ocean management with significant water implications.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Involves multiple related activities aimed at ocean science and policy.
mainOrgs.notableSignals: Created by state legislation, strong connections with California government agencies.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: With over 20 years of experience, OST has a solid financial standing with a high current ratio and strong revenue growth, enabling it to effectively pursue its mission in ocean science and management.
mainOrgs.websiteRecentActivity: No recent activity visible on the website since the last tax year.
mainOrgs.softFlags: Revenue concentrated in Contributions (77%); Program expense ratio below tier threshold (75%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: CALIFORNIA OCEAN SCIENCE TRUST (OST) IS A NON-PROFIT ORGANIZATION DEDICATED TO ACCELERATING PROGRESS TOWARDS A HEALTHY AND PRODUCTIVE OCEAN FUTURE FOR CALIFORNIA. CREATED BY STATE LEGISLATION, OST BRIDGES THE GAP BETWEEN CUTTING-EDGE SCIENTIFIC RESEARCH AND SOUND OCEAN MANAGEMENT. OST DELIVERS ON THIS MANDATE BY DRAWING RESOURCES TO SOLUTIONS-ORIENTED SCIENCE, SUPPORTING GRADUATE EDUCATION TO FOSTER THE SCIENCE-POLICY WORKFORCE OF TOMORROW, AND DELIVERING PRAGMATIC, SALIENT SCIENCE ADVISE TO CALIFORNIA POLICYMAKERS, REGULATORS, AND MANAGERS.
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
LOBBYING EXPENSES RELATED TO SUPPORTING LEGISLATION THAT CLARIFIES THE ABILITY OF THE OCEAN SCIENCE TRUST TO PROVIDE SERVICES TO AGENCIES AND DEPARTMENTS IN CALIFORNIA STATE GOVERNMENT. THIS INCLUDED SHEPHERDING THE LEGISLATION THROUGH THE LEGISLATURE AND WITH THE GOVERNOR'S OFFICE.

THE SECRETARY OF THE CALIFORNIA ENVIRONMENTAL PROTECTION AGENCY, THE SECRETARY OF THE CALIFORNIA NATURAL RESOURCES AGENCY AND THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA DIRECTOR OF FINANCE EACH APPOINT ONE TRUSTEE. THE NATURAL RESOURCES SECRETARY RECEIVES NOMINATIONS FOR THE REMAINING SEVEN TRUSTEES AND APPOINTS THE TRUSTEES AS FOLLOWS: THREE TRUSTEES REPRESENTING THE CALIFORNIA STATE UNIVERSITY AND THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA; TWO TRUSTEES REPRESENTING OCEAN AND COASTAL INTEREST GROUPS OF THE STATE; AND TWO TRUSTEES REPRESENTING THE GENERAL PUBLIC.

OST DOES NOT HAVE COMMITTEES WITH THE AUTHORITY TO ACT ON BEHALF OF THE BOARD.

THE EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR AND BOARD FINANCE SUBCOMMITTEE REVIEWED THE FORM 990 BEFORE IT WAS FILED WITH THE IRS; A COPY OF THE FILED 990 WILL BE PRESENTED AT THE NEXT FULL BOARD MEETING.

CALIFORNIA OCEAN SCIENCE TRUST GOVERNANCE DOCUMENTS INCLUDE A CONFLICT OF INTEREST POLICY REQUIRING ALL MEMBERS OF THE BOARD OF TRUSTEES TO DISCLOSE ANY AFFILIATIONS WITH OUTSIDE ENTITIES THAT HAVE RECEIVED, CURRENTLY RECEIVE, OR HAVE PLANS OR ASPIRATIONS TO RECEIVE FUNDING FROM THE TRUST; SUCH MEMBERS ARE REQUIRED TO RECUSE THEMSELVES FROM PARTICIPATING IN DECISION-MAKING THAT IMPACTS THEIR AFFILIATED ENTITIES. THIS POLICY WILL CONTINUE TO BE ENFORCED IN THE CURRENT YEAR AND IN FUTURE YEARS. IN ADDITION, ALL MEMBERS OF THE OST BOARD OF TRUSTEES ANNUALLY FILE A CALIFORNIA FORM 700 STATEMENT OF ECONOMIC INTERESTS, WHICH INCLUDES DISCLOSURES OF ALL BUSINESS INTERESTS, INVESTMENTS, INCOME, PROPERTY, AND/OR GIFTS.

A FORMAL EVALUATION OF THE EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR MUST TAKE PLACE AT LEAST EVERY TWO YEARS, AND A SHORTER NARRATIVE EVALUATION MAY BE UNDERTAKEN IN BETWEEN. THE BOARD WILL SEEK THE INPUT OF THE EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR ON MATTERS OF PERFORMANCE AND COMPENSATION.THE BOARD IS RESPONSIBLE FOR DETERMINING AND ADJUSTING THE EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR'S COMPENSATION PACKAGE, AND WILL REVIEW THE COMPENSATION (SALARY AND BENEFITS) AND AVAILABLE BUDGET UPON COMPLETION OF EACH FORMAL PERFORMANCE REVIEW. THE BOARD OF TRUSTEES, WHO ARE VOLUNTEERS AND NOT COMPENSATED BY OST, WILL OPERATE INDEPENDENTLY, AND NO MEMBER OF THE BOARD WILL BE A STAFF MEMBER, THE RELATIVE OF A STAFF MEMBER, OR HAVE ANY RELATIONSHIP WITH STAFF THAT COULD PRESENT A CONFLICT OF INTEREST. THE REVIEW WILL INVOLVE A COMPARISON OF THE ED'S SALARY AND BENEFITS WITH OTHERS IN COMPARABLE POSITIONS AND COMPARABLE ORGANIZATIONS. THE ED'S ASSISTANCE MAY BE ENLISTED IN DETERMINING WHAT COMPARISON DATA WILL BE USED, AND THIS DATA MAY INCLUDE ANY OF THE FOLLOWING: - SALARY AND BENEFIT COMPENSATION STUDIES BY INDEPENDENT SOURCES; - WRITTEN JOB OFFERS FOR POSITIONS AT SIMILAR ORGANIZATIONS; - DOCUMENTED TELEPHONE CALLS ABOUT SIMILAR POSITIONS AT BOTH NONPROFIT AND FOR-PROFIT ORGANIZATIONS; AND - INFORMATION OBTAINED FROM THE IRS FORM 990 FILINGS OF SIMILAR ORGANIZATIONS.

THE ORGANIZATIONS' GOVERNING DOCUMENTS, CONFLICT OF INTEREST POLICY, AND FINANCIAL STATEMENTS ARE AVAILABLE TO THE PUBLIC UPON REQUEST.

PROFESSIONAL FEES - OTHER: PROGRAM SERVICE EXPENSES 313,848. MANAGEMENT AND GENERAL EXPENSES 6,797. FUNDRAISING EXPENSES 5,699. TOTAL EXPENSES 326,344.

THE BOARD AUDIT & FINANCE SUBCOMMITTEE ASSUMES RESPONSIBILITY FOR OVERSIGHT OF THE AUDIT AND SELECTION OF AN INDEPENDENT ACCOUNTANT. THIS COMMITTEE MEETS 2-4 TIMES ANNUALLY, AND REVIEWS BUDGETS, FINANCIAL STATEMENTS, TAX RETURNS, ETC., PRIOR TO THEIR PRESENTATION TO THE FULL BOARD.

FOR THE YEAR ENDED DECEMBER 31, 2024, MANAGEMENT OF THE CENTER IS NOT AWARE OF ANY MATERIAL UNCERTAIN TAX POSITIONS. ALL TAX EXEMPT ENTITIES ARE SUBJECT TO REVIEW AND AUDIT BY FEDERAL, STATE AND OTHER APPLICABLE AGENCIES. SUCH AGENCIES MAY REVIEW THE TAXABILITY OF UNRELATED BUSINESS INCOME, OR THE QUALIFICATION OF THE TAX-EXEMPT ENTITY UNDER THE INTERNAL REVENUE CODE AND APPLICABLE STATE STATUES. FOR FEDERAL TAX PURPOSES, THE TAX RETURNS REMAIN OPEN FOR POSSIBLE EXAMINATION FOR A PERIOD OF THREE YEARS AFTER THE RESPECTIVE FILING DEADLINES OF THOSE RETURNS.
