The Water Conservation Garden has nearly five acres of displays that showcase water conservation through a series of beautiful themed gardens, such as native plant garden and a vegetable garden, as well as how-to displays such as mulch and irrigation exhibits. The Garden can be viewed on a self-guided tour or through one of our programs. Additional programs include:- Four to Six classes per month for the general public, including landscaping, gardening, and art offerings. - Training in water conservation for professional landscapers.- School tours and education programs.- Regularly-scheduled adult group tours. - Large educational special events.- Outreach programs, including a speaker's bureau. The Garden can be a learning laboratory for the dedicated gradener, a delightful place for an afternoon stroll, or a place to gather lots of information about water-wise, or California-Friendly Garnening, with attending a fun family festival.

Copy of 990 provided to all Board Members to review prior to submitting form to IRS.

The Board reviews the Conflict of Interest Policy annually and all Board Members must disclose any conflicts immediately to the Board.

CEO & Top ManagementCompensation of all organization employees, other than Executive Director, is determined by the Executive Director with the Endoresment of the Board of Directors via approval of annual budget. Compensation for Executive Director established by Board of Directors. Members of the Board of Directors are volunteers and, hence, are not compensated.

Officers & Key EmployeesSEE 15A

AvailableUPON REQUEST

Items expensed in prior years for classroom built at the Garden were capitalized during the fiscal year that ended June 30, 2022. Proper allocations were not done by prior accountants as items were fulfilled under grants which mostly had items expensed for program purposes. The amount put into Fixed Assets during the current fiscal year were noted as the organization updated the financial software. Also note that the organization's operations were reduced during Covid which gave rise to coding discrepencies.

mainOrgs.softFlags: Revenue concentrated in Contributions (96%); Program expense ratio below tier threshold (73%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: THE SNAKE RIVER FUND IS A 501(C)3 NONPROFIT DEDICATED TO PROMOTING STEWARDSHIP OF AND PUBLIC ACCESS TO THE SNAKE RIVER WATERSHED IN WYOMING, WITH AN EMPHASIS ON PARTNERSHIPS, EDUCATION AND PUBLIC OUTREACH.
mainOrgs.program2Desc: WATERSHED/EDUCATION: THE UPPER SNAKE RIVER WATERSHED IS PRISTINE, BUT SEES HEAVY USE ON MANY REACHES. THE SRF PARTICIPATES IN OPPORTUNITIES TO FORMALIZE PROTECTIVE MEASURES, PARTICIPATE IN RESTORATION PROJECTS AND EDUCATE AND INFORM THE GENERAL PUBLIC REGARDING CURRENT ISSUES, ECOLOGY, USE PATTERNS, AND INFRASTRUCTURE. SRF COLLABORATES WITH OTHER NGOs AND LAND MGMT AGENCIES TO PROVIDE EDUCATIONAL OPPORTUNITIES FOR VISITORS AND LOCALS EACH YEAR. SRF WORKS WITH PARTNERS TO GET UNDERSERVED MEMBERS OF OUR COMMUNITY FROM ALL WALKS OF LIFE ON TO THE WATER TO PROMOTE THE CONCEPT OF OUTREACH, EDUCATION, WATERSHED MANAGEMENT AND RECREATIONAL OPPORTUNITIES FOR ALL.
mainOrgs.program3Desc: PUBLIC LAND TRANSFERS: THE SNAKE RIVER FUND IS COLLABORATING WITH TETON CO AND OTHERS IN THE TRANSFER PROCESS OF MORE THAN 1000 ACRES OF RIPARIAN HABITAT FROM THE BLM TO TETON COUNTY ALONG THE SNAKE RIVER CORRIDOR FROM MOOSE TO SOUTH PARK. THE FUND WILL ENSURE THAT THE PARCELS REMAIN IN PUBLIC OWNERSHIP FOR USE AS PUBLIC ACCESS, RECREATION, WILDLIFE HABITAT AND OPEN SPACE. WE ARE PLAYING A KEY ROLE IN THE DEVELOPMENT OF FACILITIES AND ADOPTION OF COUNTY MGMT OF THE WILSON TO S. PARK REACH OF THE RIVER.

*    

**SNAKE RIVER FUND**

### Protecting and promoting the Snake River for the benefit of present and future generations.

![Image 1: Old Bills Fun Run](https://snakeriverfund.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/08/Old-Bills-Fun-Run.jpg)

**GIVING SEASON ENDS SEPTEMBER 12TH!**

### Ensuring Our Watershed’s Ecological Integrity

The Snake River Fund has a long history of leading conservation efforts throughout our watershed, including:

*   The annual Spring Sweep cleanup on the Snake River
*   Partner-led stream restoration on numerous tributaries
*   Water quality monitoring on the Hoback River
*   Advocacy for responsible flow management at Jackson Lake Dam

Stewardship is a year-round responsibility, and we can only accomplish this work with your support. Join us this Giving Season by contributing through Old Bills! Giving Season ends September 12th.

![Image 2: Snake River](https://snakeriverfund.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/05/Snake-River.webp)

**OUR MISSION**

### Promoting stewardship of and public access to the Snake River watershed in Wyoming.

We place a strong emphasis on partnerships, education, and public outreach.

Since 1999, the Snake River Fund has been Northwest Wyoming’s leading organization advocating for recreational access to and stewardship of the Snake River Headwaters.

**OUR PRIORITIES**

### Our organization seeks to fun

![Image 1](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/6547d9cf47942756472ff251/927f1d62-de7e-47a0-8879-6b4acfc626f9/main_hero_2.jpg)

## **_Environmental_**_,_**_Economic_**

## _and_**_Social Well-Being_**_._

Groundwork San Diego stands in solidarity with the Kumeyaay people, who have understood and cared for this land since time immemorial. We recognize that we stand upon land that carries the footsteps of **Indigenous Kumeyaay people** across generations. The Kumeyaay have long lived in relationship with this place, guided by traditions that honor the balance of earth and sky, and the interconnectedness of all living beings. We honor and pay respect to Kumeyaay elders and descendants, past, present, and emerging as they continue their stewardship of these lands and waters. 
**_With gratitude, we acknowledge the privilege and opportunity to live, learn, and work on their ancestral homelands._**

mainOrgs.missionSummary: Protects, preserves, and restores natural resources in the West Atlanta Watershed.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Community-led environmental stewardship projects and training programs.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Core focus on watershed protection and environmental justice.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Focus on environmental stewardship and community training.
mainOrgs.notableSignals: Engages multiple grassroots partners for community-based solutions.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: With 9.9 years active and $1.1M revenue, WAWA demonstrates strong growth and a 0.9% program expense ratio while effectively engaging local communities.
mainOrgs.websiteRecentActivity: Upcoming events include WAWA Wednesdays Homeschool Program and Open House: Outdoor Activity Center.
mainOrgs.softFlags: Revenue concentrated in Contributions (97%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: The mission of the West Atlanta Watershed Alliance is to improve the quality of life within the West Atlanta Watershed by protecting, preserving, and restoring our communitys natural resources.
mainOrgs.program2Desc: In partnership with grassroots community-based partners, environmental non-profit organizations, and local collegiate partners such as the Proctor Creek Stewardship Council, Green Team of English Avenue, Cascade Springs Nature Conservancy, Hunter Hills, the Mozeley Park Neighborhood Association, the Student Conservation Association, and Spelman and Morehouse Colleges, WAWA led 25 nature-based, environmental stewardship volunteer projects helping to sustain the 22-acre old growth urban forest at the OAC and other West Atlanta parks and greenspaces in the Proctor, Utoy, and Sandy Creek Watersheds. With these efforts, we cleared over 17,000 sq. ft. of nonnative species and engaged over 500 volunteers to care for the native species, food, and wildlife at the OAC. In addition to the OAC, we cleared non-native species, cleaned West Atlanta waterways, and mapped urban heat islands across West Atlanta neighborhoods through additional volunteers in over 2,170 hours of service, and contributing over $72,471 in volunteer labour to steward the City of Atlantas parks and greenspaces and to advance local climate resilience initiatives.
mainOrgs.program3Desc: WAWA invested in community-led solutions to environmental challenges in urban watershed by co-facilitating the training of 10, Metropolitan Atlanta-based community leaders through the Atlanta Watershed Learning Network (AWLN) and supporting their initiation of community-based Champions projects to address watershed protection, equitable development, and environmental justice issues in their communities. WAWA also helped to grow and distributed 994 pounds of fresh produce through a community-based mutual aid effort; conducted on-site torus at the Hartnett Community Garden at the OAC and led educational programming on local food sovereignty efforts; hosted a capacity-building workshop for 40 community based organizations to learn grant-writing,environmental mapping, and coalition-building; and led place-keeping efforts through our OAC/Bush Mountain Oral History Project.

The conflict is then addressed/eliminated which can include removal from the Board of Directors. To date, no such actions have had to be taken.

The organization made governing documents, conflict of interest policy, and financial statements available to the public during the tax year upon request at its physical location: 1442 Richland Rd. SW, Atlanta, GA 30310.

mainOrgs.scheduleO:
THE EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR AND THE ORGANIZATION ACCOUNTANT REVIEWS THE DRAFT OF FORM 990 BEFORE FILING.

Reef Check Foundation has established a Conflict of Interest Policy to ensure that the organizations decision-making processes remain transparent, impartial, and free from personal or financial influence. This policy requires all board members, officers, and key employees to act in the best interests of the organization while performing their duties and to disclose any relationships or activities that could pose a potential conflict.Each board member and officer is required to review and sign the policy annually, acknowledging their responsibility to disclose any potential conflicts. If a potential conflict arises, the individual must fully disclose it to the board, which will evaluate the situation to determine whether a conflict exists and take appropriate action to manage or resolve it. This helps maintain trust and integrity within the organization and ensures compliance with federal and state regulations.The policy outlines procedures for identifying and addressing conflicts, maintaining records of disclosures, and implementing measures to mitigate conflicts to safeguard the organizations mission and operations.

The process for determining the compensation of the organization's top management officials includes a review and approval by independent members of the governing body. This process involves obtaining and considering compensation data for similar positions in comparable organizations and ensuring the decision-making process is documented. The organization conducts periodic compensation studies to benchmark salaries against industry standards and ensure reasonable and fair compensation practices.

THE ORGANIZATION MAKES ITS GOVERNING DOCUMENTS, CONFLICT OF INTEREST POLICY, FINANCIAL STATEMENTS AND INFORMATIONAL RETURNS AVAILABLE UPON WRITTEN REQUEST. THE INFORMATIONAL RETURNS ARE ALSO MADE AVAILABLE TO THE PUBLIC THROUGH WWW.GUIDESTAR.ORG, A PUBLIC WEBSITE.

mainOrgs.name: Hawaii Rural Water Association
mainOrgs.missionSummary: Enhancing life quality in rural Hawaii through training and technical assistance for water utilities.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Wastewater Technical Assistance and Source Water Protection Program.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Focuses on water quality, treatment, and conservation in Hawaii.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Provides training and support through two key programs related to water and wastewater.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: Established seven years with $1.3M revenue. High current ratio indicates strong financial health despite limited unrestricted funds. Focused mission on water stewardship enhances community impact.
mainOrgs.websiteRecentActivity: Upcoming training classes on safety, wastewater management, and backflow prevention scheduled for April and May 2026.
mainOrgs.websiteConsistencySignal: Website emphasizes safety training and wildfire preparedness, 990 focuses on quality of life through technical assistance.
mainOrgs.softFlags: Days cash below tier threshold (36 < 60); Months unrestricted below threshold; Revenue concentrated in Contributions (86%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: TO ENHANCE THE QUALITY OF LIFE IN RURAL AND SMALL MUNICIPALITIES IN THE STATE OF HAWAII BY PROVIDING TRAINING AND TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO NON- PROFIT PUBLIC WATER, WASTE AND LIKE UTILITIES.
mainOrgs.program2Desc: THE WASTEWATER TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE AND TRAINING PROGRAM PROVIDES FULL-TIME WASTEWATER TECHNICIAN ASSISTANCE/TRAINING. THESE TECHNICIANS PROVIDE TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE/TRAINING TO SYSTEM OPERATORS IN THE AREAS OF WATER AND WASTEWATER TREATMENT, FACILITY OPERATIONS/MAINTENANCE, SYSTEM FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT, REGULATORY COMPLIANCE, AND CONSERVATION ISSUES.
mainOrgs.program3Desc: THE SOURCE WATER PROTECTION PROJECT FACILITATES LOCAL SOURCE WATER PROTECTION TEAMS OF KEY PARTIES TO CREATE LOCAL SOURCE WATER PROTECTION PLANNING AND IMPLEMENTION OF VOLUNTARY PROTECTION MEASUIRES AT THE LOCAL LEVEL.
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
THE ASSOCIATION IS EXEMPT FROM FEDERAL INCOME TAXES UNDER SECTION 501(C)(3) OF THE INTERNAL REVENUE CODE AND IS CLASSIFIED AS AN ORGANIZATION THAT IS NOT A PRIVATE FOUNDATION AS DEFINED IN SECTION 509(A) OF THE INTERNAL REVENUE CODE. THE ASSOCIATION IS ALSO EXEMPT FROM STATE INCOME TAXES UNDER SECTION 235 OF THE HAWAII REVISED STATUTES. IN EVALUATING A TAX POSITION FOR RECOGNITION, THE ASSOCIATION EVALUATES WHETHER IT IS MORE-LIKELY-THAN-NOT THAT A TAX POSITION WILL BE SUSTAINED UPON EXAMINATION, INCLUDING RESOLUTION OF RELATED APPEALS OR LITIGATION PROCESSES, BASED ON THE TECHNICAL MERITS OF THE POSITION. IF THE TAX POSITION MEETS THE MORE-LIKELY-THAN-NOT RECOGNITION THRESHOLD, THE TAX POSITION IS MEASURED AND RECOGNIZED AS THE LARGEST AMOUNT OF TAX BENEFIT THAT, IN MANAGEMENT'S JUDGMENT, IS GREATER THAN 50% LIKELY OF BEING REALIZED UPON ULTIMATE SETTLEMENT. IT IS THE ASSOCIATION'S POLICY TO ACCRUE INTEREST RELATED TO ANY UNRECOGNIZED TAX BENEFITS, AS WELL AS ANY RELATED PENALTIES IN OTHER EXPENSES. THE ASSOCIATION DID NOT RECOGNIZE ANY INTEREST OR PENALTIES ASSOCIATED WITH UNRECOGNIZED TAX BENEFITS DURING THE YEARS ENDED DECEMBER 31, 2024 AND 2023. THE ASSOCIATION FILES INFORMATION RETURNS IN THE UNITED STATES (U.S) FEDERAL JURISDICTION. THE ASSOCIATION'S EVALUATION OF TAX POSITIONS WAS PERFORMED FOR THE YEAR ENDED DECEMBER 31, 2020 THROUGH 2023, FOR THE U.S. FEDERAL JURISDICTION, THE TAX YEARS THAT REMAIN SUBJECT TO EXAMINATION BY THE INTERNAL REVENUE SERVICE AS OF DECEMBER 31, 2024.

THE DRINKING WATER STATE REVOLVING FUND PROJECT IS TO DEVELOP AND IMPLEMENT A CONTINUING EDUCATION TRAINING PROGRAM FOR PUBLIC WATER SYSTEM OPERATIONS IN THE STATE OF HAWAII.

HAWAII RURAL WATER ASSOCIATION HAS THREE TYPES OF MEMBERSHIP. THE VOTING MEMBERS CONSIST OF DUES PAYING UTILITIES OWNING PUBLIC WATER AND/OR WASTEWATER FACILITIES. THE ASSOCIATE MEMBERS ARE ANY FIRM, CORPORATION, OR ORGANIZATION THAT PROVIDES GOODS, MATERIALS, PROFESSIONAL SERVICES, OR OTHER SERVICE TO WATER AND/OR WASTEWATER UTILITIES. THE INDIVIDUAL MEMBERS ARE PERSONS WHO ARE INVOLVED IN THE WATER AND/OR WASTEWATER UTILITIES.

MEMBERS AND ASSOCIATE MEMBERS ELECT AND REMOVE BOARD MEMBERS AS PER THE BYLAWS.

THE EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR REVIEWS THE TAX RETURN WITH THE BOARD PRESIDENT AND TREASURER FOR APPROVAL.

BOARD MEMBERS ARE REQUIRED TO SIGN AN ANNUAL STATEMENT TO CONFIRM COMPLIANCE WITH THE CONFLICT OF INTEREST POLICY. IF A CONFLICT ARISES, THE BOARD MEMBER IS REQUIRED TO RECUSE THEMSELVES.

THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS EVALUATE THE EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR EACH YEAR AND CONDUCT A REVIEW OF HIS/HER JOB AS COMPARED TO THE JOB DESCRIPTION OF THE EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR. ONCE THE EVALUATION AND REVIEW ARE COMPLETE, THE BOARD MAKES THE DETERMINATION OF WHETHER A RAISE IS EARNED. THEY ARE ALSO THE BODY THAT DETERMINES THE STARTING SALARY OF THE EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR.

THE ORGANIZATION MAKES ITS GOVERNING DOCUMENTS AND FINANCIAL STATEMENTS AVAILABLE TO THE PUBLIC DURING THE TAX YEAR BY REQUEST. IF, OR WHEN, ANY ONE MAY REQUEST ANY ONE OR ALL OF THEIR GOVERNING DOCUMENTS OR FINANCIAL STATEMENTS, THE ORGANIZATION WILL MAKE COPIES TO SUPPLY THE REQUEST.

mainOrgs.name: Willow Waterhole Greenspace Conservancy Inc
mainOrgs.missionSummary: Advocates for public greenspace improvements and nature preservation at Willow Waterhole Greenway.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Capital improvements for conservation and access across Willow Waterhole.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Focus on greenspace preservation with minimal direct water conservation emphasis.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Primarily focused on greenspace improvements with some public access initiatives.
mainOrgs.notableSignals: Long-standing community partnerships and extensive greenway development.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: With over 24 years of experience, the organization has built substantial cash reserves and community support, but faces a concerning revenue decline trend. Its mission focuses on enhancing public greenspace, which indirectly supports water conservation efforts.
mainOrgs.websiteRecentActivity: Spring newsletter discusses new community engagement events and outdoor activities planned for the upcoming season.
mainOrgs.softFlags: Months unrestricted below threshold; Declining revenue trend (-26% over 3 yrs); Revenue concentrated in Contributions (78%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: The Willow Waterhole Greenspace Conservancy ("Willow Waterhole") serves as the leading advocate for public greenspace improvements and nature preservation at the 291-acre Willow Waterhole Greenway. (See Schedule O)Willow Waterhole partners with community residents, governmental entities, and environmental organizations to develop amenities for hiking, biking, picnicking, bird watching, fishing, and canoeing/kayaking and to preserve native prairies, forests, and wetlands.
mainOrgs.program2Desc: Capital Improvements: Capital improvements for the Willow Waterhole Greenspace Conservancy include projects that enhance both conservation and public access across the park's six lakes and surrounding habitats. These efforts encompass the construction of new paved walking and biking trails that connect key areas of the greenway, along with the addition of amenities such as parking lots, picnic areas, fishing piers, way-finding signage, benches, and trailheads to support visitor use and enjoyment. They also include land acquisition and expansion of the greenway to protect natural habitats, increase flood control capacity, and preserve native prairie reserves. These infrastructure improvements restore and sustain the ecological health of environment while providing meaningful public access to nature.
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
A preliminary draft of the Form 990 is prepared by independent CPA. This draft is reviewed by the Finance Committee Chair and all necessary changes are then made. A review draft is distributed to all members of the Board of Directors with a three (3) day review period. A majority of Board members must vote to approve and submit.

All budgeted transactions are reviewed by the Board President and Treasurer for potential conflicts of interest. If a conflict is identified in a policy matter, that Board member would be directed to abstain from voting.

The Executive Director's compensation is established at levels comparable to similar organizations in the Houston area and is subject to review and approval by the Board of Directors.

The organization's audited financial statements are made publicly available on its website.

mainOrgs.websiteTextExcerpt:
Title: CREEJ

URL Source: https://www.creej.org/

Published Time: Wed, 22 Apr 2026 21:00:18 GMT

Markdown Content:
**The Challenge at Hand**

## Functional sanitation and clean water are human rights—not privileges.

Failing sanitation plagues communities across the U.S., forcing them to contend with polluted water and chronic health threats that result from living around sewage. These challenges—impacting both rural and urban communities alike—often go unseen, but their consequences are urgent and real.

At CREEJ, we refuse to accept this as normal. Every community deserves to thrive in a safe, healthy and clean environment.

![Image 1: Aerial view of wastewater treatment ponds and construction equipment in Lowndes County, Alabama](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/69caa768ac23a52855056ce3/69cac76fd234b07a5b7c19dd_Placeholder%20Image.png)

## National and International Headlines

We’ve brought sanitation inequality to national headlines and effected real change, but there’s still work to be done.

[![Image 2: Catherine walking with President Biden](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/69caa768ac23a52855056ce3/69cac7a9a96e1ccedaa01d83_71c100881ae9c08b8c3bb3c7c38b3aff_Placeholder%20Image%201.png)](https://www.creej.org/#)[![Image 3: Catherine on TIME 100 show](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/69caa768ac23a52855056ce3/69cac7a947ac9137f68ed8f5_49ed187bbc4f2666740eca46a3cf8ece_Placeholder%20Image%203.png)](https://www.creej.org/#)[![Image 4: Catherine at a book signing](https://

mainOrgs.missionStatement: SEE SCHEDULE ORESTORE THE DELTA IS AN INDEPENDENT PUBLIC BENEFIT CORPORATION OPERATING WITH THE GOAL OF MAKING THE SACRAMENTO-SANJOAQUIN DELTA ("DELTA") FISHABLE, SWIMMABLE, DRINKABLE AND FARMABLE FOR THE BENEFIT OF ALL CALIFORNIANS. WE INTEND TO ACCOMPLISH THIS THROUGH PUBLIC EDUCATION, RESEARCH, COMMUNITY ORGANIZATION, MONITORING POLICIES AND PROGRAMS CREATED BY GOVERNMENT AGENCIES AND REPORTING ON A PUBLIC AND PRIVATE ACTIVITIES THAT IMPACT THE DELTA. OUR PURPOSE IS TO ENSURE THAT THE STORY OF HUMAN AND NATURAL DELTA COMMUNITIES IS TOLD AND TO INCREASE THE VISIBILITY OF THE DELTA AS A UNIQUE PLACE WORTHY OF SOCIAL AND CULTURAL AS WELL AS ENVIRONMENTAL PROTECTION.

Title: Restore the Delta – Save the SF Bay-Delta Estuary for our future.

URL Source: https://restorethedelta.org/

Markdown Content:
![Image 1](https://restorethedelta.org/wp-content/uploads/Restore-the-Delta-Website-Banners-1920-x-720-px-1920-x-900-px-14.png)

Restore the Delta Website Banners (1920 x 720 px) (1920 x 900 px) (12)

![Image 2: previous arrow](blob:http://localhost/4fdaa2afb3ece9cbc604b142bf6ade92)

![Image 3: next arrow](blob:http://localhost/20afc0d31e0222a1c6b0c388e5f00aae)

### OUR MISSION IS TO ...

...ensure the health of the San Francisco Bay-Delta estuary and Delta communities.

### What do we do?

We advocate for a Delta with waters that are fishable, swimmable, and drinkable as described in the Clean Water Act in order to support the health of the estuary, San Francisco Bay, and the ocean beyond.

### What is the overall goal?

We envision the Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta as a place where a vibrant local economy, tourism, recreation, farming, wildlife, and fisheries thrive as a result of resident efforts to protect our waterways. We seek water quality protections for all communities and community protections from flood and drought impacts.

Title: The Reef Institute

URL Source: https://www.reefinstitute.org/

Markdown Content:
Scroll

## **Dedicated to coral conservation through education, research, and restoration.**

00:00

03:02

00:00

## **OUR IMPACT**

Our bold vision is to save the ocean. This starts and ends with coral, relying on partnership and collaboration. We aim to transition our education systems from mere awareness of ocean challenges to fostering active stewardship of our reefs. The Reef Institute focuses on biobanking corals, promoting propagation, facilitating larval settlement, juvenile rearing, and outplanting corals. We are dedicated to rebuilding the reef, beginning in Palm Beach County, extending throughout Florida, and collaborating across the Caribbean.

![Image 1](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/5ffdee34dded0c5b6608704c/1698357080546-VPMGGT980PE6FMLCTXPI/Education.png)

### [**EDUCATION**](https://www.reefinstitute.org/educationoptions)

C&C OF HONOLULU FEE 65,223

C&C HONOLULU FEE 65,223

THE WAIKIKI BEACH SPECIAL IMPROVEMENT DISTRICT IS DEDICATED TO CARRYING OUT AND EXECUTING THE RESPONSIBILITIES AND ACTIVITIES PRESCRIBED IN THE WAIKIKI BEACH SPECIAL IMPROVEMENT DISTRICT PLAN FOR THE CITY AND COUNTY OF HONOLULU SPECIAL IMPROVEMENT DISTRICT NO. 3, ESTABLISHED BY ORDINANCE OF THE CITY COUNCIL, CITY AND COUNTY OF HONOLULU, AND PREPARED AND SPONSORED BY THE WAIKIKI IMPROVEMENT ASSOCIATION PURSUANT TO HAWAII REVISED STATUTE 46-80.5 AND REVISED ORDINANCES OF HONOLULU 1990, CHAPTER 36; TO RELIEVE THE BURDENS OF GOVERNMENT AND ENHANCE THE QUALITY OF LIFE IN WAIKIKI; TO WORK IN PARTNERSHIP WITH GOVERNMENT AND THE PRIVATE SECTOR TO DEVELOP AND IMPLEMENT PROGRAMS THAT PROMOTE THE OVERALL VITALITY OF WAIKIKI BEACH AND THE STATE OF HAWAII BY STRENGTHENING THEIR ROLES ON BEHALF OF ALL RESIDENTS OF HAWAII AS A WORLD-CLASS RESORT DESTINATION FOR VISITORS; TO OPERATE EXCLUSIVELY FOR CHARITABLE, RELIGIOUS, LITERARY, EDUCATIONAL, AND SCIENTIFIC PURPOSES, WITHIN THE MEANING OF SECTION 501(C)(3) OF THE INTERNAL REVENUE CODE, INCLUDING FOR SUCH PURPOSES, THE MAKING OF DISTRIBUTIONS TO ORGANIZATIONS THAT QUALIFY AS TAX-EXEMPT ORGANIZATIONS UNDER SECTION 501(C)(3) OF THE INTERNAL REVENUE CODE OF 1986 (OR FUTURE CORRESPONDING PROVISIONS.)

THE ASSOCIATION IS EXEMPT FROM THE ANNUAL HAWAII CHARITY LAW FILING. THIS EXEMPTION WAS APPROVED BY THE STATE OF HAWAII ATTORNEY GENERAL'S OFFICE IN CHARGE OF COMPLIANCE.

THE ASSOCIATION SHALL HAVE THREE CLASSES OF MEMBERSHIP: (I) CLASS A MEMBERS SHALL CONSIST OF THE ASSESSED FEE SIMPLE OWNERS AND GROUND LESSEES OF RECORD OF THE REAL PROPERTY LOCATED WITHIN THE WAIKIKI BEACH SID, PROVIDED HOWEVER THAT CITY AND COUNTY OF HONOLULU SHALL NOT BE ELIGIBLE FOR CLASS A MEMBERSHIP; (II) CLASS B MEMBERS SHALL CONSIST OF THE COMMERCIAL TENANTS LEASING SPACE WITHIN THE WAIKIKI BEACH SID WHO HAVE APPLIED FOR AND BE ADMITTED TO MEMBERSHIP IN ACCORDANCE WITH THE PROCEDURES SET FORTH IN THE BYLAWS; AND (III) CLASS C MEMBERS SHALL CONSIST OF FEE OWNERS AND GROUND LESSEES OF RECORD OF THE REAL PROPERTY LOCATED WITHIN THE WAIKIKI SID WHO ARE EXEMPT FROM PAYING ASSESSMENTS UNDER THE BYLAWS, NONVOTING EX OFFICIO MEMBERS OF THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS, AND SUCH OTHER PERSONS HAVING AN INTEREST IN THE CHARITABLE PURPOSE OF THE CORPORATION (EACH OF WHOM, WHERE APPLICABLE, HAS APPLIED FOR AND BEEN ADMITTED TO MEMBERSHIP IN ACCORDANCE WITH THE PROCEDURES SET FORTH IN THE BYLAWS).

VOTING RIGHTS SHALL BE RESERVED TO THE CLASS A AND CLASS B MEMBERS ONLY AND SHALL BE WEIGHTED IN ACCORDANCE WITH THE BYLAWS. VOTING RIGHTS FOR EACH CLASS A MEMBER SHALL BE BASED ON THE AMOUNT OF ASSESSMENT PAID OR TO BE PAID BY THE CLASS A MEMBER UNDER THE PLAN. VOTING RIGHTS FOR EACH CLASS B MEMBER SHALL BE BASED ON THE AMOUNT OF SQUARE FEET OF NET RENTABLE COMMERCIAL SPACE LEASED BY THE CLASS B MEMBER IN THE WAIKIKI BEACH SID.

REVIEWED BY THE EXECUTIVE COMMITTEE BEFORE FILED WITH THE INTERNAL REVENUE SERVICE.

mainOrgs.program2Desc: PRODUCED WEBINARS FOR KNOWLEDGE SHARING. HOSTED ON-LINE WEB PLATFORM AND PUBLISHED NEW CONTENT INCLUDING CASE STUDIES OF TECHNOLOGY PILOTS AND PROFILES OF NEW INNOVATION PRIORITIES.
mainOrgs.program3Desc: HOSTED KNOWLEDGE SHARING SUMMIT THAT BROUGHT TOGETHER INDUSTRY WATER LEADERS TO DISCUSS PRIORITIES AND RESULTS OF PROGRAM INITATIVES.
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
PILOT PROJECTS ARE CONTINUALLY MONITORED AND THE END RESULTS ARE PUBLISHED AS A CASE STUDY FOR KNOWLEDGE SHARING. IN ADDITION, GRANTEES ARE SUBJECT TO REPORTING REQUIREMENTS PER THE AWARD CONTRACT.

EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION IS BENCHMARKED WITH A SALARY SURVEY PROVIDED BY OUR PEO PROVIDER AS WELL AS APPROVED BY THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS.

WATERSTART DIRECTORS APPROVE NOMINATIONS TO THE BOARD OF WATERSTART CHANNELS FOR INNOVATION.

WATERSTART DIRECTORS CAN HELP DIRECT DECISIONS FOR WATERSTART CHANNELS FOR INNOVATION.

A COMPLETE COPY OF FORM 990 IS PROVIDED TO ALL BOARD MEMBERS ELECTRONICALLY BEFORE FILING. THE 990 IS REVIEWED BY THE BOOKKEEPER AND APPROVED BY EITHER THE PRESIDENT OR VICE-PRESIDENT.

ANNUAL POLICY REMINDERS SENT TO APPROPRIATE PARTIES AS A REMINDER TO REVIEW AND COMPLY. IT IS THE EMPLOYEE/DIRECTOR'S RESPONSIBILITY TO REPORT ANY ACTUAL OR POTENTIAL CONFLICT THAT MAY EXIST BETWEEN THE EMPLOYEE (AND THE EMPLOYEE'S IMMEDIATE FAMILY) AND THE COMPANY, AT WHICH TIME MANAGEMENT RESERVES THE RIGHT TO DECIDE AS TO THE EXTENT OR EXISTENCE OF A POSSIBLE CONFLICT, AND THE MANNER TO WHICH SUCH A POTENTIAL CONFLICT SHALL BE ADDRESSED.

THE BOARD DOES RESEARCH ON SALARY AND BENCHMARKS FOR A PARTICULAR POSITION. THE DATA IS ANALYZED, REVIEWED, AND FINAL APPROVAL ON THE COMPENSATION IS DONE BY THE BOARD AND CONTEMPORANEOUSLY DOCUMENTED.

NO OTHER DOCUMENTS AVAILABLE TO THE PUBLIC.

mainOrgs.name: Friends Of The Yampa Inc
mainOrgs.missionSummary: Protect and enhance the Yampa River's environmental and recreational integrity through stewardship, advocacy, education, and partnerships.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Yampa River Stewardship Program and Yampa River Awareness Project.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Focused on river health and restoration, directly aligned with water conservation.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Two main programs addressing river stewardship and public awareness.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: With over 15 years of experience, Friends of the Yampa operates with a strong financial foundation, indicated by a 81.8% revenue trend and a current ratio of 8.0, ensuring capacity to further its river conservation mission.
mainOrgs.websiteRecentActivity: Upcoming Yampa River Cleanup and Yampa River Month events highlighted on the website.
mainOrgs.softFlags: Months unrestricted below threshold; Revenue concentrated in Contributions (88%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: OUR MISSION IS TO PROTECT AND ENHANCE THE ENVIRONMENTAL AND RECREATIONAL INTEGRITY OF THE YAMPA RIVER AND ITS TRIBUTARIES, THROUGH STEWARDSHIP, ADVOCACY, EDUCATION AND PARTNERSHIPS.
mainOrgs.program2Desc: THE YAMPA RIVER STEWARDSHIP PROGRAM (YSTEW) IS A PLANNING AND CAPACITY BUILDING PROGRAM LED BY FRIENDS OF THE YAMPA AND SUPPORTED BY THE YAMPA RIVER COLLABORATIVE. USING RIVER HEALTH DATA AND STAKEHOLDER INPUT, YSTEW IDENTIFIES SPECIFIC WAYS TO MOVE THE YAMPA TO A HEALTHIER STATE THROUGH EMPOWERING LANDOWNERS, LAND MANAGERS, AND PRACTITIONERS TO IMPLEMENT RIVER RESTORATION PROJECTS AND LAND MANAGEMENT PRACTICES THAT SUPPORT RIVER HEALTH. SPECIFICALLY, YSTEW DOES THIS BY: 1) ENGAGING STAKEHOLDERS TO DEFINE DESIRED FUTURE CONDITIONS AND DEVELOP STRATEGIES TO MOVE EXISTING CONDITIONS TOWARD THIS VISION, 2) IDENTIFYING COMMON BARRIERS TO IMPLEMENTING RIVER RESTORATION PROJECTS AND LAND MANAGEMENT PRACTICES, AND 3) PROVIDING LANDOWNERS, LAND MANAGERS, AND PRACTITIONERS WITH TRAINING ON HOW TO USE THE IDENTIFIED STRATEGIES, RESOURCES, AND SUPPORT TO REDUCE BARRIERS TO PROJECT IMPLEMENTATION.
mainOrgs.program3Desc: THE YAMPA RIVER AWARENESS PROJECT'S (YRAP) GOALS ARE TO: DOCUMENT THE UNIQUE RESOURCES AND VALUES OF THE WILD AND FREE FLOWING YAMPA RIVER. INFORM THE PUBLIC AND POLICY MAKERS ABOUT THESE VALUES AND WHAT COULD BE LOST IF A MAJOR DAM, DIVERSION OR DEWATERING PROJECT OCCURS ON THE YAMPA. INSPIRE PEOPLE TO PROTECT THE YAMPA RIVER FOR FUTURE GENERATIONS. YAMPA RIVER FESTIVAL WHICH RAISES AWARENESS OF THE YAMPA RIVER LOCATED IN COLORADO AND UTAH. THE EVENT COMBINES FEATURED SPEAKERS DISCUSSING THE IMPORTANCE OF THE YAMPA RIVER AS WELL AS RIVER RELATED COMPETETIVE EVENTS AND ACTIVITIES. YAMPA RIVER YOUTH CAMPS: THE MAIN GOAL OF THIS PROGRAM IS TO EMPOWER YOUTH TO TRAVEL SAFELY IN WHITEWATER RIVER CONDITIONS WHILE FALLING IN LOVE WITH ONE OF THE MOST IMPORTANT RESOURCES HERE IN THE YAMPA VALLEY. THIS CLASS WILL CREATE OPPORTUNITIES FOR THE YOUTH OF NORTHWEST COLORADO TO GET THEIR FOOT IN THE DOOR WITH NOT ONLY BEGINNER RIVER SKILLS BUT ALSO ON LOCAL RIVER KNOWLEDGE. THROUGH RIVER CONSERVATION AND ENVIRONMENTAL EDUCATION YOUTH WILL COME AWAY AS AGENTS OF CHANGE AND STEWARDS TO OUR LOCAL RIVER AND ADJACENT AGRICULTURAL, PUBLIC AND PRIVATE LANDS. PUBLIC RELATIONS DESIGNED TO RAISE AWARENESS OF THE IMPORTANCE OF THE YAMPA RIVER AND IT'S TRIBUTARIES.
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
THE YAMPA RIVER STEWARDSHIP PROGRAM (YSTEW) IS A PLANNING AND CAPACITY BUILDING PROGRAM LED BY FRIENDS OF THE YAMPA AND SUPPORTED BY THE YAMPA RIVER COLLABORATIVE. USING RIVER HEALTH DATA AND STAKEHOLDER INPUT, YSTEW IDENTIFIES SPECIFIC WAYS TO MOVE THE YAMPA TO A HEALTHIER STATE THROUGH EMPOWERING LANDOWNERS, LAND MANAGERS, AND PRACTITIONERS TO IMPLEMENT RIVER RESTORATION PROJECTS AND LAND MANAGEMENT PRACTICES THAT SUPPORT RIVER HEALTH. SPECIFICALLY, YSTEW DOES THIS BY: 1) ENGAGING STAKEHOLDERS TO DEFINE DESIRED FUTURE CONDITIONS AND DEVELOP STRATEGIES TO MOVE EXISTING CONDITIONS TOWARD THIS VISION, 2) IDENTIFYING COMMON BARRIERS TO IMPLEMENTING RIVER RESTORATION PROJECTS AND LAND MANAGEMENT PRACTICES, AND 3) PROVIDING LANDOWNERS, LAND MANAGERS, AND PRACTITIONERS WITH TRAINING ON HOW TO USE THE IDENTIFIED STRATEGIES, RESOURCES, AND SUPPORT TO REDUCE BARRIERS TO PROJECT IMPLEMENTATION.

THE YAMPA RIVER AWARENESS PROJECT'S (YRAP) GOALS ARE TO: DOCUMENT THE UNIQUE RESOURCES AND VALUES OF THE WILD AND FREE FLOWING YAMPA RIVER. INFORM THE PUBLIC AND POLICY MAKERS ABOUT THESE VALUES AND WHAT COULD BE LOST IF A MAJOR DAM, DIVERSION OR DEWATERING PROJECT OCCURS ON THE YAMPA. INSPIRE PEOPLE TO PROTECT THE YAMPA RIVER FOR FUTURE GENERATIONS. YAMPA RIVER FESTIVAL WHICH RAISES AWARENESS OF THE YAMPA RIVER LOCATED IN COLORADO AND UTAH. THE EVENT COMBINES FEATURED SPEAKERS DISCUSSING THE IMPORTANCE OF THE YAMPA RIVER AS WELL AS RIVER RELATED COMPETETIVE EVENTS AND ACTIVITIES. YAMPA RIVER YOUTH CAMPS: THE MAIN GOAL OF THIS PROGRAM IS TO EMPOWER YOUTH TO TRAVEL SAFELY IN WHITEWATER RIVER CONDITIONS WHILE FALLING IN LOVE WITH ONE OF THE MOST IMPORTANT RESOURCES HERE IN THE YAMPA VALLEY. THIS CLASS WILL CREATE OPPORTUNITIES FOR THE YOUTH OF NORTHWEST COLORADO TO GET THEIR FOOT IN THE DOOR WITH NOT ONLY BEGINNER RIVER SKILLS BUT ALSO ON LOCAL RIVER KNOWLEDGE. THROUGH RIVER CONSERVATION AND ENVIRONMENTAL EDUCATION YOUTH WILL COME AWAY AS AGENTS OF CHANGE AND STEWARDS TO OUR LOCAL RIVER AND ADJACENT AGRICULTURAL, PUBLIC AND PRIVATE LANDS. PUBLIC RELATIONS DESIGNED TO RAISE AWARENESS OF THE IMPORTANCE OF THE YAMPA RIVER AND IT'S TRIBUTARIES.

YAMPA RIVER SCORECARD PROJECT: THE YRSP IS A LONG-TERM RIVER HEALTH MONITORING AND EVALUATION PROGRAM FOR THE YAMPA BASIN THAT WILL RATE THE OVERALL CONDITION OF DIFFERENT SEGMENTS OF THE YAMPA RIVER AND ARTICULATE RESULTS THROUGH "SCORECARD". A COMPREHENSIVE LONG-TERM MONITORING PROGRAM HAS YET TO EXIST IN THE YAMPA BASIN.

FORM 990 IS REVIEWED BY THE TREASURER BEFORE IT IS SUBMITTED TO THE IRS.

BOARD MEMBERS ARE REQUIRED TO DISCLOSE ANY CONFLICT OF INTEREST ON AN ANNUAL BASIS.

BOARD APPROVES THE EXECUTIVE DIRECTORS SALARY ANNUALLY. SALARIES FOR KEY EMPLOYEES ARE COMPARED TO SIMILAR ORGANIZATIONS.

DOCUMENTS ARE MADE AVAILABLE UPON REASONABLE REQUEST.

mainOrgs.missionSummary: Focused on addressing water pollution and climate change through education and innovative projects.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Developing strategies for coastal marine ecosystems and river/lake conservation.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Strong environmental work with a significant water quality component.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Focuses on marine ecosystems, rivers, and climate issues, interconnected yet distinct.
mainOrgs.notableSignals: Utilizes art, science, culture, and design for sustainability initiatives.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: Blue Lab Preservation Society has been active for over six years with a significant focus on funding innovative environmental projects. However, it faces a declining revenue trend at -62.7%, which could impact future program effectiveness.
mainOrgs.softFlags: Revenue concentrated in Contributions (100%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: THE ORGANIZATION IS FOCUSED ON ADDRESSING WATER POLLUTION, CLIMATE CHANGE, AND ENVIRONMENTAL ISSUES. IT EMPLOYS ART, SCIENCE, CULTURE, AND DESIGN AS GALVANIZING CHANNELS FOR ITS SUSTAINABILITY PROGRAM. IT DEVELOPS RESPONSIVE STRATEGIES AND SOLUTIONS WITH A CORE MISSION TO EDUCATE AND INSPIRE GLOBAL ENVIRONMENTAL ACTION BY DIRECTING FUNDS RAISED THROUGH PHILANTHROPY TOWARD INNOVATIVE PROJECTS (DOMESTIC AND WORLDWIDE) THAT SEEK TO RESTORE, REINFORCE, AND PROTECT COASTAL MARINE ECOSYSTEMS, RIVERS, AND LAKES.
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
THE REEFLINE IS A NOT-FOR-PROFIT ORGANIZATION THAT IS EXEMPT FROM FEDERAL INCOME TAXES UNDER SECTION 501(C)(3) OF THE INTERNAL REVENUE CODE (THE "CODE"). UNDER THESE PROVISIONS, NO TAX IS PAID ON INCOME RECEIVED AS LONG AS THE REEFLINE MAINTAINS ITS TAX-EXEMPT STATUS. ACCORDINGLY, NO PROVISION FOR INCOME TAXES IS PROVIDED IN THE ACCOMPANYING FINANCIAL STATEMENTS. ALTHOUGH THE REEFLINE IS EXEMPT FROM INCOME TAX, IT MAY BE SUBJECT TO TAX ON INCOME UNRELATED TO ITS EXEMPT PURPOSE, UNLESS THAT INCOME IS OTHERWISE EXCLUDED UNDER THE CODE. AS OF DECEMBER 31, 2024, THE REEFLINE DETERMINED THAT THERE ARE NO SIGNIFICANT, UNCERTAIN TAX POSITIONS WITHIN ITS FINANCIAL STATEMENTS

PROCESS USED TO REVIEW THE FORM 990 BLUE LAB PRESERVATION SOCIETY'S FORM 990 WAS PREPARED BY BLUE LAB PRESERVATION SOCIETY'S EXTERNAL ACCOUNTANT, FRENDEL BROWN & WEISSMAN LLP, IN CONSULTATION WITH BLUE LAB PRESERVATION SOCIETY'S BOARD OF DIRECTORS. THE COMPLETED VERSION OF THE FORM 990 WAS PROVIDED TO THE BOARD FOR ITS REVIEW AND COMMENT. UPON APPROVAL BY THE BOARD, THE FORM 990 WAS FINALIZED AND FILED WITH THE IRS.

COMPLIANCE WITH CONFLICT OF INTEREST POLICY THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS OVERSEES THE SUBMISSION OF THE ANNUAL DISCLOSURES BY THE DIRECTORS, OFFICERS, TRUSTEES, AND KEY EMPLOYEES, REVIEWS THE DISCLOSURES TO DETERMINE WHETHER THERE ARE ANY CONFLICTS OF INTEREST, AND MONITORS OVERALL COMPLIANCE WITH THE POLICY. IF ANY ACTUAL OR POTENTIAL CONFLICT WERE TO ARISE, LEGAL COUNSEL WOULD WORK WITH THE BOARD TO FACILITATE THE FAIRNESS OF THE TRANSACTION, TO ENSURE THE RECUSAL OF ANY INTERESTED PARTIES FROM ANY DELIBERATIONS, AND OTHERWISE MONITOR COMPLIANCE WITH THE POLICY.

PROCESS FOR DETERMINING COMPENSATION DIRECTORS OF THE CORPORATION MAY RECEIVE REASONABLE COMPENSATION FOR THEIR SERVICES AS DIRECTORS, PROVIDED THAT SUCH COMPENSATION IS DETERMINED TO BE FAIR AND IN THE BEST INTEREST OF THE CORPORATION. THE AMOUNT OF COMPENSATION SHALL BE APPROVED BY A MAJORITY VOTE OF THE DISINTERESTED DIRECTORS. ALL COMPENSATION ARRANGEMENTS MUST COMPLY WITH THE CORPORATION'S CONFLICT OF INTEREST POLICY, WHICH REQUIRES DIRECTORS TO DISCLOSE ANY FINANCIAL INTEREST IN TRANSACTIONS INVOLVING THE CORPORATION. THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS SHALL ENSURE THAT ANY COMPENSATION PAID TO DIRECTORS IS REASONABLE AND DOES NOT EXCEED WHAT IS CUSTOMARY FOR SIMILAR SERVICES IN THE NONPROFIT SECTOR.

PROCESS FOR MAKING DOCUMENTS AVAILABLE TO THE PUBLIC BLUE LAB PRESERVATION SOCIETY MAKES ITS GOVERNING DOCUMENTS, CONFLICT OF INTEREST POLICY AND FINANCIAL STATEMENTS AVAILABLE TO THE PUBLIC ON REQUEST.

mainOrgs.name: Santa Barbara Channel Keeper
mainOrgs.missionSummary: Protect and restore the Santa Barbara Channel and its watersheds through advocacy, education, and community engagement.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Education, Community Engagement, and Citizen Enforcement of environmental laws.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Focuses primarily on watershed protection and water quality.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Engages in multiple related programs emphasizing education and community involvement.
mainOrgs.notableSignals: Engages 1,200 students and mobilizes community volunteers for environmental actions.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: With 24 years of experience, $958K in revenue, and strong community engagement, the organization effectively mobilizes volunteers for local watershed protection.
mainOrgs.websiteRecentActivity: Channelkeeper celebrates 25 years of protecting the Santa Barbara Channel with ongoing monitoring and educational programming.
mainOrgs.softFlags: Revenue concentrated in Contributions (97%); Program expense ratio below tier threshold (65%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: THE ORGANIZATION WAS ESTABLISHED TO PROTECT AND RESTORE THE SANTA BARBARA CHANNEL AND ITS WATERSHEDS THROUGH SCIENCE-BASED ADVOCACY, EDUCATION, FIELD WORK, COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT, AND CITIZEN ENFORCEMENT OF ENVIRONMENTAL LAWS.
mainOrgs.program2Desc: EDUCATION - THE ORGANIZATION INSPIRES LOCAL STUDENTS TO UNDERSTAND AND APPRECIATE THEIR LOCAL ENVIRONMENT THROUGH A COMBINATION OF INTERACTIVE CLASSROOM LESSONS AND OUTDOOR EDUCATIONAL EXPERIENCES. IN 2024, WE PROVIDED LESSONS AND/OR EXPERIENCES TO 1,200 LOCAL STUDENTS THROUGH OUR ENVIRONMENTAL SCIENCE EDUCATION PROGRAM. WE HOSTED OUR 21ST ANNUAL STUDENT ART SHOW ON THE THEME OF "WHAT THE CHANNEL MEANS TO ME." WE SUPPORTED SOUTHERN CALIFORNIA BASED RESEARCHERS EXAMINING THE IMPACT OF HARMFUL ALGAL BLOOMS BY COLLECTING PLANKTON AND BIOTOXIN SAMPLES IN THE CHANNEL
mainOrgs.program3Desc: COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT - THE ORGANIZATION MOTIVATES AND MOBILIZES COMMUNITY VOLUNTEERS TO TAKE SPECIFIC ACTIONS THAT PROTECT LOCAL CREEKS, BEACHES, COASTAL WATERS, AND THE CHANNEL ISLANDS. WE TRAINED 61 PROSPECTIVE VOLUNTEERS TO PARTICIPATE IN SHORE-BASED SURVEYS OF THREE LOCAL, COASTAL MARINE PROTECTED AREAS, TO SUPPLEMENT OUR 17 STAFF-CONDUCTED BOAT-BASED SURVEYS. VOLUNTEERS CONDUCTED 694 SHORE BASED SURVEYS. WE CARRIED OUT OUR WATERSHED BRIGADE PROGRAM WITH 446 VOLUNTEERS. THEY REMOVED MORE THAN 16,000 POUNDS OF TRASH AND MARINE DEBRIS IN CLEAN-UP EVENTS. WE WORKED WITH LOCAL FISHERMEN TO REMOVE 30 DERELICT LOBSTER TRAPS FROM BEACHES STRETCHING FROM SHORELINE PARK TO GOLETA BEACH. WE COORDINATED TWO CLEAN UPS AT THE CHANNEL ISLANDS, RESULTING IN THE REMOVAL OF MORE THAN 7,800 POUNDS OF TRASH.
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
THE BOARD REVIEWS THE DRAFT 990 PRIOR TO SUBMISSION TO IRS

AT BEGINNING OF EACH YEAR THE POLICY IS DISTRIBUTED TO ALL BOARD MEMBERS AND STAFF. THEY ARE REQUIRED TO SIGN THE CONFLICT OF INTEREST POLICY.

THE BOARD IS SENT A EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR PERFORMANCE EVALUATION DOCUMENT. BOARD MEMBERS FILL IT OUT AND SEND IT TO THE BOARD PRESIDENT, WHO COMPILES AND SUMMARIZES THE ANSWERS. THEN THE BOARD MEETS TO DISCUSS EVALUATION AND ESTABLISH THE EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR'S SALARY FOR THE UPCOMING YEAR.

UPON REQUEST

THERE WERE NO CHANGES TO THE OVERSIGHT OR SELECTION PROCESS.
mainOrgs.websiteTextExcerpt:
Title: Santa Barbara Channelkeeper

URL Source: https://www.sbck.org/

Published Time: Fri, 24 Apr 2026 13:34:42 GMT

Markdown Content:
### Education

Building environmental awareness and stewardship, changing attitudes and influencing the decisions of tomorrow by educating policy makers, students and the wider community about the biggest threats to the Santa Barbara Channel and its watersheds and solutions to address those threats.

![Image 1](https://www.sbck.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/11/Education_3.jpg)

### Field Work

Patrolling the Santa Barbara Channel and its tributaries to identify and deter pollution and respond to citizen reports of pollution. We conduct scientific research to investigate critical threats facing our waters, and collect and disseminate scientifically sound water quality data.

![Image 2](https://www.sbck.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/03/sbck-field-work-Ingra-and-Sayo.jpg)

### Advocacy

Representing our community’s interest in clean water by providing expert, science-based stakeholder advocacy and testifying before government officials to influence policies to better protect our waters and habitats.

![Image 3](https://www.sbck.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/11/Advocacy_resize.jpg)

### Enforcement

Ensuring that entities, both public and private, comply with environmental laws, and that government agencies enforce those laws. Channelkeeper occasionally uses citizen lawsuits to protect the public interest in clean water and healthy aquatic habitats.

!

mainOrgs.name: One People One Reef
mainOrgs.missionSummary: Protect critical marine habitats through innovative partnerships and capacity building.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Technical assistance for reef conservation and education outreach.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Core focus on marine conservation and habitat protection.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Two primary program areas centered on conservation and education.
mainOrgs.notableSignals: Strong community engagement through traditional knowledge integration.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: With 5.5 years active and innovative programs, this organization shows strength in community engagement and strong revenue growth, crucial for effective marine conservation efforts.
mainOrgs.softFlags: Months unrestricted below threshold; Revenue concentrated in Contributions (92%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: -To achieve effective and enduring protection of critical marine habitats and the people who rely on them.-To develop innovative & diverse partnerships that advance adaptive management and conservation in Micronesian outer islands.-To develop local capacity through a unique collaborative approach that weaves traditional knowledge & management practices together with modern science and technology
mainOrgs.program2Desc: Technical Assistance for Community-led Reef Conservation and ManagementLong-term goals: Support sustainable coastal resource management by integrating Western science with traditional and local knowledge systems, supporting communities to make informed decisions that align with their cultural values and environmental stewardship goals. 2024 summary of accomplishments:Provided technical support to communities in Ulithi Atoll to develop sustainable reef management plans integrating Western science with traditional knowledge. Conducted 5 community meetings, conducted 7 focus groups on ecological and cultural impacts of spearfishing, reported results of a well-being survey to 4 communities, and trained 15 youth leaders in documenting traditional management practices.
mainOrgs.program3Desc: Education and OutreachLong-term goals: Support the documentation and preservation of culture and traditional practices by creating media, art, and educational materials in collaboration with local communities to share with local communities, the general public, and policymakers. 2024 summary of accomplishments:Science Video: Captured interviews with a wide breadth of community members (ages 15 to 70+), local and western scientists, documented innovative community-led workshops, and live audio for a short science documentary highlighting cutting edge technologies and approaches of OPOR. Animations: Two animation videos, highlight Indigenous and modern knowledge systems?Video 1:.Two-Eyed Seeing: Harmonizing Indigenous and Western Knowledge in Ulithi Atoll (working title) ?Video 2: People and the Sea: The Crucial Role of Community-Centric MPA (working title) Music Album: 9 tracks recorded, thematically focusing on Ulithian history, culture, and environmental stewardship through non-colonial lens. Podcast: 25 interviews conducted, leading to 7 podcast episodes that explore topics ranging from cultural taboo and resource management to fishing techniques and climate changes?Podcast: Completed final editing and released all 7 podcast episodes, ensuring cultural accuracy and community approval, distributing it through digital platforms such as Apple podcasts and Google podcasts.?Music Album: Finalized album art, distribute it through digital platforms like iTunes, Spotify, and Google Play.?Science Video: Finalized post-production and publish video to OPOR website, YouTube and Vimeo. Shared out across social media platforms.?Animations: Completed post production of animation videos and prepared them for community approval and dissemination on OPOR website, YouTube and Vimeo. Shared out across social media platforms.?Print Booklets: Based on community demand, made more copies of printed digital storytelling project booklets for distribution across Ulithi Atoll.

990 sent to board of directors for review prior to finalizing

The organization does a review of current and equivalent positions (compatibility data), review data with people not part of OPOR (outside advice), and involve the Board in decisions about hiring and compensation through review and approval prior to taking action.

No documents available to the public.

mainOrgs.name: Houston Wilderness Inc
mainOrgs.missionSummary: Protects and promotes diverse ecoregions around Houston, Galveston Bay, and the Gulf of Mexico.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Riverine Targeted Use of Buyouts, Gulf-Houston Regional Conservation Plan.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Focuses on ecosystem protection with significant water-related efforts.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Includes two related programs emphasizing conservation and resilience.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: Houston Wilderness has a mature operational history of 22.8 years with $1.3M in revenue. The organization effectively promotes water conservation through its innovative programs and strong partnerships.
mainOrgs.websiteRecentActivity: Upcoming Annual Luncheon and volunteer opportunities highlighted on the website.
mainOrgs.softFlags: Revenue concentrated in Contributions (102%); Program expense ratio below tier threshold (79%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: HOUSTON WILDERNESS WORKS WITH A BROAD-BASED ALLIANCE OF BUSINESS, ENVIRONMENTAL AND GOVERNMENT INTERESTS TO PROTECT AND PROMOTE THE 10 DIVERSE ECOREGIONS OF THE 13+ COUNTY AREA AROUND HOUSTON, GALVESTON BAY, AND THE GULF OF MEXICO, INCLUDING COASTAL PRAIRIES, FORESTS, WETLANDS, AND WATERWAYS.
mainOrgs.program2Desc: THE RIVERINE TARGETED USE OF BUYOUTS (TUBS) PROGRAM - The pioneering Riverine Targeted Use of Buyouts (Riverine TUBs) Program is an innovative approach to coastal resilience and hazard mitigation through a long-term strategy for habitually flooded properties to be 1) purchased by respective regional counties or municipalities, 2) held by the county or municipality - with maintenance/enhancement MOUs with community partners or transferred to a state agency or NGO for fee-simple ownership, and 3) enhanced with native bioswales, large-scale native trees/plants, replaced coastal wetlands, and other types of nature-based infrastructure techniques.
mainOrgs.program3Desc: GULF HOUSTON REGIONAL CONSERVATION PLAN Facilitated by Houston Wilderness, the Gulf-Houston Regional Conservatin Plan ("RCP") is a long-term collaborative of environmental, business, and governmental entities working together to implement an ecosystem continuity and connectivity plan for the Gulf-Houston region through implementation of three key goals for enhanced resilience and nature-based infrastructure. Ecoregions Affected: All 10 ecoregions Ecosystem Services Enhanced/Utilized: Air & Water Quality, Water Absorption (flood abatement), Carbon Sequestration, Wildlife Habitat, Urban Heat Island Reduction, Ecotourism, Erosion Control, Energy savings
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
THE ORGANIZATION IS EXEMPT FROM FEDERAL INCOME TAXES UNDER SECTION 501(c)(3) OF THE INTERNAL REVENUE CODE AND IS CLASSIFIED AS A 170(b)(1)(A)(vi) PUBLIC CHARITY. THE ORGANIZATION UTILIZES THE ACCOUNTING REQUIREMENTS ASSOCIATED WITH UNCERTAINTY IN INCOME TAXES USING THE PROVISIONS OF FINANCIAL ACCOUNTING STANDARDS BOARD (FASB) ASC 740, INCOME TAXES. USING THAT GUIDANCE, TAX POSITIONS INITIALLY NEED TO BE RECOGNIZED IN THE FINANCIAL STATEMENTS WHEN IT IS MORE-LIKELY-THAN-NOT THE POSITIONS WILL BE SUSTAINED UPON EXAMINATION BY THE TAX AUTHORITIES. IT ALSO PROVIDES GUIDANCE FOR DERECOGNITION, CLASSIFICATION, INTEREST AND PENALTIES, ACCOUNTING IN INTERIM PERIODS, DISCLOSURE AND TRANSITION. AS OF JUNE 30, 2024 AND 2023, THE ORGANIZATION HAS NO UNCERTAIN TAX POSITIONS THAT QUALIFY FOR RECOGNITION OR DISCLOSURE IN THE FINANCIAL STATEMENTS.

TEMPORARY ENDOWMENT FUNDS ARE USED TO FURTHER THE DESIGNATED ACTIVITIES OF THE ORGANIZATION.

REVIEW AND APPROVAL AT BOARD OF DIRECTORS MEETING.

ANNUAL CONFIRMATION OF COMPLIANCE PROVIDED IN WRITING BY BOARD.

BOARD EVALUATES COMPENSATION ANNUALLY.

REQUIRED DOCUMENTS ON HOUSTON WILDERNESS WEBSITE.

Direct Program Costs-Contract Services: Program service expenses 70,780. Management and general expenses 0. Fundraising expenses 37,500. Total expenses 108,280.

NO CHANGE IN PROCESS FROM PRIOR YEAR HAS OCCURRED IN CURRENT YEAR

mainOrgs.name: Hermits Peak Watershed Alliance
mainOrgs.missionSummary: Protects and restores ecological and cultural heritage through environmental education and community action in Hermits Peak region.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Youth conservation projects, post-fire watershed restoration, and environmental education.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Focused on watershed restoration and ecological education.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Engages in related programs that blend conservation, education, and restoration efforts.
mainOrgs.notableSignals: Long-term active community engagement and partnerships with local youth programs.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: The Hermits Peak Watershed Alliance has 16.4 years of experience with $1M revenue. They maintain strong cash reserves and a solid current ratio, ensuring sustainability in their water conservation efforts.
mainOrgs.softFlags: Revenue concentrated in Contributions (100%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: The Hermits Peak Watershed Alliance HPWA works to protect and restore the ecological and cultural heritage of the Hermits Peak region through restoration environmental education and community action.
mainOrgs.program2Desc: The most significant education project was to employ train and guide eleven 14-25 year olds in post-fire watershed restoration for the summer of 2025. This education and workforce development was done with support from the New Mexico Youth Conservation Corps Commission. This was our third year in carrying out this YCC project. With state funding from the Outdoor Equity Program we conducted youth nature appreciation and outdoor recreation programs for a group of middle school students. Education included birding macroinvertebrate identification and ecology and flyfishing. Other education programs featured the development and distribution of a series of videos on Post-Fire Restoration techniques including erosion control reforestation and reseeding with herbaceous plants. These are available to the public.
mainOrgs.program3Desc: Seven private landowners were assisted with post-fire land restoration work with private contracts. Very few contractors exist in our area to assist private landowners with watershed restoration needs. We began providing this service in 2024.
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
The organizations Form 990 is first reviewed by the Treasurer and Executive Director. It is then sent to all members of the Board who have an opportunity to review the Form 990 electronically. Any issues are resolved.

Conflict of Interest Policy Enforcement: The Executive Director reviews all contracts for potential conflicts of interest and bring them to the attention of the Board. The Board then investigates to determine the existence of a conflict; whether alternatives exist that would avoid giving rise to a conflict; and if none whether it is in the best interest of the organization to continue to pursue the contract. Each officer and director signs an annual statement which affirms compliance with and understanding of the policy. Periodic reviews of all contracts are conducted by the Executive Director.

The organizations governing documents conflict of interest policy and financial statements are made available upon request.

mainOrgs.name: Maui Nui Marine Resource Council Inc
mainOrgs.missionSummary: Protecting ocean water quality and promoting healthy coral reefs and native fish through community partnership.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Ocean Water Quality Monitoring and Reef Friendly Landscaping.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Focus on ocean water quality and coral reef health directly supports water conservation.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Two related programs focus on water quality and environmental sustainability.
mainOrgs.notableSignals: Community-based monitoring and unique reef-friendly certification course.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: With over 10 years of experience, MNMRC has strong community partnerships and a solid financial standing, demonstrating a commitment to ocean conservation and education.
mainOrgs.websiteRecentActivity: Celebrating 17 years of conservation work, with ongoing projects like coral reef monitoring and pollution tracking.
mainOrgs.softFlags: Revenue concentrated in Contributions (98%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: MAUI NUI MARINE RESOURCE COUNCIL (MNMRC) IS A MAUI-BASED NONPROFIT RECOGNIZED FOR OUR ABILITY TO WORK IN PARTNERSHIP WITH THE COMMUNITY TOWARD OUR VISION OF CLEAN OCEAN WATER, HEALTHY CORAL REEFS AND THRIVING NATIVE FISH. WE CELEBRATED OUR 15TH ANNIVERSARY IN 2022.
mainOrgs.program2Desc: OCEAN WATER QUALITY MONITORING PROGRAM:MNMRC CO-MANAGES A UNIQUE COMMUNITY-BASED OCEAN WATER QUALITY MONITORING PROGRAM, HUI O KA WAI OLA. THIS PROGRAM MONITORED OCEAN WATER QUALITY AT MAUI BEACHES EVERY THREE WEEKS THROUGHOUT THE YEAR, GROWING FROM 29 TO 35 SITES DURING 2023. THE HUI WAS ONE OF THE FIRST GROUPS TO TEST WATER CONDITIONS IN WEST MAUI AFTER THE LAHAINA WILDFIRE AND ADDED FOUR NEW TESTING SITES WITHIN THE BURN ZONE (IN ADDITION TO THE THREE SITES ESTABLISHED BEFORE THE FIRE). THE HUI ALSO EXPANDED TO LANA'I, LAUNCHING TWO SITES ON THE NEIGHBOR ISLAND IN 2023. THE PROGRAM PROVIDES QUALITY-ASSURED DATA TO THE STATE OF HAWAI'I DEPARTMENT OF HEALTH CLEAN WATER BRANCH. SINCE THE MONITORING PROGRAM LAUNCHED IN 2016, HUI O KA WAI OLA HAS COLLECTED OVER 4,141 WATER SAMPLES ALONG THE LEEWARD COAST OF MAUI.
mainOrgs.program3Desc: REEF FRIENDLY LANDSCAPING PROGRAM:MNMRC DEVELOPED HAWAI'I'S FIRST REEF-FRIENDLY LANDSCAPING (RFL) CERTIFICATION COURSE TO HELP LANDSCAPING PROFESSIONALS, PROPERTY OWNERS AND AGRICULTURAL BUSINESSES TRANSITION FROM HERBICIDES AND PESTICIDES TO NATURAL LAND-CARE PRODUCTS AND PRACTICES. MNMRC WORKED WITH LOCAL INDUSTRY EXPERTS TO DEVELOP THE COURSE CURRICULUM, WHICH COVERS TOPICS LIKE SOIL HEALTH AND MICROBIOLOGY, NATIVE, EXOTIC AND INVASIVE PLANTS, ENERGY, POLLUTION AND CLIMATE CHANGE, FERTILIZERS, AND PLANTING. THE GOAL OF THE PROGRAM IS TO NOT ONLY YIELD HEALTHIER SOIL AND PLANT LIFE BUT ALSO DECREASE THE AMOUNT OF TOXIC CHEMICALS THAT ARE WASHED INTO THE OCEAN AND ONTO NEARBY CORAL REEFS. A COHORT OF 12 PROPERTIES ACROSS THE ISLAND SAW PROMISING RESULTS AFTER PARTICIPATING IN THE RFL PILOT PROGRAM, WHICH RAN FROM OCTOBER TO DECEMBER 2023. PARTICIPATING PROPERTIES INCLUDED HOTELS AND RESORTS, CONDOS, CONSTRUCTION SITES, AND FARMS. ALL PARTICIPANTS SAW A HOST OF POSITIVE RESULTS ON THEIR TEST PLOTS FROM IMPROVED PLANT GROWTH AND APPEARANCE, TO A DECREASE IN PESTS, PLANT DISEASE AND WEED PATCHES.
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
ROBIN NEWBOLD AND MICHAEL FOGARTY ARE DOMESTIC PARTNERS. THIS INFORMATION IS PROVIDED IN THE ORGANIZATION'S ANNUAL CONFLICT OF INTEREST FORMS.

BOARD MEMBERS AND THE CEO ARE REQUIRED TO DISCLOSE ANNUALLY BY FILLING OUT A DISCLOSURE FORM IDENTIFYING ANY BUSINESS OR FINANCIAL INTERESTS THAT COULD RESULT IN AN ACTUAL OR PERCEIVED CONFLICT OF INTEREST. mainOrgs.name: Eagle River Watershed Council Inc
mainOrgs.missionSummary: Advocates for the health of the Eagle River and Colorado River basins through research, education, and projects.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Water quality monitoring, community water planning, and education initiatives for river conservation.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Focused on watershed protection, water quality, and community engagement.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Combines water quality monitoring, community planning, and educational programs.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: With nearly 20 years of experience, EAGLE RIVER WATERSHED COUNCIL has a strong financial position and actively engages the community in water conservation initiatives.
mainOrgs.websiteRecentActivity: In 2025, 1,546 volunteers participated in clean-up efforts and the Wild & Scenic Film Festival.
mainOrgs.softFlags: Months unrestricted below threshold; Revenue concentrated in Contributions (99%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: EAGLE RIVER WATERSHED COUNCIL, DBA EAGLE RIVER COALITION, ADVOCATES FOR THE HEALTH OF THE UPPER COLORADO AND EAGLE RIVER BASINS THROUGH RESEARCH, EDUCATION AND PROJECTS. THE WATERSHED COUNCIL STRIVES TO PROTECT AND ENHANCE THE HIGH-QUALITY NATURAL, SCENIC AND ECONOMIC VALUES THAT OUR RIVERS AND TRIBUTARIES PROVIDE TO THE CITIZENS, VISITORS AND WILDLIFE OF THE EAGLE RIVER AND COLORADO RIVER WATERSHEDS LOCATED IN EAGLE COUNTY.
mainOrgs.program2Desc: EAGLE RIVER COMMUNITY WATER PLAN: THIS IS AN EAGLE RIVER WATERSHED COUNCIL-LED EFFORT TO CREATE A PATH FORWARD IN ORDER TO PROTECT OUR WATERSHED AGAINST UNDESIRABLE FUTURE CONDITIONS. MANY OTHER PLANNING EFFORTS HAVE FOCUSED ON A SNAPSHOT IN TIME AND WATER QUALITY; HOWEVER, THIS PLAN HAS A LARGE FOCUS ON WATER QUANTITY AND HOW IT WILL BE IMPACTED BY POPULATION GROWTH, CLIMATE CHANGE, AND POTENTIAL WATER RIGHTS DEVELOPMENT. FROM THIS, OUR COMMUNITY WILL HAVE A PATH FORWARD TO IMPLEMENT PROJECTS, PROGRAMS AND POLICIES THAT UPHOLD OUR COMMUNITY VALUES AND PROTECT OUR RIVERS. IN 2024, WE DISTRIBUTED 40 RAIN BARRELS TO COMMUNITY MEMBERS, HELPING TO CONSERVE WATER IN THE EAGLE RIVER VALLEY.
mainOrgs.program3Desc: EDUCATION: IT'S BEEN SAID THAT PEOPLE CONSERVE WHAT THEY LOVE; THEY LOVE WHAT THEY UNDERSTAND; THEY UNDERSTAND WHAT THEY LEARN. THIS PERSPECTIVE INFORMS US AND HAS HELPED US IMPLEMENT A VIBRANT EDUCATION PROGRAM THAT CONNECTS OUR COMMUNITY TO THE RIVERS, CREATING LIFELONG RIVER LOVERS AND ADVOCATES. THROUGH PROGRAMS LIKE THE EAGLE RIVER WATER FESTIVAL THAT BRINGS TOGETHER MORE THAN 450 5TH GRADERS, AND OUR WATERSHED IMMERSIONS THAT PROVIDE HANDS-ON EXPERIENCES, WE ARE ABLE TO SHOWCASE OUR WATERSHED AND INSPIRE ACTION TO PROTECT IT. DURING 2024, WE EDUCATED MORE THAN 950 COMMUNITY MEMBERS ON PROTECTING WATERSHED HEALTH, AND WE HOSTED A FREE WATER EDUCATION DAY FOR 350 LOCAL 5TH GRADERS IN THE EAGLE RIVER VALLEY.
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
EAGLE RIVER WATERSHED COUNCIL, DBA EAGLE RIVER COALITION, ADVOCATES FOR THE HEALTH OF THE UPPER COLORADO AND EAGLE RIVER BASINS THROUGH RESEARCH, EDUCATION AND PROJECTS. THE WATERSHED COUNCIL STRIVES TO PROTECT AND ENHANCE THE HIGH-QUALITY NATURAL, SCENIC AND ECONOMIC VALUES THAT OUR RIVERS AND TRIBUTARIES PROVIDE TO THE CITIZENS, VISITORS AND WILDLIFE OF THE EAGLE RIVER AND COLORADO RIVER WATERSHEDS LOCATED IN EAGLE COUNTY.

WATER QUALITY MONITORING AND ASSESSMENT PROGRAM: THIS IS A COORDINATED EFFORT LED BY THE WATERSHED COUNCIL TO COLLECT, ANALYZE AND REPORT ON WATER QUALITY DATA AT 75 SITES ON THE EAGLE RIVER WITH 12 OTHER ENTITIES WITHIN THE EAGLE VALLEY. THROUGH THIS EFFORT, WE ARE ABLE TO MAKE GUIDED DECISIONS ON RESTORATION EFFORTS, EDUCATION FOCUSES AND PROVIDE CONTEXT TO COMMUNITY DECISION-MAKERS REGARDING WATER QUALITY WITHIN THE WATERSHED. 2024 EXPENSE 153,240 EAGLE RIVER FUND: AS OUR COMMUNITY COMPLETES THE EAGLE RIVER COMMUNITY WATER PLAN, THERE WILL BE A STRONG LIST OF PROJECTS, POLICIES, AND PROGRAMS THAT WILL NEED TO BE IMPLEMENTED IN ORDER TO MITIGATE UNDESIRABLE FUTURE CONDITIONS. TO HELP ACHIEVE THAT, THE WATERSHED COUNCIL IS WORKING TO ESTABLISH AN ENDOWMENT FUND - THE EAGLE RIVER FUND - THAT WOULD HELP TO LEVERAGE STATE AND FEDERAL DOLLARS AND ENABLE MORE LOCAL WORK WITHIN OUR ORGANIZATION AND BEYOND. 2024 EXPENSE 92,007 CLEANUPS: THE COMMUNITY PRIDE HIGHWAY CLEANUP AND EAGLE RIVER CLEANUP ARE TWO LONGSTANDING, COMMUNITY-WIDE EVENTS THAT MOBILIZE HUNDREDS OF VOLUNTEERS AND REMOVE LITERAL TONS OF TRASH FROM OUR WATERSHED. THIS HELPS TO PROTECT THE NATURAL RESOURCE THAT OUR RIVERS AND STREAMS PROVIDE AS A DRINKING WATER SOURCE, RECREATION OPPORTUNITIES, AND HABITAT FOR NUMEROUS FLORA AND FAUNA, WHILE ALSO PROVIDING BEAUTIFICATION TO OUR WATERSHED FOR PASSERSBY, VISITORS, AND LOCALS ALIKE. DURING 2024, WE REMOVED OVER 20 TONS OF TRASH DURING OUR CLEANUPS. 2024 EXPENSE 35,415

THE FORM 990 IS REVIEWED AT A MEETING OF THE BOARD'S FINANCIAL ADVISORY COMMITTEE. THE FINANCIAL ADVISORY COMMITTEE THEN RECOMMENDS APPROVAL OF THE 990 AT THE NEXT BOARD MEETING.

ERWC'S CONFLICT OF INTEREST POLICY STATEMENT REQUIRES EACH DIRECTOR, PRINCIPAL OFFICER, AND COMMITTEE MEMBER TO ANNUALLY SIGN A STATEMENT AFFIRMING RECEIPT AND UNDERSTANDING OF, AND COMPLIANCE WITH THE POLICY. WHILE THE POLICY NOTES THE DUTY OF EACH INTERESTED PARTY TO DISCLOSE ACTUAL OR POTENTIAL CONFLICTS OF INTEREST TO THE BOARD, THE POLICY ALSO CALLS FOR THE BOARD (OR ITS DESIGNEES) TO CONDUCT PERIODIC REVIEWS FOR COMPLIANCE INCLUDING WHETHER COMPENSATION ARRANGEMENTS AND BENEFITS ARE REASONABLE, AND WHETHER ARRANGEMENTS WITH OTHER ENTITIES CONFORM TO ERWC'S WRITTEN POLICIES, ARE PROPERLY RECORDED, REFLECT REASONABLE INVESTMENT OR PAYMENTS FOR GOODS AND SERVICES, FURTHER CHARITABLE PURPOSES, AND DO NOT RESULT IN INUREMENT, IMPERMISSIBLE PRIVATE BENEFIT, OR EXCESS BENEFIT TRANSACTIONS.

REASONABLE COMPENSATION IS DETERMINED BASED ON SALARY AND WAGES FOR COMPARABLE JOB DESCRIPTIONS AND DUTIES OBTAINED THROUGH SURVEYS AND PUBLIC RECORDS OF LOCAL NOT-FOR-PROFITS AND GOVERNMENTAL ORGANIZATIONS.

GOVERNING DOCUMENTS, POLICIES (INCLUDING THE CONFLICT OF INTEREST POLICY) AND FINANCIALS RECORDS (INCLUDING APPPLICABLE IRS FILINGS) ARE MADE AVAILABLE TO THE PUBLIC UPON REQUEST AT ERWC'S OFFICES. IN ADDITION, ALL BI-MONTHLY MEETINGS OF THE BOARD ARE OPEN TO THE PUBLIC.

mainOrgs.name: Big Thompson Watershed Coalition
mainOrgs.missionSummary: Protecting and restoring the Big Thompson watershed for community benefit through collaborative efforts.
mainOrgs.primaryPrograms: Forest health, stream and water resources management, and disaster recovery.
mainOrgs.missionAlignmentRationale: Core focus on watershed protection and ecological health.
mainOrgs.programFocusRationale: Engages in several related programs addressing forest health and water resources.
mainOrgs.capacityNarrative: Established for nearly 10 years, BTWC has a strong financial position with a current ratio of 6.07. However, it faces a revenue decline of 23%, raising concerns for sustainability. Its mission is focused on watershed health, aligning strongly with funding priorities.
mainOrgs.websiteRecentActivity: Completed 15 restoration projects and 2 infrastructure replacements, focusing on flood and wildfire resilience.
mainOrgs.softFlags: Months unrestricted below threshold; Revenue concentrated in Contributions (100%)
mainOrgs.missionStatement: VISION: A HEALTHY AND RESILIENT BIG THOMPSON WATERSHED BENEFITTING FISH, WILDLIFE, AND THE PEOPLE ITSERVES THROUGH COLLABORATIVE EFFORTS FOR CURRENT AND FUTURE GENERATIONS. MISSION: WORKING WITH OTHERS TO TAKE ACTION THAT PROTECTS AND RESTORES THE HEALTH AND VITALITY OF THE BIG THOMPSON WATERSHED FOR THE USE AND ENJOYMENT OF OUR COMMUNITY.
mainOrgs.program2Desc: FOREST HEALTH AND MANAGEMENT GOAL: MITIGATING WILDFIRE RISK AND ENHANCING FOREST HEALTH THROUGH COLLABORATIVE FOREST MANAGEMENT PROJECTS. UNDER THE LEADERSHIP OF OUR FORESTRY PROJECT MANAGER, OUR FORESTRY PROGRAM EXPANDED THROUGHOUT 2023. THROUGHOUT THE YEAR WE WORKED IN THREE BIG T COMMUNITIES TREATING FORESTS AND ROADWAYS, TREATING 32 ACRES BETWEEN THE GLEN HAVEN AND STORM MOUNTAIN COMMUNITIES AND 1.25 MILES OF ROADWAY IN WALTONIA. WE WORKED WITH 32 DIFFERENT PRIVATE LANDOWNERS TO MITIGATE WILDFIRE RISK, ENHANCE FOREST HEALTH, AND INCREASE EMERGENCY INGRESS/EGRESS ALONG PRIORITY ROADWAYS. WE STRENGTHENED PARTNERSHIPS AS A MEMBER OF THE BIG THOMPSON WATERSHED HEALTH PARTNERSHIP, WORKING TO IDENTIFY THE MOST STRATEGIC PLACES TO IMPLEMENT FOREST HEALTH AND FUELS REDUCTION PROJECTS TO MAKE THE BIGGEST IMPACT ON THE LANDSCAPE. FINALLY, IN PARTNERSHIP WITH LOVELAND FIRE RESCUE AUTHORITY, WE PURCHASED AN AIR CURTAIN BURNER A NEW TOOL TO HELP REDUCE WOODY FUELS GENERATED FROM FORESTRY PROJECTS. THIS TOW BEHIND MOBILE UNIT WILL BURN WOODY MATERIAL AT THE RATE OF ABOUT 1 TON/HOUR, PRODUCE LESS SMOKE, AND INCREASE SAFETY AROUND BURNING.
mainOrgs.program3Desc: STREAM & WATER RESOURCES GOAL: ENHANCING THE ECOLOGICAL HEALTH, FUNCTION, AND CONNECTIVITY OF OUR RIVER SYSTEM FOR HUMAN AND NATURAL COMMUNITIES. THE RIVER ENVISIONING PROJECT IS A STREAM MANAGEMENT PLANNING PROJECT THAT CONSIDERS THE FUTURE OF THE WATERSHED AND THE BIG THOMPSON RIVER SYSTEM THROUGH LOVELAND. THE GOAL IS TO BUILD A SHARED VISION FOR IMPROVING THE BIG THOMPSON RIVER BY IDENTIFYING STRATEGIES AND ACTION PLANS THAT RESPECT PROPERTY AND WATER RIGHTS, ADDRESS WATER USER NEEDS, AND ENHANCE ENVIRONMENTAL CONDITIONS AND RECREATIONAL OPPORTUNITIES. THE PLAN ASSESSED THE RIVER HEALTH CONDITIONS AND PROVIDED A ROAD MAP FOR THE NEXT 20 YEARS BY PRIORITIZING AREAS OF THE RIVER THAT CAN BENEFIT FROM RIVER HEALTH AND IMPROVEMENT PROJECTS. IN 2023, WE WORKED TO ENGAGE OUR COMMUNITY AND PARTNERS TO DETERMINE PROJECTS WITHIN THESE PRIORITY AREAS THAT ARE COMMUNITY SUPPORTED AND PROVIDE BENEFIT TO RIVER HEALTH, RECREATION, AND RIVER USERS. WE HOSTED FOUR PUBLIC MEETINGS TO LEARN WHAT PROJECTS WOULD BE MOST VALUABLE TO THE BIG THOMPSON RIVER FROM THE COMMUNITYS PERSPECTIVE. WE COMPLETED 10 SITE VISITS WITH STAKEHOLDERS AND PARTNERS TO LOOK AT ON-THE- GROUND RIVER CONDITIONS AND PLAN PROJECTS. WE IDENTIFIED 23 TOTAL COMMUNITY AND PARTNER SUPPORTED RIVER PROJECTS AND PRIORITIZED EIGHT OF THOSE TO BEGIN MOVING FORWARD IN 2024.
mainOrgs.scheduleO:
VISION: A HEALTHY AND RESILIENT BIG THOMPSON WATERSHED BENEFITTING FISH, WILDLIFE, AND THE PEOPLE ITSERVES THROUGH COLLABORATIVE EFFORTS FOR CURRENT AND FUTURE GENERATIONS. MISSION: WORKING WITH OTHERS TO TAKE ACTION THAT PROTECTS AND RESTORES THE HEALTH AND VITALITY OF THE BIG THOMPSON WATERSHED FOR THE USE AND ENJOYMENT OF OUR COMMUNITY.

BTWC HAS HOSTED SEVERAL VOLUNTEER EVENTS (REFORESTATION, DEBRIS REMOVAL FROM FLOODING) AND OUR BOARD IS ENTIRELY MADE UP OF VOLUNTEERS.

EDUCATION AND OUTREACH

DISASTER RECOVERY GOAL: HELPING OUR LANDS AND COMMUNITY RECOVER FROM FLOOD AND FIRE THROUGH COLLABORATION, RESTORATION, AND RESILIENCY BUILDING. AN IMMEDIATE NEED FOLLOWING CATASTROPHIC FIRES LIKE THE CAMERON PEAK FIRE IS TO REDUCE EROSION ON HILLSLOPES. HIGH SEVERITY FIRES BURN AWAY THE MAJORITY OF VEGETATION AND TOPSOIL ON HILLSLOPES LEAVING HYDROPHOBIC (WATER REPELLENT) SOILS AND BURNING THE SEED SOURCES FOR FUTURE VEGETATION GROWTH. HYDROPHOBIC SOILS WITH LITTLE VEGETATION CAN BE HEAVILY IMPACTED BY EVEN SMALL RAIN EVENTS LEADING TO SEVERE EROSION AND SEDIMENT, ASH, AND DEBRIS FLOWING DOWNHILL INTO WATERWAYS. WHEN OUR WATERWAYS ARE INUNDATED WITH EXCESS WATER, SEDIMENT, AND DEBRIS, POST-FIRE FLOODING OCCURS IMPACTING DOWNSTREAM VALUES, AQUATIC POPULATIONS, COMMUNITIES, AND ROADWAYS. WITH NO SEED SOURCES TO PRODUCE NEW TREE GROWTH, SOME AREAS MAY TAKE DECADES OR LONGER TO BECOME REFORESTED. TO ADDRESS THIS NEED IN 2023 WE TOOK A TWO-FOLD APPROACH FOCUSING ON STREAM RESTORATION AND REFORESTATION EFFORTS. LED BY OUR NEWLY HIRED (JUNE), FULL-TIME, PERMANENT STREAM RESTORATION PROJECT MANAGER, WE EXPANDED OUR POST-FIRE RECOVERY WORK TO ADDRESS RESTORATION IN STREAMS THAT HAVE HIGH AMOUNTS OF POST-FIRE FLOODING, DEBRIS FLOWS, AND SEDIMENT TRANSPORT. OUR PROJECTS FOCUS ON INSTALLING WOODY STRUCTURES IN PRIORITY STREAMS TO SLOW THE FLOW OF WATER, CATCH DEBRIS AND SEDIMENT, AND SLOWLY RAISE ERODED STREAMBEDS TO RECONNECT THEM WITH THEIR FLOODPLAINS. THE IN-STREAM PROJECTS INCLUDED VOLUNTEER OPPORTUNITIES AND OCCURRED ON BOTH USFS LAND AND PRIVATE PROPERTIES. WE INSTALLED 126 IN-STREAM STRUCTURES TO HELP RESTORE 2.35 MILES OF STREAMS, DREDGED WATERWAYS AND PONDS TO REMOVE 640 CUBIC YARDS OF SEDIMENT DEPOSITED FROM POST-FIRE FLOODING, AND RESEEDED 2 ACRES USING NATIVE SEED AND INSTALLED 2,000 WILLOW STAKES ALONG RIPARIAN AREAS. TO COUNTER THE LACK OF TREE REGROWTH IN SEVERELY BURNED AREAS OF THE FIRE, WE HOSTED EIGHT VOLUNTEER DAYS AND CONTRACTED LABOR TO PLANT 6,390 PONDEROSA PINE SEEDLINGS ACROSS 77 ACRES OF SEVERELY BURNED AREAS OF THE BIG THOMPSON WATERSHED IN 2023. BETWEEN THE TWO FIRE RECOVERY PROJECT TYPES, WE PROVIDED OPPORTUNITIES FOR APPROXIMATELY 200 VOLUNTEERS WHO CONTRIBUTED NEARLY 1,500 VOLUNTEER HOURS.

FOREST HEALTH AND MANAGEMENT GOAL: MITIGATING WILDFIRE RISK AND ENHANCING FOREST HEALTH THROUGH COLLABORATIVE FOREST MANAGEMENT PROJECTS. UNDER THE LEADERSHIP OF OUR FORESTRY PROJECT MANAGER, OUR FORESTRY PROGRAM EXPANDED THROUGHOUT 2023. THROUGHOUT THE YEAR WE WORKED IN THREE BIG T COMMUNITIES TREATING FORESTS AND ROADWAYS, TREATING 32 ACRES BETWEEN THE GLEN HAVEN AND STORM MOUNTAIN COMMUNITIES AND 1.25 MILES OF ROADWAY IN WALTONIA. WE WORKED WITH 32 DIFFERENT PRIVATE LANDOWNERS TO MITIGATE WILDFIRE RISK, ENHANCE FOREST HEALTH, AND INCREASE EMERGENCY INGRESS/EGRESS ALONG PRIORITY ROADWAYS. WE STRENGTHENED PARTNERSHIPS AS A MEMBER OF THE BIG THOMPSON WATERSHED HEALTH PARTNERSHIP, WORKING TO IDENTIFY THE MOST STRATEGIC PLACES TO IMPLEMENT FOREST HEALTH AND FUELS REDUCTION PROJECTS TO MAKE THE BIGGEST IMPACT ON THE LANDSCAPE. FINALLY, IN PARTNERSHIP WITH LOVELAND FIRE RESCUE AUTHORITY, WE PURCHASED AN AIR CURTAIN BURNER A NEW TOOL TO HELP REDUCE WOODY FUELS GENERATED FROM FORESTRY PROJECTS. THIS TOW BEHIND MOBILE UNIT WILL BURN WOODY MATERIAL AT THE RATE OF ABOUT 1 TON/HOUR, PRODUCE LESS SMOKE, AND INCREASE SAFETY AROUND BURNING.

STREAM & WATER RESOURCES GOAL: ENHANCING THE ECOLOGICAL HEALTH, FUNCTION, AND CONNECTIVITY OF OUR RIVER SYSTEM FOR HUMAN AND NATURAL COMMUNITIES. THE RIVER ENVISIONING PROJECT IS A STREAM MANAGEMENT PLANNING PROJECT THAT CONSIDERS THE FUTURE OF THE WATERSHED AND THE BIG THOMPSON RIVER SYSTEM THROUGH LOVELAND. THE GOAL IS TO BUILD A SHARED VISION FOR IMPROVING THE BIG THOMPSON RIVER BY IDENTIFYING STRATEGIES AND ACTION PLANS THAT RESPECT PROPERTY AND WATER RIGHTS, ADDRESS WATER USER NEEDS, AND ENHANCE ENVIRONMENTAL CONDITIONS AND RECREATIONAL OPPORTUNITIES. THE PLAN ASSESSED THE RIVER HEALTH CONDITIONS AND PROVIDED A ROAD MAP FOR THE NEXT 20 YEARS BY PRIORITIZING AREAS OF THE RIVER THAT CAN BENEFIT FROM RIVER HEALTH AND IMPROVEMENT PROJECTS. IN 2023, WE WORKED TO ENGAGE OUR COMMUNITY AND PARTNERS TO DETERMINE PROJECTS WITHIN THESE PRIORITY AREAS THAT ARE COMMUNITY SUPPORTED AND PROVIDE BENEFIT TO RIVER HEALTH, RECREATION, AND RIVER USERS. WE HOSTED FOUR PUBLIC MEETINGS TO LEARN WHAT PROJECTS WOULD BE MOST VALUABLE TO THE BIG THOMPSON RIVER FROM THE COMMUNITYS PERSPECTIVE. WE COMPLETED 10 SITE VISITS WITH STAKEHOLDERS AND PARTNERS TO LOOK AT ON-THE- GROUND RIVER CONDITIONS AND PLAN PROJECTS. WE IDENTIFIED 23 TOTAL COMMUNITY AND PARTNER SUPPORTED RIVER PROJECTS AND PRIORITIZED EIGHT OF THOSE TO BEGIN MOVING FORWARD IN 2024.

EDUCATION AND OUTREACH GOAL: PROVIDING OUR COMMUNITIES WITH EDUCATIONAL EVENTS, COMMUNITY MEETINGS AND GATHERINGS, AND VOLUNTEER EVENTS PRESENTATIONS, AND DIGITAL OUTREACH TO BUILD RELATIONSHIPS, INCREASE AWARENESS ABOUT WATERSHED HEALTH AND THE WORK WE DO, AND STEWARD THE WATERSHED. WE WORK TO BUILD EDUCATION AND OUTREACH INTO ALL OUR THREE MAIN PROGRAMMATIC AREAS. UNDER THE LEADERSHIP OF OUR COMMUNICATIONS AND OUTREACH MANAGER, WE REACHED NEW AUDIENCES, CONTINUED TO BUILD RELATIONSHIPS WITH OUT COMMUNITIES, UPDATED OUR WEBSITE AND PRODUCED NEW EDUCATIONAL RESOURCES. WE GAVE 13 PRESENTATIONS AND LED OR ASSISTED WITH 24 COMMUNITY MEETINGS AND/OR WORKSHOPS REACHING OVER 500 PEOPLE. WE HELD OUR THIRD ANNUAL TREK THE THOMPSON EDUCATIONAL EVENT AND HOSTED OUR FIRST DONOR APPRECIATION EVENT REACHING 160 COMMUNITY MEMBERS. ALL OTHER PROGRAMS

THE FORM 990 IS DISTRIBUTED TO ALL BOARD MEMBERS AFTER IT IS REVIEWED AND APPROVED BY THE FINANCE COMMITTEE FOR SUBMISSION TO GOVERNMENT AGENCIES.

THE CONFLICT OF INTEREST POLICY AND ANNUALLY SIGNED STATEMENTS ARE REVIEWD AND ENFORCED BY THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS.

GOVERNING DOCUMENTS ARE AVAILABLE ON THE BTWC SHARED SERVER AND UPON REQUEST.

THE CONTRACTED BOOKKEEPER, THE TREASURER OF THE BOARD, AND THE EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR MEET BI-MONTHLY TO REVIEW MAJOR INCOME AND EXPENSE TRANSACTIONS. THE INFORMATION ON THE 990 IS ALSO REVIEWED BY THE FINANCE COMMITTEE.

## About BTWC

The Big Thompson Watershed Coalition (BTWC) formed as a 501(c)3 to help private and public landowners collaborate recovery efforts after the 2013 state-declared flooding emergency. Our flood recovery efforts brought over $15 million instate, federal, and local funds to the watershed allowing us to complete 15 restoration projects and 2 infrastructure replacement projects. Today, we are focused on creating a bright future for the Big Thompson watershed. Through dedicated partnerships we work to build resilience in the watershed through projects that reduce the risk and potential impacts of natural disasters like floods and wildfires and continue to help the watershed recover from events like the Cameron Peak Fire. Our goal as a Watershed Coalition is to bolster the health and integrity of this critical river system for the wild and natural communities that depend on it through engaging watershed residents and completing on-the-ground conservation projects.

## Welcome to the Big Thompson Watershed

#### Click on the interactive map to learn more!

## ACTAI Global is a nonprofit organization whose mission is to support environmental conservation and economic empowerment through entrepreneurship.

#### What began in 2007 as an intimate gathering in Maui has since evolved into a global community of Athletes, Conservationists, Technologists, Artists, and Innovators—the collective that forms the ACTAI name.

#### Since incorporating in 2012, ACTAI Global has produced more than 100 events across 30+ cities, bringing together leading founders, investors, tech innovators, and impact‑driven thought leaders. Renowned for its invite‑only gatherings in destinations that also offer world‑class kitesurfing, ACTAI has become known for its unique blend of curated programming, adventure, and high‑caliber networking.

To prohibit development on the Protected Property and protect and preserve,in perpetuity, the conservation values including the natural, scenic, and special character ecoclogical and hydrological integrity, and habitat for indigenous plant and animal life of the Protected Property. Specifically, the purposes of the Conservation Easement are to protect and facilitate wetland health and water quality and promote fish, wildlife, plants, and associated habitat.

Conservation easements are reported as Program revenue (where billable) and program expenses. Any revenue or grants paid in connection with easements are shown on Revenue statements.
